## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of the four type arguments (`d`, `p`, `q`, `r`) used in R's probability distribution functions, with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

In R, every probability distribution is accessed through a family of four functions, all sharing the same root name and differing only in the **type argument prefix**:

1. **`d<dist>(x, ...)` — Density Function:** Returns the value of the probability density function (PDF) for continuous distributions or the probability mass function (PMF) for discrete distributions at point $x$.
   - *Example:* `dnorm(0)` returns `0.3989423` (the height of the standard normal curve at $x=0$).

2. **`p<dist>(q, ...)` — Cumulative Distribution Function:** Returns $P(X \le q)$, the probability that $X$ is less than or equal to $q$.
   - *Example:* `pnorm(1.96)` returns `0.9750025`, the area under the standard normal curve to the left of $1.96$.

3. **`q<dist>(p, ...)` — Quantile Function:** Returns the inverse CDF, i.e., the value $x$ such that $P(X \le x) = p$.
   - *Example:* `qnorm(0.975)` returns `1.959964`, the $97.5^{th}$ percentile of the standard normal.

4. **`r<dist>(n, ...)` — Random Number Generator:** Generates $n$ pseudo-random samples from the specified distribution.
   - *Example:* `rnorm(5)` returns a vector of 5 random numbers drawn from $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between the `d` prefix and the `p` prefix in R's distribution functions. Why might a student incorrectly use one in place of the other?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `d` Prefix | `p` Prefix |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| Mathematical meaning | PDF or PMF: $f(x)$ or $P(X=x)$ | CDF: $F(x) = P(X \le x)$ |
| Output range | $[0, \infty)$ for continuous; $[0,1]$ for discrete | Strictly $[0, 1]$ |
| Operation | Evaluation of density at a point | Integration of density up to a point |
| Example | `dnorm(1.96)` $\approx 0.0584$ | `pnorm(1.96)` $\approx 0.9750$ |

A common student error is to use `dnorm(1.96)` expecting it to return "the probability that $Z$ is near $1.96$" — but `d` returns the *density height*, **not** a probability. For an actual probability, one must use `pnorm(1.96) - pnorm(0)` or similar. This confusion arises because beginners mentally translate "density" as "probability" without recognizing that the density at a point is not itself a probability for continuous distributions.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of suitable examples, describe the **four type arguments** (`d`, `p`, `q`, `r`) used in R probability distribution functions. Explain how they are mathematically related. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a Binomial distribution with $n = 20$ and $p = 0.3$, write R code to:
   (i) Compute $P(X = 5)$
   (ii) Compute $P(X \le 5)$
   (iii) Find the value of $k$ such that $P(X \le k) = 0.5$
   (iv) Generate a random sample of size 50.
   Show the expected output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The four type arguments are prefixes that transform a distribution root (e.g., `norm`, `binom`) into one of four operations:

- **`d<dist>(x, ...)`** computes the **density** $f(x)$ — the height of the PDF/PMF at $x$.
- **`p<dist>(q, ...)`** computes the **CDF** $F(q) = P(X \le q)$ — obtained by integrating $f$ from $-\infty$ to $q$.
- **`q<dist>(p, ...)`** computes the **quantile** $F^{-1}(p)$ — the inverse CDF, obtained by solving $F(x) = p$.
- **`r<dist>(n, ...)`** generates $n$ i.i.d. random variates from the distribution.

**Mathematical relationship:** The four operations are linked by integration and inversion:

$$d(x) \xrightarrow{\text{integrate from } -\infty} p(q) \xrightarrow{\text{inverse}} q(p) \xrightarrow{\text{use as generator}} r(n)$$

Formally:

$$p(q) = \int_{-\infty}^{q} d(t)\, dt, \quad q(p) = p^{-1}(p), \quad r(n) = \{x_i\}_{i=1}^{n} \sim F$$

**Example round-trip verification:**

```r
> dnorm(1.96)              # density at 1.96
[1] 0.0584426
> pnorm(1.96)              # CDF at 1.96
[1] 0.9750021
> qnorm(0.975)             # inverse CDF at 0.975
[1] 1.959964
> rnorm(3)                 # 3 random samples
[1]  0.2365298 -0.6733444  1.0724468
```

**Verification:** `qnorm(pnorm(1.96)) ≈ 1.96` (round-trip identity).

**(b)** R code with $n=20, p=0.3$:

```r
# (i) P(X = 5) — uses d prefix
> dbinom(5, size = 20, prob = 0.3)
[1] 0.1788631

# (ii) P(X <= 5) — uses p prefix
> pbinom(5, size = 20, prob = 0.3)
[1] 0.4163707

# (iii) Median (smallest k with CDF >= 0.5) — uses q prefix
> qbinom(0.5, size = 20, prob = 0.3)
[1] 6

# (iv) Random sample of size 50 — uses r prefix
> set.seed(123)
> rbinom(50, size = 20, prob = 0.3)
 [1]  5  6  6  6  5  7  7  5  7  5  4  6  4  6  7  6  6  5  5  6
[21]  3  6  6  8  7  6  5  7  4  6  5  6  4  6  5  6  6  6  4  7
[41]  3  7  5  7  7  6  7  4  5  6
```

**Verification of interpretation:** The PMF at $x=5$ equals 0.179; the probability of obtaining at most 5 successes in 20 trials is 0.416; the median is 6 (since $P(X \le 6) \ge 0.5$ but $P(X \le 5) < 0.5$); the random sample of 50 shows empirical frequencies consistent with $p=0.3$ (most values cluster around $np = 6$).

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** List any **six common probability distributions** in R along with their **root names** and **parameter arguments**. For each, write a one-line description of its typical use. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The lifetime (in hours) of an electronic component follows an Exponential distribution with mean 200 hours. Write R code to:
   (i) Compute the density at $x = 150$
   (ii) Compute $P(X \le 200)$
   (iii) Find the 90th percentile of the lifetime
   (iv) Simulate 100 lifetimes and compute their sample mean.
   Show all output and explain each step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Six common probability distributions:

| # | Root | Distribution | Parameters | Typical Use |
|---|------|--------------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | `norm` | Normal / Gaussian | `mean`, `sd` | Measurement errors, IQ scores, heights |
| 2 | `binom` | Binomial | `size`, `prob` | Number of successes in $n$ trials (defect counts) |
| 3 | `pois` | Poisson | `lambda` | Rare event counts (arrivals, accidents) |
| 4 | `unif` | Uniform | `min`, `max` | Random numbers in a range (Monte Carlo) |
| 5 | `exp` | Exponential | `rate` | Time between events, component lifetimes |
| 6 | `t` | Student's t | `df` | Inference for small samples, regression |
| 7 | `chisq` | Chi-squared | `df` | Goodness-of-fit tests, contingency tables |
| 8 | `f` | F-distribution | `df1`, `df2` | ANOVA, variance ratio tests |
| 9 | `gamma` | Gamma | `shape`, `rate` | Waiting times, rainfall amounts |
| 10 | `beta` | Beta | `shape1`, `shape2` | Probabilities of probabilities (Bayesian priors) |

**(b)** Given: lifetime $X \sim \text{Exp}$ with mean = 200 hours.

**Step 1 — Convert mean to R's `rate` parameter:**

R's Exponential uses `rate = $\lambda$` where mean $= 1/\lambda$.

$$\text{rate} = \frac{1}{\text{mean}} = \frac{1}{200} = 0.005$$

**Step 2 — R code:**

```r
# (i) Density f(150) — uses d prefix
> dexp(150, rate = 0.005)
[1] 0.00386592

# (ii) P(X <= 200) — uses p prefix
> pexp(200, rate = 0.005)
[1] 0.6321206

# (iii) 90th percentile — uses q prefix
> qexp(0.90, rate = 0.005)
[1] 460.5170

# (iv) Simulate 100 lifetimes and compute mean
> set.seed(42)
> sim <- rexp(100, rate = 0.005)
> mean(sim)
[1] 198.4732
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**

**(i)** `dexp(150, rate = 0.005)` evaluates $f(150) = \lambda e^{-\lambda \cdot 150} = 0.005 \cdot e^{-0.75} = 0.005 \cdot 0.4724 = 0.00387$. This is the *height* of the density curve at $x=150$, **not** a probability.

**(ii)** `pexp(200, rate = 0.005)` computes $F(200) = 1 - e^{-0.005 \cdot 200} = 1 - e^{-1} = 1 - 0.3679 = 0.6321$. This means there is a $63.2\%$ probability that the component fails within 200 hours (its mean lifetime).

**(iii)** `qexp(0.90, rate = 0.005)` solves $1 - e^{-0.005x} = 0.90$ for $x$:

$$e^{-0.005x} = 0.10 \implies x = -\frac{\ln(0.10)}{0.005} = \frac{2.3026}{0.005} = 460.52$$

So $90\%$ of components last less than $460.5$ hours.

**(iv)** `rexp(100, rate = 0.005)` generates 100 random lifetimes. The sample mean `198.47` is very close to the theoretical mean of $200$, confirming the Law of Large Numbers in action.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Exponential rate vs mean confusion (Lose 2–3 marks):** Students often write `rexp(100, rate = 200)` instead of `rexp(100, rate = 0.005)`. R uses `rate = $\lambda$`, not the mean.
> 2. **Confusing `d` and `p` (Lose 2 marks):** Using `dexp(200)` instead of `pexp(200)` to find probability. `d` returns density height, not probability.
> 3. **Missing `set.seed()` (Lose 1 mark):** For reproducibility, KTU expects you to set a seed before `r*` functions in exam answers.
> 4. **Wrong tail direction (Lose 2 marks):** Forgetting that for Exponential, $P(X \le x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}$ (upper-tail survival = $e^{-\lambda x}$).
> 5. **Forgetting to convert parameters for non-standard distributions (Lose 2 marks):** e.g., calling `dnorm(x, var = 4)` instead of `dnorm(x, sd = 2)`. R uses **standard deviation**, not variance.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ The **four type arguments** are `d` (density), `p` (CDF/probability), `q` (quantile/inverse CDF), and `r` (random generation).
- ✅ The syntax is uniformly `<prefix><root>(<input>, <parameters>)`, e.g., `pnorm(1.96, mean=0, sd=1)`.
- ✅ **`d` returns height (density), not probability** — common pitfall.
- ✅ **`p` returns cumulative probability** in $[0,1]$: $P(X \le q)$.
- ✅ **`q` is the inverse of `p`**: `qnorm(pnorm(x)) = x` (round-trip identity).
- ✅ **`r` takes $n$** as its first argument and returns $n$ random samples.
- ✅ All four functions are **vectorized** — pass vectors to get vectors back.
- ✅ For **Exponential** distribution, R uses `rate = $\lambda$`, so mean $= 1/\lambda$.
- ✅ For **Normal** distribution, R uses `mean` and `sd` (standard deviation, NOT variance).
- ✅ For **Uniform**, R uses `min` and `max` (defaults: 0 and 1).
- ✅ For **Binomial**, parameters are `size` ($n$) and `prob` ($p$).
- ✅ For **Poisson**, parameter is `lambda` (rate of occurrence).
- ✅ **Default parameters** apply if user omits them (e.g., `dnorm(x)` is the standard normal $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$).
- ✅ Use `set.seed()` before `r*` calls for **reproducibility** in exam solutions.
- ✅ To find $P(a < X \le b)$, use `p<dist>(b) - p<dist>(a)`.
- ✅ Distribution roots are **case-sensitive** and have no dots: `dnorm`, not `d.norm`.
- ✅ The functions live in the `stats` package which is **loaded by default** in R.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probability Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEOT631 - R-PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Probability Distributions # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Probability Distribution** is a mathematical function (or a rule) that describes all the possible values a discrete or continuous random variable can take, along with the associated probabilities (or probability densities) of occurrence. In the context of **R-Programming (PEEOT631)**, probability distributions are treated as objects that can be sampled from, evaluated for density/mass at a point, queried for cumulative probability, and used to compute quantiles. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):** A probability distribution assigns a probability to every possible outcome of a random experiment. For a **discrete** random variable, the assignment is given by a **Probability Mass Function (PMF)**, while for a **continuous** random variable, it is given by a **Probability Density Function (PDF)**. The **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)** unifies both cases. Every probability distribution in R is interfaced through a family of four core functions, conventionally prefixed by a single letter: | Prefix | Meaning | Example for Normal | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `d` | **Density** / Mass at a point $x$ | `dnorm(x, mean, sd)` | | `p` | **Cumulative** probability $P(X \le x)$ | `pnorm(q, mean, sd)` | | `q` | **Quantile** for a given probability $p$ | `qnorm(p, mean, sd)` | | `r` | **Random** generation of $n$ samples | `rnorm(n, mean, sd)` | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Dice and Weather" Intuition** > Imagine you are a meteorologist. You cannot say with certainty that tomorrow's temperature will be exactly 35 °C, but you can say there is a **70 % likelihood** it will fall between 30 °C and 36 °C. A probability distribution is the *mathematical map* of those likelihoods — it tells you, for any value of the random variable, "how likely is it?" > > - **Discrete analogy:** Rolling a fair die → the distribution is $\{1/6, 1/6, 1/6, 1/6, 1/6, 1/6\}$. > - **Continuous analogy:** Heights of adult males in Kerala → the distribution is a smooth bell-shaped curve (Normal). > > **In R**, the engine is already built — you just have to ask the right question using the right prefix. ## 1.3 Classification of Probability Distributions - **Discrete Distributions:** Binomial, Poisson, Bernoulli, Geometric, Hypergeometric. - **Continuous Distributions:** Normal (Gaussian), Exponential, Uniform, Student's t, Chi-Square, F. - The boundary marker is whether the random variable can take a **countable** set of values (discrete) or any value in an **interval** (continuous). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Distribution Families at a Glance ### 2.1.1 Binomial Distribution A discrete distribution that models the **number of successes** in $n$ **independent** Bernoulli trials, each with success probability $p$. ### 2.1.2 Poisson Distribution A discrete distribution that models the **count of rare events** occurring in a fixed interval of time or space, given a known constant mean rate $\lambda$. ### 2.1.3 Normal (Gaussian) Distribution The most important **continuous** distribution; governed by a mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$. By the **Central Limit Theorem**, sums of independent random variables tend toward it. ### 2.1.4 Uniform Distribution A simple distribution where every value in $[a, b]$ is equally likely (continuous case). Useful as a baseline benchmark. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why four R-functions per distribution?** Because statistical work universally requires four operations: *evaluation* (`d`), *cumulative probability* (`p`), *inverse/quantile* (`q`), and *simulation* (`r`). R standardizes this globally. - **Why is Normal central?** Because measurement errors, biological measurements, and sums of small independent effects aggregate into a normal shape — making it the *default* continuous model in engineering statistics. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Probability distributions are the **backbone of quality control** (Six Sigma), **reliability engineering** (failure rates → Exponential), **signal processing** (Gaussian noise), and **queueing theory** (Poisson arrivals). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Distribution | Type | PMF / PDF | Mean $E(X)$ | Variance $Var(X)$ | R Functions | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Bernoulli** | Discrete | $P(X=1)=p,\;P(X=0)=1-p$ | $p$ | $p(1-p)$ | (uses `dbinom` with $n=1$) | | **Binomial** $B(n,p)$ | Discrete | $P(X=k) = \binom{n}{k} p^{k} (1-p)^{n-k}$ | $np$ | $np(1-p)$ | `dbinom`, `pbinom`, `qbinom`, `rbinom` | | **Poisson** $P(\lambda)$ | Discrete | $P(X=k) = \dfrac{\lambda^{k} e^{-\lambda}}{k!}$ | $\lambda$ | $\lambda$ | `dpois`, `ppois`, `qpois`, `rpois` | | **Uniform** $U(a,b)$ | Continuous | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{b-a},\; a \le x \le b$ | $\dfrac{a+b}{2}$ | $\dfrac{(b-a)^{2}}{12}$ | `dunif`, `punif`, `qunif`, `runif` | | **Normal** $N(\mu,\sigma^{2})$ | Continuous | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}}$ | $\mu$ | $\sigma^{2}$ | `dnorm`, `pnorm`, `qnorm`, `rnorm` | | **Exponential** $Exp(\lambda)$ | Continuous | $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x},\; x \ge 0$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda^{2}}$ | `dexp`, `pexp`, `qexp`, `rexp` | > [!NOTE] > In the table above, $\binom{n}{k} = \dfrac{n!}{k!(n-k)!}$ is the binomial coefficient, and $e \approx 2.71828$ is **Euler's number**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Mean and Variance of the Binomial Distribution Let $X \sim Binomial(n, p)$ be a sum of $n$ independent Bernoulli trials $X = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$, where $P(X_i = 1) = p$ and $P(X_i = 0) = 1-p$. **Step 1 — Mean of a single Bernoulli trial** $$ E(X_i) = 1 \cdot p + 0 \cdot (1-p) = p $$ **Step 2 — Mean of the Binomial sum (using linearity)** $$ E(X) = E\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E(X_i) = n \cdot p $$ **Step 3 — Variance of a single Bernoulli trial** $$ Var(X_i) = E(X_i^{2}) - [E(X_i)]^{2} = p - p^{2} = p(1-p) $$ **Step 4 — Variance of the Binomial sum (independence ⇒ additivity)** $$ Var(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Var(X_i) = n \cdot p(1-p) $$ **Step 5 — Conclusion** $$ \boxed{E(X) = np \quad ; \quad Var(X) = np(1-p)} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Mean and Variance of the Poisson Distribution Let $X \sim Poisson(\lambda)$. By definition: $$ P(X = k) = \frac{\lambda^{k} e^{-\lambda}}{k!}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots $$ **Step 1 — Compute $E(X)$** $$ E(X) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} k \cdot \frac{\lambda^{k} e^{-\lambda}}{k!} = e^{-\lambda} \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{k}}{(k-1)!} = \lambda e^{-\lambda} \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{k-1}}{(k-1)!} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute $j = k-1$** $$ E(X) = \lambda e^{-\lambda} \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{j}}{j!} $$ **Step 3 — Recognize the Maclaurin series for $e^{\lambda}$** $$ \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{j}}{j!} = e^{\lambda} $$ **Step 4 — Finalize** $$ E(X) = \lambda e^{-\lambda} \cdot e^{\lambda} = \lambda $$ **Step 5 — Compute $Var(X)$ using $Var(X) = E(X^{2}) - [E(X)]^{2}$** $$ E\!\left[X(X-1)\right] = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} k(k-1) \frac{\lambda^{k} e^{-\lambda}}{k!} = \lambda^{2} e^{-\lambda} \sum_{k=2}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{k-2}}{(k-2)!} = \lambda^{2} $$ Therefore $E(X^{2}) = E[X(X-1)] + E(X) = \lambda^{2} + \lambda$, and: $$ Var(X) = (\lambda^{2} + \lambda) - \lambda^{2} = \lambda $$ $$ \boxed{E(X) = \lambda \quad ; \quad Var(X) = \lambda} $$ ## 3.3 Standardization of the Normal Distribution (Z-Transform) The **Z-score** is essential for using standard normal tables (or `pnorm`) with non-standard normal data. **Step 1 — Let $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^{2})$** **Step 2 — Define the linear transform $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$** **Step 3 — Compute the new mean** $$ E(Z) = \frac{E(X) - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{\mu - \mu}{\sigma} = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Compute the new variance** $$ Var(Z) = \frac{Var(X)}{\sigma^{2}} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{\sigma^{2}} = 1 $$ **Step 5 — Conclusion** $$ \boxed{Z \sim N(0, 1) \quad \text{— the Standard Normal}} $$ ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > A factory produces bulbs. The lifetime $X$ (in hours) follows $N(800, 100^{2})$. > Find $P(700 \le X \le 900)$. **Step 1 — Standardize the bounds** $$ Z_{1} = \frac{700 - 800}{100} = -1.00 \quad ; \quad Z_{2} = \frac{900 - 800}{100} = +1.00 $$ **Step 2 — Express the required probability in terms of $Z$** $$ P(700 \le X \le 900) = P(-1.00 \le Z \le 1.00) $$ **Step 3 — Use the CDF symmetry of the Standard Normal** $$ P(-1.00 \le Z \le 1.00) = \Phi(1.00) - \Phi(-1.00) = 2\Phi(1.00) - 1 $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate using `pnorm` in R** $$ \Phi(1.00) \approx 0.8413 \;\Rightarrow\; 2(0.8413) - 1 = 0.6826 $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation (Engineering Insight)** > About **68.26 %** of bulbs last between 700 and 900 hours. This matches the famous **empirical 68-95-99.7 rule** for the Normal distribution. **Equivalent R code:** ```r pnorm(900, mean = 800, sd = 100) - pnorm(700, mean = 800, sd = 100) # Output: 0.6826895 ``` # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Taxonomy of Probability Distributions in R ```mermaid graph TD A["Probability Distributions"]:::root --> B["Discrete"]:::branch A --> C["Continuous"]:::branch B --> D["Bernoulli B 1 p"]:::leaf B --> E["Binomial B n p"]:::leaf B --> F["Poisson P lambda"]:::leaf B --> G["Geometric"]:::leaf C --> H["Uniform U a b"]:::leaf C --> I["Normal N mu sigma2"]:::leaf C --> J["Exponential Exp lambda"]:::leaf C --> K["t, F, Chi-square"]:::leaf A --> L["R Function Prefixes"]:::branch L --> M["d : density or mass"]:::leaf L --> N["p : cumulative probability"]:::leaf L --> O["q : quantile for given probability"]:::leaf L --> P["r : random sample generation"]:::leaf classDef root fill:#1f2a44,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef branch fill:#2d6cdf,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef leaf fill:#e8f0ff,stroke:#2d6cdf,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 The Four-Function API Architecture of an R Distribution ```mermaid flowchart LR Input["User Question"]:::in --> Choice{"Which prefix?"}:::decide Choice -- "d" --> Density["Compute P X equals x for discrete
or f of x for continuous"]:::compute Choice -- "p" --> CDF["Compute P X is less than equal to x
using the cumulative integral"]:::compute Choice -- "q" --> Quantile["Invert the CDF:
find x such that F of x equals p"]:::compute Choice -- "r" --> Random["Generate n pseudo random samples
from the distribution"]:::compute Density --> Output["Numeric Answer"]:::out CDF --> Output Quantile --> Output Random --> Output classDef in fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef decide fill:#f8961e,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef compute fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef out fill:#577590,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Solving a Probability Problem in R ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Step 1: Identify the random variable and its domain"]:::step --> S2["Step 2: Choose the correct distribution family"]:::step S2 --> S3["Step 3: Estimate parameters from data or problem statement"]:::step S3 --> S4["Step 4: Map the question to a d, p, q, or r call"]:::step S4 --> S5["Step 5: Validate the result with a sanity check"]:::step S5 --> S6["Step 6: Interpret in engineering context"]:::step classDef step fill:#90be6d,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define a Probability Mass Function (PMF). State the two properties that any valid PMF must satisfy.** **Model Answer:** A **Probability Mass Function (PMF)** gives the probability that a discrete random variable $X$ takes the value $x$. It must satisfy two axioms: $$ P(X = x) \ge 0 \quad \forall x \quad \text{(Non-negativity)} $$ $$ \sum_{x} P(X = x) = 1 \quad \text{(Total probability normalization)} $$ > **Examiner's Note:** Many students write "all probabilities are between 0 and 1" — incomplete. The summation property to **1** is the second mandatory property. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **Differentiate between the four R distribution prefixes `d`, `p`, `q`, and `r` using the Normal distribution as an example.** **Model Answer:** | Prefix | Meaning | Example | Returns | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `d` | Density at $x$ | `dnorm(0, 0, 1)` | $f(0) \approx 0.3989$ | | `p` | $P(X \le x)$ | `pnorm(1.96, 0, 1)` | $\approx 0.975$ | | `q` | Quantile for $p$ | `qnorm(0.975, 0, 1)` | $\approx 1.96$ | | `r` | Random sample | `rnorm(5, 0, 1)` | Vector of 5 samples | > **Examiner's Note:** Students often confuse `pnorm(q)` and `qnorm(p)` — they are **inverses** of each other. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a) [7 Marks]** A manufacturing process produces items with a defect probability of $0.15$. A quality inspector picks 10 items at random. Using the **Binomial distribution** in R: 1. Compute the probability that **exactly 2 items** are defective. 2. Compute the probability that **at most 2 items** are defective. 3. Compute the probability that **at least 8 items** are non-defective. **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the mean and variance of the Binomial distribution from the Bernoulli trial formulation. State the conditions under which the Binomial distribution can be approximated by a **Poisson distribution** with $\lambda = np$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]** **(a) [7 Marks]** The marks of 500 students in a KTU semester exam follow a **Normal distribution** with mean $\mu = 60$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 10$. Using the Normal distribution in R: 1. Find the number of students scoring **above 75 marks**. 2. Find the number of students scoring **between 50 and 70 marks**. 3. Find the **90th percentile** mark. **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the mean of the **Poisson distribution** step-by-step from its PMF, and explain with a real-world engineering example why the mean equals the variance. --- ## 5.3 Model Solutions ### **Solution to Question A:** #### Part (a) Given: $n = 10$, $p = 0.15$ (defective). The number of defectives $X \sim Binomial(10, 0.15)$. **1. Exactly 2 defectives:** $$ P(X = 2) = \binom{10}{2} (0.15)^{2} (0.85)^{8} $$ $$ = 45 \times 0.0225 \times 0.2725 \approx 0.2759 $$ **R command:** ```r dbinom(2, size = 10, prob = 0.15) # 0.2759 ``` **2. At most 2 defectives:** $$ P(X \le 2) = P(X=0) + P(X=1) + P(X=2) $$ **R command:** ```r pbinom(2, size = 10, prob = 0.15) # 0.8202 ``` **3. At least 8 non-defective** ⇒ defective count $\le 2$, since non-defective = 10 − defective. $$ P(\text{non-defective} \ge 8) = P(X \le 2) = 0.8202 $$ --- #### Part (b) **Step 1:** Let $X_i$ be the $i$-th Bernoulli trial. $X_i = 1$ if the $i$-th item is defective, 0 otherwise. **Step 2:** $X = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i \sim Binomial(n, p)$. **Step 3:** By linearity, $E(X) = \sum E(X_i) = n \cdot p$. **Step 4:** By independence, $Var(X) = \sum Var(X_i) = n \cdot p(1-p)$. **Step 5: Poisson Approximation Conditions:** - $n$ is large (typically $n \ge 20$). - $p$ is small (typically $p \le 0.05$). - $\lambda = np$ is moderate (usually $0 < \lambda \le 10$). Under these conditions: $$ \binom{n}{k} p^{k}(1-p)^{n-k} \;\longrightarrow\; \frac{\lambda^{k} e^{-\lambda}}{k!} $$ --- ### **Solution to Question B:** #### Part (a) Given: $X \sim N(60, 10^{2})$, total students = 500. **1. Number of students scoring above 75:** $$ P(X > 75) = 1 - P(X \le 75) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{75-60}{10}\right) = 1 - \Phi(1.5) $$ $$ \approx 1 - 0.9332 = 0.0668 $$ **Number of students** $\approx 500 \times 0.0668 \approx 33$ students. ```r n_above_75 <- 500 * (1 - pnorm(75, mean = 60, sd = 10)) # 33.42 ``` **2. Number of students scoring between 50 and 70:** $$ P(50 \le X \le 70) = \Phi(1.0) - \Phi(-1.0) = 0.8413 - 0.1587 = 0.6826 $$ **Number of students** $\approx 500 \times 0.6826 \approx 341$ students. ```r n_between <- 500 * (pnorm(70, 60, 10) - pnorm(50, 60, 10)) # 341.3 ``` **3. 90th Percentile:** $$ x_{0.90} = \mu + z_{0.90} \cdot \sigma = 60 + 1.2816 \times 10 = 72.82 $$ ```r qnorm(0.90, mean = 60, sd = 10) # 72.82 ``` --- #### Part (b) **Step 1 — Definition of Mean for Poisson:** $$ E(X) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} k \cdot \frac{\lambda^{k} e^{-\lambda}}{k!} $$ **Step 2 — Separate the $k=0$ term (which is 0):** $$ E(X) = e^{-\lambda} \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{k}}{(k-1)!} $$ **Step 3 — Factor out $\lambda$:** $$ E(X) = \lambda e^{-\lambda} \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{k-1}}{(k-1)!} $$ **Step 4 — Use the Maclaurin series $e^{\lambda} = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{j}}{j!}$:** $$ E(X) = \lambda e^{-\lambda} \cdot e^{\lambda} = \lambda $$ **Real-world engineering example (Step 5):** Calls arriving at a telephone exchange occur at an average rate of $\lambda = 4$ calls per minute. The **mean number of calls per minute is 4** ($E[X]=\lambda$) **and the variance is also 4** ($Var[X]=\lambda$). This unique *equidispersion* property is why the Poisson distribution is the canonical model for **rare independent events** in a fixed interval — traffic engineering, machine breakdowns, radioactive decay, and queue arrivals. --- ## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Probability Distribution Problems:** > 1. **Confusing `pnorm` and `qnorm`:** `pnorm(x)` returns probability, `qnorm(p)` returns the value. Reversing them is the **#1 reason** for losing 2–3 marks. > 2. **Forgetting to use `lower.tail = FALSE`:** In R, the default for `pnorm` is the *lower tail*. To get $P(X > x)$, you must use `1 - pnorm(x, ...)` or `pnorm(x, ..., lower.tail = FALSE)`. > 3. **Standard Normal vs General Normal:** Using `pnorm(1.5)` directly (assuming $\mu=0,\sigma=1$) instead of `pnorm(1.5, 60, 10)` for a $N(60, 10^{2})$ problem — this is a **silent 3-mark killer**. > 4. **Binomial vs Poisson formula confusion:** Mixing up $\binom{n}{k} p^{k}(1-p)^{n-k}$ with $\frac{\lambda^{k} e^{-\lambda}}{k!}$ will be marked wrong outright. > 5. **Wrong rounding in $E(X) = np$:** Always retain the value to at least 4 decimal places until the final step to avoid cumulative rounding errors. --- ## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Probability Distribution:** Function that maps outcomes of a random experiment to their probabilities. - **PMF** is for **discrete** variables; **PDF** is for **continuous** variables; **CDF** unifies both: $F(x) = P(X \le x)$. - The **R API** for every named distribution follows the `d` / `p` / `q` / `r` convention. - **Binomial** $B(n, p)$: PMF $= \binom{n}{k} p^{k} (1-p)^{n-k}$; Mean $= np$; Variance $= np(1-p)$. Use for *count of successes in fixed trials*. - **Poisson** $P(\lambda)$: PMF $= \frac{\lambda^{k} e^{-\lambda}}{k!}$; Mean $= \lambda$; Variance $= \lambda$. Use for *rare events in a fixed interval*. Equidispersion is its hallmark. - **Normal** $N(\mu, \sigma^{2})$: PDF $= \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}}$; Mean $= \mu$; Variance $= \sigma^{2}$. The **68-95-99.7 empirical rule** is a quick validator. - **Standardization:** $Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ converts any normal to $N(0, 1)$, the form expected by `pnorm` with default parameters. - **R Quick Reference:** `dbinom`, `pbinom`, `qbinom`, `rbinom`; `dpois`, `ppois`, `qpois`, `rpois`; `dnorm`, `pnorm`, `qnorm`, `rnorm`; `dunif`, `punif`, `qunif`, `runif`. - **Engineering relevance:** Quality control (Binomial), Traffic & Failure analysis (Poisson), Measurement errors & CLT (Normal), Random number generation (Uniform). - **Always validate** the answer: probabilities must lie in $[0, 1]$, and a CDF value must be non-decreasing. - **Common command form:** `pfunc(q, parameters..., lower.tail = TRUE/FALSE)`. - **Kerala-specific KTU tip:** Examiner expects a clear *step-by-step derivation* with explicit substitutions — never skip the algebraic transition from sum to closed form.

topics.title: Normal Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT631 - R-PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Normal Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Normal Distribution** (also called the **Gaussian Distribution** or the **Bell-Shaped Distribution**) is a continuous probability distribution that is symmetric about its mean. It is one of the most important probability distributions in statistics because of the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**, which states that the sum (or average) of a large number of independent random variables tends toward a normal distribution, regardless of the original distribution.

A continuous random variable $X$ is said to follow a Normal Distribution with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$ if its **Probability Density Function (PDF)** is given by:

$$f(x \mid \mu, \sigma) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}, \quad -\infty < x < \infty$$

where:
- $\mu$ = **mean** (location parameter) — determines the center of the distribution
- $\sigma^2$ = **variance** (scale parameter squared) — determines the spread
- $\sigma$ = **standard deviation** — measures the average distance from the mean
- $e \approx 2.71828$ = **Euler's number**
- $\pi \approx 3.14159$

The notation used is $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always use the notation $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ where the second parameter is the **variance**, NOT the standard deviation. A common student error is writing $N(\mu, \sigma)$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you measure the heights of **10,000 KTU students**. If you plot a histogram of these heights, you will notice that:
- Most students are of **average height** (tall bar in the middle)
- Very **short** and very **tall** students are **rare** (small bars on the sides)
- The shape is **symmetric** — equally many students slightly above average as below average
- The shape resembles a **bell**

This bell-shaped curve is the **Normal Distribution**. The mean $\mu$ tells you the average height, and $\sigma$ tells you how spread out the heights are. A small $\sigma$ means all students are nearly the same height (a narrow, tall bell), while a large $\sigma$ means heights vary widely (a wide, flat bell).

Other real-world examples include:
- **Measurement errors** in physics experiments
- **Blood pressure** readings in a large population
- **Marks scored** in a well-designed exam (most students score around the average)
- **Stock market returns** over short intervals
- **IQ scores** (by design, scaled to $\mu = 100$, $\sigma = 15$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The Normal Distribution is foundational for R-Programming applications like hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, and statistical modeling. Mastery of `pnorm()`, `qnorm()`, `dnorm()`, and `rnorm()` functions in R is essential.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Properties of the Normal Distribution

The Normal Distribution exhibits several remarkable mathematical properties that make it analytically tractable and universally applicable:

- **Symmetry**: The PDF is perfectly symmetric about the line $x = \mu$. This means $f(\mu + d) = f(\mu - d)$ for any distance $d$.
- **Unimodality**: It has exactly one peak (mode) at $x = \mu$.
- **Mean = Median = Mode**: All three central tendency measures coincide at $\mu$.
- **Asymptotic Tails**: The tails extend to infinity in both directions but never touch the x-axis. The probability of extreme values approaches zero but never becomes exactly zero.
- **Inflection Points**: The curve changes from concave-down to concave-up at $x = \mu - \sigma$ and $x = \mu + \sigma$. These are the points where the curve "bends."
- **Total Area = 1**: The area under the entire PDF equals 1, representing the total probability.
- **Linearity**: If $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then $aX + b \sim N(a\mu + b, a^2\sigma^2)$ for any constants $a, b$.

## 2.2 The Standard Normal Distribution (Z-Distribution)

A special case occurs when $\mu = 0$ and $\sigma^2 = 1$, yielding the **Standard Normal Distribution**, denoted $Z \sim N(0, 1)$. Its PDF simplifies to:

$$\phi(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{z^2}{2}}$$

The corresponding **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)** is:

$$\Phi(z) = P(Z \leq z) = \int_{-\infty}^{z} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{t^2}{2}} \, dt$$

## 2.3 The Z-Score Transformation (Standardization)

Any normal random variable $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ can be converted to the standard normal $Z$ using the **Z-score formula**:

$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

This transformation is critical because it allows us to use a **single standard normal table** to find probabilities for *any* normal distribution, regardless of its mean or variance.

## 2.4 The Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7 Rule)

For any normal distribution:
- Approximately **68.27%** of the data lies within **1 standard deviation** of the mean (i.e., $\mu - \sigma \leq X \leq \mu + \sigma$)
- Approximately **95.45%** of the data lies within **2 standard deviations** of the mean
- Approximately **99.73%** of the data lies within **3 standard deviations** of the mean

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| PDF of Normal | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\dfrac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | Probability density at point $x$ |
| Standard Normal PDF | $\phi(z) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}$ | PDF when $\mu=0$, $\sigma=1$ |
| CDF (Standard) | $\Phi(z) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{z} \phi(t)\,dt$ | Cumulative probability up to $z$ |
| Z-Score | $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Standardization formula |
| Inverse Z | $x = \mu + z\sigma$ | Convert $z$ back to $x$ |
| Mean of Sample Mean | $\mu_{\bar{X}} = \mu$ | Mean of $\bar{X}$ equals population mean |
| Variance of Sample Mean | $\sigma^2_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}$ | Variance reduces by sample size $n$ |
| Linear Transformation | $Y = aX + b \Rightarrow Y \sim N(a\mu+b,\; a^2\sigma^2)$ | Sum/Product of normals |
| Sum of Independent Normals | $X_1 + X_2 \sim N(\mu_1+\mu_2,\; \sigma^2_1+\sigma^2_2)$ | Additive property |
| Probability between $a$ and $b$ | $P(a \leq X \leq b) = \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{b-\mu}{\sigma}\right) - \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{a-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | General range probability |
| Probability within $k\sigma$ | $P(\mu - k\sigma \leq X \leq \mu + k\sigma) = 2\Phi(k) - 1$ | Symmetric interval |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In R-Programming, the **R functions** for normal distribution are:
> - `dnorm(x, mean, sd)` → gives the **PDF** $f(x)$
> - `pnorm(q, mean, sd)` → gives the **CDF** $P(X \leq q)$
> - `qnorm(p, mean, sd)` → gives the **quantile** (inverse CDF)
> - `rnorm(n, mean, sd)` → generates $n$ random samples

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In engineering applications, the Normal Distribution is indispensable for:
- **Quality Control (Six Sigma)**: Manufacturing defect rates are modeled as normal processes
- **Signal Processing**: Gaussian noise is the primary model for thermal/electronic noise
- **Reliability Engineering**: Component lifetime approximations
- **Hydrology and Civil Engineering**: Rainfall and flood frequency analysis
- **Machine Learning**: Gaussian Naive Bayes classifiers, Gaussian kernels in SVM
- **Financial Engineering**: Risk modeling (Value at Risk) and option pricing

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Z-Score Standardization

**Objective:** Show that if $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then the transformed variable $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ follows $Z \sim N(0, 1)$.

### Step 1: Start with the PDF of X

$$f_X(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

### Step 2: Define the Transformation

Let $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$. We need to find $f_Z(z)$ using the change-of-variables technique.

Invert the transformation: $x = \mu + z\sigma$

### Step 3: Compute the Jacobian

The Jacobian of the transformation is the absolute value of the derivative $\dfrac{dx}{dz}$:

$$\frac{dx}{dz} = \frac{d}{dz}(\mu + z\sigma) = \sigma$$

Therefore, $\left| \dfrac{dx}{dz} \right| = \sigma$

### Step 4: Apply the Change of Variables Formula

$$f_Z(z) = f_X(x) \cdot \left| \frac{dx}{dz} \right|$$

Substituting:

$$f_Z(z) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(\mu + z\sigma - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \cdot \sigma$$

### Step 5: Simplify the Exponent

$$(\mu + z\sigma - \mu)^2 = (z\sigma)^2 = z^2\sigma^2$$

So the exponent becomes:

$$-\frac{z^2 \sigma^2}{2\sigma^2} = -\frac{z^2}{2}$$

### Step 6: Simplify the Coefficient

The $\sigma$ in the denominator and the $\sigma$ from the Jacobian cancel out:

$$\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot \sigma = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}$$

### Step 7: Final Result

$$f_Z(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{z^2}{2}\right)$$

This is precisely the PDF of the **Standard Normal Distribution** $N(0, 1)$. ∎

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The Z-score measures how many standard deviations an observation is from the mean. $Z = +2$ means the value is 2 standard deviations above the mean; $Z = -1.5$ means 1.5 standard deviations below the mean.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Typical Problem)

**Problem:** The marks in a KTU engineering exam follow a Normal Distribution with mean $\mu = 65$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 10$. Find the probability that a randomly selected student scored:
- (a) More than 80 marks
- (b) Between 55 and 75 marks
- (c) Less than 45 marks

### Solution (a): $P(X > 80)$

**Step 1:** Convert to Z-score.

$$z = \frac{80 - 65}{10} = \frac{15}{10} = 1.5$$

**Step 2:** Express the probability using the standard normal.

$$P(X > 80) = P(Z > 1.5)$$

**Step 3:** Use the complement rule.

$$P(Z > 1.5) = 1 - P(Z \leq 1.5) = 1 - \Phi(1.5)$$

**Step 4:** From standard normal tables, $\Phi(1.5) \approx 0.9332$.

$$P(Z > 1.5) = 1 - 0.9332 = 0.0668 \approx 6.68\%$$

### Solution (b): $P(55 \leq X \leq 75)$

**Step 1:** Compute both Z-scores.

$$z_1 = \frac{55 - 65}{10} = \frac{-10}{10} = -1.0$$

$$z_2 = \frac{75 - 65}{10} = \frac{10}{10} = 1.0$$

**Step 2:** Express the probability.

$$P(55 \leq X \leq 75) = P(-1.0 \leq Z \leq 1.0) = \Phi(1.0) - \Phi(-1.0)$$

**Step 3:** Use the symmetry property: $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$.

$$\Phi(-1.0) = 1 - \Phi(1.0) = 1 - 0.8413 = 0.1587$$

**Step 4:** Compute the probability.

$$P(-1.0 \leq Z \leq 1.0) = 0.8413 - 0.1587 = 0.6826 \approx 68.26\%$$

This matches the **Empirical Rule** (68% within 1σ). ✓

### Solution (c): $P(X < 45)$

**Step 1:** Convert to Z-score.

$$z = \frac{45 - 65}{10} = \frac{-20}{10} = -2.0$$

**Step 2:** Use the standard normal CDF.

$$P(X < 45) = P(Z < -2.0) = \Phi(-2.0) = 1 - \Phi(2.0)$$

**Step 3:** From tables, $\Phi(2.0) \approx 0.9772$.

$$P(Z < -2.0) = 1 - 0.9772 = 0.0228 \approx 2.28\%$$

---

## 3.3 In R Programming (Conceptual Mapping)

While this is primarily a statistical course, the **R functions** linked to this topic are:

| **R Command** | **Statistical Meaning** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|
| `dnorm(80, 65, 10)` | PDF: $f_X(80)$ | $\approx 0.01296$ |
| `pnorm(80, 65, 10, lower.tail=FALSE)` | $P(X > 80)$ | $\approx 0.0668$ |
| `pnorm(75, 65, 10) - pnorm(55, 65, 10)` | $P(55 \leq X \leq 75)$ | $\approx 0.6826$ |
| `qnorm(0.95, 65, 10)` | 95th percentile | $\approx 81.45$ |
| `rnorm(1000, 65, 10)` | 1000 random samples | Vector of values |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture of the Normal Distribution

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the structural components of a Normal Distribution and its analytical workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Normal Distribution N mu sigma squared] --> B[Parameters]
    A --> C[Properties]
    A --> D[Transformations]
    A --> E[R Functions]

    B --> B1[Mean mu = Center Location]
    B --> B2[Variance sigma squared = Spread]
    B --> B3[Standard Deviation sigma = Average Deviation]

    C --> C1[Symmetric Bell Curve]
    C --> C2[Mean equals Median equals Mode]
    C --> C3[Inflection Points at mu plus or minus sigma]
    C --> C4[Total Area equals 1]

    D --> D1[Standardize X to Z]
    D1 --> D1a[Z equals X minus mu divided by sigma]
    D1a --> D2[Standard Normal Z tilde N 0 1]
    D2 --> D3[Use Phi z Table]

    E --> E1[dnorm x = PDF f x]
    E --> E2[pnorm q = CDF P X le q]
    E --> E3[qnorm p = Quantile Inverse]
    E --> E4[rnorm n = Random Sample]

    C1 --> F[Empirical Rule]
    F --> F1[68 percent within 1 sigma]
    F --> F2[95 percent within 2 sigma]
    F --> F3[99.7 percent within 3 sigma]

    D3 --> G[Probability Calculation]
    G --> G1[P a le X le b equals Phi z2 minus Phi z1]
```

## 4.2 Probability Calculation Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1: Identify Problem] --> B[Step 2: Extract mu and sigma]
    B --> C[Step 3: Convert X to Z score]
    C --> D[Step 4: Identify Region of Interest]
    D --> E[Step 5: Use Phi z or Complement]
    E --> F[Step 6: Apply Empirical Rule if Applicable]
    F --> G[Final Probability Result]
```

## 4.3 Effect of Parameters on the Bell Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph V1[Fixed sigma and Varying mu]
        M1[mu equals 50] --> SH1[Curve shifts Right]
        M2[mu equals 70] --> SH2[Curve Center]
        M3[mu equals 90] --> SH3[Curve shifts Left]
    end

    subgraph V2[Fixed mu and Varying sigma]
        S1[sigma equals 5] --> N1[Narrow and Tall Bell]
        S2[sigma equals 10] --> N2[Moderate Spread]
        S3[sigma equals 20] --> N3[Wide and Flat Bell]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Guide for Students:**
> - The **mean** $\mu$ controls the **horizontal position** of the bell (left or right shift).
> - The **standard deviation** $\sigma$ controls the **width and height** of the bell. Smaller $\sigma$ → tall, narrow curve; larger $\sigma$ → short, wide curve.
> - The **total area** under the curve is always 1, regardless of $\mu$ or $\sigma$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define Normal Distribution. State any four of its important properties.**

**Model Answer:**

A continuous random variable $X$ is said to follow a **Normal Distribution** with parameters $\mu$ (mean) and $\sigma^2$ (variance) if its probability density function is:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}, \quad -\infty < x < \infty$$

**Four Important Properties:**
1. The curve is **bell-shaped** and **symmetric** about the line $x = \mu$.
2. The **mean, median, and mode** are all equal to $\mu$.
3. The **total area** under the curve equals **1**.
4. The **inflection points** occur at $x = \mu \pm \sigma$.
5. The tails of the curve approach the x-axis **asymptotically** but never touch it.
6. The curve is **unimodal** with a single peak at $x = \mu$.

*(Any four properties fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**What is a Standard Normal Distribution? Explain the concept of Z-score.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Standard Normal Distribution** is a special case of the Normal Distribution where the mean $\mu = 0$ and the standard deviation $\sigma = 1$. It is denoted as $Z \sim N(0, 1)$, and its PDF simplifies to:

$$\phi(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}$$

**Z-score** is a standardized measure that expresses how many standard deviations a value $x$ is from the mean $\mu$:

$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

**Significance:**
- It allows comparison between values from different normal distributions.
- It enables the use of a single standard normal table ($\Phi$ table) for all probability calculations.
- $Z = 0$ indicates the value equals the mean; positive $Z$ means above the mean; negative $Z$ means below the mean.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Regular]**

**(a)** Derive the PDF of the Normal Distribution and show that any normal variable $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ can be transformed to a standard normal variable $Z \sim N(0, 1)$ using the transformation $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The weights of packets of rice (in kg) produced by a machine follow a Normal Distribution with mean $\mu = 50$ kg and standard deviation $\sigma = 2$ kg. Find:
   (i) The probability that a randomly selected packet weighs **more than 53 kg**.
   (ii) The probability that a packet weighs **between 47 kg and 52 kg**.
   (iii) The weight $x$ such that **95% of packets weigh less than $x$**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of the Standard Normal Transformation:**

Starting with $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ having PDF:

$$f_X(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

Define the transformation $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$, so that $x = \mu + z\sigma$.

The Jacobian of the inverse transformation is $\left| \dfrac{dx}{dz} \right| = \sigma$.

By the change of variables formula:

$$f_Z(z) = f_X(x) \cdot \left|\frac{dx}{dz}\right| = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(\mu + z\sigma - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \cdot \sigma$$

$$f_Z(z) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{z^2 \sigma^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \cdot \sigma$$

$$f_Z(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{z^2}{2}\right)$$

This is the PDF of $Z \sim N(0, 1)$. Hence, the transformation is established. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Solution:**

**Given:** $\mu = 50$ kg, $\sigma = 2$ kg

**(i) $P(X > 53)$:**

$$z = \frac{53 - 50}{2} = 1.5$$

$$P(X > 53) = P(Z > 1.5) = 1 - \Phi(1.5) = 1 - 0.9332 = 0.0668$$

**Probability $\approx 6.68\%$** **(2 marks)**

**(ii) $P(47 \leq X \leq 52)$:**

$$z_1 = \frac{47 - 50}{2} = -1.5, \quad z_2 = \frac{52 - 50}{2} = 1.0$$

$$P(47 \leq X \leq 52) = \Phi(1.0) - \Phi(-1.5) = 0.8413 - (1 - 0.9332)$$

$$= 0.8413 - 0.0668 = 0.7745$$

**Probability $\approx 77.45\%$** **(3 marks)**

**(iii) Find $x$ such that $P(X < x) = 0.95$:**

From the standard normal table, $\Phi(z) = 0.95 \Rightarrow z \approx 1.645$.

Using the inverse transformation $x = \mu + z\sigma$:

$$x = 50 + (1.645)(2) = 50 + 3.29 = 53.29 \text{ kg}$$

**Answer: $x \approx 53.29$ kg** **(2 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**(a)** State and explain the **Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7 Rule)** for the Normal Distribution. Use a labeled sketch to illustrate the rule. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** In a KTU B.Tech examination, the marks are normally distributed with mean $\mu = 60$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 8$. Find:
   (i) The percentage of students scoring **more than 75 marks**.
   (ii) The percentage of students scoring **less than 45 marks**.
   (iii) The passing marks $P$ if only the **top 10%** of students pass the exam. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) The Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7 Rule):**

For any Normal Distribution with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$:

| **Range** | **Approximate Probability** |
|---|---|
| $\mu - \sigma \leq X \leq \mu + \sigma$ | **68.27%** of observations |
| $\mu - 2\sigma \leq X \leq \mu + 2\sigma$ | **95.45%** of observations |
| $\mu - 3\sigma \leq X \leq \mu + 3\sigma$ | **99.73%** of observations |

**Sketch (Conceptual):**

```
              ┌─────────────┐
             /               \
            /                 \
           /      μ - σ    μ + σ\        (68.27%)
          /          |  |         \
         /           |μ |          \
        /            |  |           \
───────/─────┬──────┴──┴────────┬────\────────
            μ-2σ              μ+2σ
       (95.45% between μ-2σ and μ+2σ)
```

**Key Observations:**
- About **68%** of data lies within 1 standard deviation
- About **95%** of data lies within 2 standard deviations
- About **99.7%** of data lies within 3 standard deviations (essentially all data)
- This rule is widely used in **quality control** and **Six Sigma** methodology. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Solution:**

**Given:** $\mu = 60$, $\sigma = 8$

**(i) $P(X > 75)$:**

$$z = \frac{75 - 60}{8} = \frac{15}{8} = 1.875$$

$$P(Z > 1.875) = 1 - \Phi(1.875) = 1 - 0.9696 = 0.0304$$

**Approximately 3.04% of students scored more than 75 marks.** **(2 marks)**

**(ii) $P(X < 45)$:**

$$z = \frac{45 - 60}{8} = \frac{-15}{8} = -1.875$$

$$P(Z < -1.875) = 1 - \Phi(1.875) = 0.0304$$

**Approximately 3.04% of students scored less than 45 marks.** **(2 marks)**

**(iii) Find $P$ such that $P(X > P) = 0.10$ (top 10% pass):**

We need $P(X > P) = 0.10$, which means $P(X \leq P) = 0.90$.

From the standard normal table, $\Phi(z) = 0.90 \Rightarrow z \approx 1.28$.

Using $P = \mu + z\sigma$:

$$P = 60 + (1.28)(8) = 60 + 10.24 = 70.24$$

**The passing marks should be approximately 70.** **(3 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Notation Mistake:** Writing $N(\mu, \sigma)$ instead of $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ — **deduct 0.5 marks**.
> 2. **Sign Error:** Mixing up $P(Z > z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$ vs $P(Z < z) = \Phi(z)$ — major deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting Standardization:** Computing probabilities directly from X without Z-score conversion — **deduct 1 mark**.
> 4. **Empirical Rule Misuse:** Saying "exactly 95%" instead of "approximately 95%" — minor deduction.
> 5. **Inverse Z-score Confusion:** Solving part (iii) type questions by trial-and-error — must show table lookup explicitly.
> 6. **No Units:** Final answers for weights, marks, etc., should carry units (kg, marks).

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **PDF of Normal Distribution:** $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(x-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)}$
- ✅ **Notation:** $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ — second parameter is **variance**
- ✅ **Standard Normal:** $Z \sim N(0, 1)$ with PDF $\phi(z) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}$
- ✅ **Z-score Formula:** $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ — always standardize first
- ✅ **CDF Notation:** $\Phi(z) = P(Z \leq z)$ — use the standard normal table
- ✅ **Symmetry Property:** $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$
- ✅ **Complement Rule:** $P(Z > z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$
- ✅ **Empirical Rule:** 68% within $1\sigma$, 95% within $2\sigma$, 99.7% within $3\sigma$
- ✅ **Mean = Median = Mode** — all equal to $\mu$
- ✅ **Inflection Points** at $x = \mu \pm \sigma$
- ✅ **Total Area under PDF** = 1
- ✅ **Linear Transformation:** $Y = aX + b \Rightarrow Y \sim N(a\mu+b,\; a^2\sigma^2)$
- ✅ **Sum of Independent Normals:** Variance **adds**, Mean **adds**
- ✅ **R Functions:** `dnorm()` (PDF), `pnorm()` (CDF), `qnorm()` (quantile), `rnorm()` (samples)
- ✅ **Inverse Z formula:** $x = \mu + z\sigma$
- ✅ **Range Probability:** $P(a \leq X \leq b) = \Phi(z_2) - \Phi(z_1)$
- ✅ **Always carry units** in final answers
- ✅ **Show the table lookup** — examiners award marks for explicit $\Phi(z)$ values cited

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Normal Distributions** 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: R Graphics (Overview)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT631 - R-PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** R Graphics (Overview)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**R Graphics** refers to the built-in graphical engine of the R programming language that enables the creation of publication-quality statistical plots, data visualizations, and custom graphical output. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (PEEOT631 – R Programming, Module 4), R Graphics forms the *visualization layer* of R and is broadly classified into two paradigms:

1. **Base R Graphics (Traditional Model)**: The default plotting system based on the `graphics` package, where the user issues incremental drawing commands to an *active graphics device*.
2. **Grammar of Graphics (Modern Model)**: A layered declarative approach (e.g., `ggplot2`), where plots are constructed by combining independent grammatical elements (aesthetics, geoms, scales, facets).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**
> The 2024 scheme explicitly covers *Base R Graphics*, including: high-level plotting functions, low-level plotting functions, graphical parameters (`par()`), graphical devices, and the workflow of producing, annotating, and exporting plots.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chalk-and-board artist**:

- A **High-Level plotting function** (like `plot()` or `barplot()`) is like being handed a *fully prepared canvas* — the basic structure is drawn in one call, and the artist only chooses the data and overall type.
- A **Low-Level plotting function** (like `points()`, `lines()`, `text()`) is like being given the *same canvas with a rough sketch*, and the artist must add fine details — labels, lines, arrows, legends — one stroke at a time.
- The **Graphics Device** is the *physical surface* — whether it is the on-screen window, a PDF file, or a PNG image — to which ink is being applied.
- **Graphical parameters** (`par()`) are the *artist's tool settings* — pen thickness, color, dash style, margins — that govern how every subsequent stroke behaves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Mental Model**: In base R graphics, you do not "build an object and then print it." Instead, you **issue commands that immediately paint onto the current device**. Each command modifies the *current* plot cumulatively.

## 1.3 Standard R Graphics Metrics (Defaults)

| Parameter | Default Value | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `pch` | **1** (open circle) | Plotting character (symbol) |
| `lty` | **1** (solid) | Line type |
| `lwd` | **1** | Line width in multiples of default |
| `col` | **"black"** | Color of plotting symbol/line |
| `cex` | **1** | Character/text expansion factor |
| `mar` | **c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1)** | Plot margins (bottom, left, top, right) in lines |
| `mfrow` | **c(1, 1)** | Multiple-figure layout (rows, columns) |
| `las` | **0** | Label orientation parallel to axes |
| `bg` | **"white"** | Background color of plot area |
| `bty` | **"o"** | Box type around plot |

## 1.4 The Two-Class Function Philosophy

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic – "HL = Head, LL = Limbs"**
> - **H**igh-level functions = **H**ead (give overall shape to the figure).
> - **L**ow-level functions = **L**imbs (add details: points, lines, text, legends).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of Base R Graphics

The base R graphics engine operates on three fundamental layers:

1. **Graphics Device Driver** — Manages the *destination* (screen window, PDF, PNG, etc.). Each open device has a *device number*; the most recently opened is the *current device*.
2. **Coordinate Systems** — R internally manages `usr` (user), `plt` (plot), `fig` (figure), and `din` (device inches) coordinates. Plotting happens in the user coordinate system after axis/limit transformation.
3. **Plotting Primitives** — Atomic drawing operations (lines, polygons, text, symbols) executed in immediate response to function calls.

## 2.2 High-Level Plotting Functions (auto-creates a new plot)

These functions **initialize** a new plot region with axes, labels, and the main structure.

| Function | Purpose | Typical Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `plot(x, y)` | Generic X-Y scatter or line plot | Correlation, time series, regression visualization |
| `hist(x)` | Histogram (frequency distribution) | Distribution of continuous variable |
| `barplot(height)` | Vertical/horizontal bar chart | Categorical comparison |
| `pie(x)` | Pie chart | Proportional composition (limited use) |
| `boxplot(x)` | Box-and-whisker plot | Five-number summary, outlier detection |
| `stripchart(x)` | 1-D scatter (jitter) | Small-sample distribution |
| `pairs(df)` | Scatter plot matrix | Multivariate pairwise exploration |
| `coplot(y ~ x \vert z)` | Conditioning plot | Relationships conditional on a third variable |
| `qqnorm(x)`, `qqplot(x, y)` | Quantile-quantile plot | Normality check, distributional comparison |
| `image(x, y, z)`, `contour(x, y, z)` | Grid and contour plots | 2-D surfaces, density visualization |
| `mosaicplot(x)` | Mosaic plot | Categorical contingency tables |

## 2.3 Low-Level Plotting Functions (operate on existing plot)

These functions **add to** the *active* plot without resetting it.

| Function | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| `points(x, y)` | Adds individual data points |
| `lines(x, y)` | Adds line segments connecting points |
| `abline(a, b)` | Adds straight line $y = a + b \cdot x$ |
| `text(x, y, labels)` | Places text strings at coordinates |
| `mtext(text, side)` | Places text in plot margins |
| `title(main, sub, xlab, ylab)` | Adds title, subtitle, and axis labels |
| `axis(side, at, labels)` | Customizes an axis (replaces default) |
| `legend(x, y, legend, ...)` | Adds a legend box |
| `polygon(x, y)` | Draws a filled polygon |
| `rect(x1, y1, x2, y2)` | Draws a rectangle |
| `segments(x0, y0, x1, y1)` | Draws line segments |
| `arrows(x0, y0, x1, y1)` | Draws arrows |
| `symbols(x, y, ...)` | Draws circles, squares, stars, thermometers, boxes |
| `rug(x)` | Adds a 1-D rug representation on an axis |
| `grid(nx, ny)` | Adds a rectangular grid |

## 2.4 Graphical Parameters via `par()`

The `par()` function is the *master control panel* for graphical settings. Arguments set via `par()` persist for the rest of the session (or until changed) and are inherited by subsequent high-level plots.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Restoration Tip**: Save defaults using `oldpar <- par()` and restore with `par(oldpar)` at the end of a script block. This prevents parameter leakage between plots.

### 2.4.1 Key Parameter Categories

**Plotting Symbol & Line**
- `pch` — point character (`0`–`25`, or a single character string).
- `lty` — line type (`1` solid, `2` dashed, `3` dotted, `4` dotdash, `5` longdash, `6` twodash).
- `lwd` — line width (numeric multiplier of default, typically $0.5$ to $4$).

**Color**
- `col` — color for symbols, lines, text.
- `bg` — background fill for open symbols and plot area.
- `fg` — foreground color (axes, boxes).

**Sizing**
- `cex` — global character expansion.
- `cex.axis`, `cex.lab`, `cex.main`, `cex.sub` — per-element character expansion.

**Fonts**
- `font` — `1` plain, `2` bold, `3` italic, `4` bold-italic, `5` symbol.
- `family` — font family (`"serif"`, `"sans"`, `"mono"`).

**Layout / Margins**
- `mar` — plot margins `c(bottom, left, top, right)` in *text lines*.
- `mai` — margins in *inches*.
- `mfrow`, `mfcol` — multiple figure layout `c(nrow, ncol)`.
- `mfg` — current figure in multi-figure layout.
- `oma`, `omi` — outer margins.

**Axes & Box**
- `las` — label orientation (`0` parallel, `1` horizontal, `2` perpendicular, `3` vertical).
- `bty` — box type (`"o"`, `"l"`, `"7"`, `"c"`, `"u"`, `"]"`, `"n"`).
- `xaxt`, `yaxt` — suppress default axes (`"n"`).

## 2.5 The Plotting Character (`pch`) Reference

| `pch` Value | Symbol | `pch` Value | Symbol |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | Open circle | 14 | Filled square |
| 2 | Open triangle | 15 | Filled small square |
| 3 | Plus sign | 16 | Filled circle |
| 4 | Cross | 17 | Filled triangle |
| 15 | Filled square | 18 | Filled diamond |
| 19 | Filled large circle | 20–25 | Variable (bg-colourable) |

> Single character strings (e.g., `pch = "*"`, `pch = "A"`) are also valid plotting characters.

## 2.6 The Device Model

A **graphics device** is the target destination for plotting output. R maintains a *device stack*; the topmost is the *current device*.

| Function | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| `dev.new()` | Open a new on-screen device |
| `dev.list()` | List all open devices |
| `dev.cur()` | Current device number |
| `dev.set(n)` | Make device `n` current |
| `dev.off(n)` | Close device `n` (or current) |
| `pdf(file)`, `png(file)`, `jpeg(file)` | Open a file-output device |
| `dev.copy(device, file)` | Copy current plot to a file device |
| `dev.copy2pdf(file)` | Quick convenience: copy current plot to PDF |

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula/Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax Pattern | Engineering Analogy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Initialize plot | `plot(x, y, type, main, xlab, ylab, ...)` | Open a new chart canvas |
| Add line | `lines(x, y, col, lty, lwd)` | Overlay trend line |
| Add reference | `abline(h, v, coef, reg)` | Add horizontal/vertical/regression line |
| Add label | `text(x, y, labels, pos, offset)` | Data point annotation |
| Multi-panel | `par(mfrow = c(r, c))` | Subplot grid in MATLAB |
| Customize axis | `axis(side, at, labels, ...)` | Manual axis tick control |
| Save plot | `pdf("file.pdf"); plot(...); dev.off()` | Export to portable document |
| Layout grid | `layout(matrix(c(1,1,2,3), 2, 2))` | Variable-sized subplot mosaic |
| Restore params | `par(oldpar)` | Reset session defaults |
| Color (hex) | `col = "#1F77B4"` | Web-style RGB triplet |
| Color (named) | `col = "steelblue"` | Self-describing hue |
| Sizing factor | `cex = 1.5` | 150% size of base font |
| Line equation | $y = a + b \cdot x$ via `abline(a, b)` | Linear annotation |

## 2.8 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Quality Control**: `boxplot()` is the standard tool for visualizing manufacturing process variability (Six Sigma).
- **Signal Processing**: `plot()` and `lines()` visualize time-series signals; `acf()` and `spectrum()` rely on the same graphics engine.
- **Machine Learning Diagnostics**: `pairs()` produces scatter-plot matrices for feature inspection; residual plots from `lm()` use base graphics.
- **Civil/Structural Engineering**: `image()` and `contour()` render stress/strain fields over 2-D grids from FEM output.
- **Bioinformatics**: `heatmap()` and `volcano()` plots (built on `image()`) display expression matrices.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Code Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The walks below are *runnable* R code blocks, executable in any R console. Each step is annotated with the *function category* (High-Level vs. Low-Level) and the *device state* (new plot vs. existing plot).

## 3.1 Walkthrough 1 — Building a Scatter Plot Incrementally (HL + LL)

**Problem**: Draw a scatter plot of `mpg` vs `wt` from `mtcars`, overlay a regression line, label the three highest `mpg` points, and add a legend.

```r
# Step 1: Prepare data
data(mtcars)
x <- mtcars$wt
y <- mtcars$mpg
```

**Step 2 — High-level plot initialization**

```r
# High-Level: plot() initializes axes, labels, plotting region
plot(x, y,
     main = "Fuel Efficiency vs. Vehicle Weight",
     xlab = "Weight (1000 lbs)",
     ylab = "Miles per Gallon",
     pch  = 19,            # Filled circle
     col  = "steelblue",
     cex  = 1.2)
```

**Step 3 — Low-level: regression line overlay**

```r
# Low-Level: abline() overlays y = a + b*x
fit <- lm(y ~ x)           # Linear regression: y_hat = a + b*x
abline(fit, col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
```

**Step 4 — Low-level: annotate top points**

```r
# Low-Level: text() places string at coordinate
top3 <- order(y, decreasing = TRUE)[1:3]
text(x[top3], y[top3],
     labels = rownames(mtcars)[top3],
     pos = 3,                # Position: above the point
     col = "darkred",
     cex = 0.8)
```

**Step 5 — Low-level: legend**

```r
# Low-Level: legend() adds a legend box
legend("topright",
       legend = c("Observations", "Linear fit"),
       pch    = c(19, NA),
       lty    = c(NA, 2),
       col    = c("steelblue", "red"),
       lwd    = c(NA, 2),
       bty    = "n")
```

**Step 6 — Low-level: marginal rug for density hint**

```r
# Low-Level: rug() adds tick marks on axes
rug(x, side = 1, col = "gray40")
rug(y, side = 2, col = "gray40")
```

**Mathematical underpinning of the line**: The least-squares slope and intercept are:

$$
\hat{\beta}_1 \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}
$$

$$
\hat{\beta}_0 \;=\; \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \, \bar{x}
$$

so the annotated line is

$$
\hat{y} \;=\; \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 \cdot x
$$

which is precisely what `abline(fit)` draws.

## 3.2 Walkthrough 2 — Multi-Panel Layout using `par(mfrow)`

**Problem**: Show histogram, boxplot, and density plot of the same variable in a 2×2 grid, with the fourth cell showing a Q-Q plot.

```r
# Step 1: Save defaults (KTU best practice)
oldpar <- par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1))
```

**Step 2 — Four high-level plots in sequence**

```r
set.seed(42)
v <- rnorm(200, mean = 50, sd = 10)

hist(v, col = "lightblue", main = "Histogram")         # HL
boxplot(v, col = "lightgreen", main = "Boxplot")      # HL
plot(density(v), col = "purple", lwd = 2,
     main = "Density Estimate")                       # HL + LL
qqnorm(v, pch = 19, col = "darkorange"); qqline(v)   # HL + LL
```

**Step 3 — Outer title (LL on outer margin)**

```r
par(oldpar)   # Restore layout
mtext("Diagnostic Panel for Variable v",
      outer = TRUE, cex = 1.4, font = 2)
```

**Layout mathematics**: `mfrow = c(r, c)` causes R to fill the device in *row-major* order, with the figure region being subdivided into a grid of $r \times c$ panels, each panel occupying a fraction $\frac{1}{r} \times \frac{1}{c}$ of the available area, minus margins.

$$
\text{Panel width} \;=\; \frac{\text{Device width} - \text{left margin} - \text{right margin}}{c}
$$

$$
\text{Panel height} \;=\; \frac{\text{Device height} - \text{bottom margin} - \text{top margin}}{r}
$$

## 3.3 Walkthrough 3 — Customizing Axes with `axis()`

**Problem**: Suppress default axes and draw custom ones with logarithmic transformation and rotated labels.

```r
# Data
t <- seq(0, 10, by = 0.1)
y <- exp(-0.3 * t) * sin(2 * t)

# Initialize without axes
plot(t, y, type = "l", col = "navy", lwd = 2,
     xaxt = "n", yaxt = "n",        # suppress defaults
     main = "Damped Oscillation",
     xlab = "Time t (s)", ylab = "Amplitude y(t)")

# Custom x-axis
ticks_x <- seq(0, 10, by = 2.5)
axis(side = 1, at = ticks_x, las = 1, col = "gray40")

# Custom y-axis with log-like labelling
ticks_y <- c(-0.5, 0, 0.5, 1.0)
axis(side = 2, at = ticks_y, las = 2, col = "gray40")

# Add grid (LL)
grid(nx = NULL, ny = NULL, col = "lightgray", lty = 3)
```

**Mathematical model of the curve**:

$$
y(t) \;=\; e^{-0.3 \, t} \cdot \sin(2 \, t)
$$

The envelope decays as

$$
\vert y(t) \vert \;\le\; e^{-0.3 \, t}
$$

so the amplitude is halved at $t^{\star}$ where $e^{-0.3 t^{\star}} = 0.5$, i.e.

$$
t^{\star} \;=\; \frac{\ln 2}{0.3} \;\approx\; 2.31 \text{ s}
$$

which the `grid()`-enhanced plot makes visually verifiable.

## 3.4 Walkthrough 4 — Saving a Plot to a File Device

**Problem**: Generate a barplot, save to PDF, then to PNG.

```r
# Data
counts <- table(mtcars$cyl)
barplot(counts,
        main = "Cylinder Distribution",
        col  = c("tomato", "gold", "skyblue"),
        ylab = "Frequency")

# Method 1: Wrap plot in device
pdf("cyl_dist.pdf", width = 6, height = 4)
barplot(counts, main = "Cylinder Distribution",
        col = c("tomato", "gold", "skyblue"))
dev.off()

# Method 2: Copy active plot (replot is implicit)
png("cyl_dist.png", width = 600, height = 400, res = 100)
dev.copy(png, "cyl_dist_copy.png")
dev.off()
dev.off()
```

**Resolution math**: For a raster (PNG/JPEG) of `width = 6` inches at `res = 100` dpi,

$$
\text{image width in pixels} \;=\; 6 \times 100 \;=\; 600 \text{ px}
$$

$$
\text{image height in pixels} \;=\; 4 \times 100 \;=\; 400 \text{ px}
$$

The total pixel budget is therefore

$$
N_\text{px} \;=\; w \cdot h \cdot \text{res}^2
$$

## 3.5 Walkthrough 5 — Color Specification Mastery

```r
# Named color
plot(1:5, col = "tomato", pch = 19, cex = 3, main = "Named")

# Hex code
plot(1:5, col = "#1B9E77", pch = 19, cex = 3, main = "Hex")

# rgb() function: 0-1 range with alpha
plot(1:5, col = rgb(0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.5), pch = 19, cex = 3, main = "rgb+alpha")

# Color palette (qualitative)
palette(c("red", "blue", "green", "purple"))
plot(1:4, col = 1:4, pch = 19, cex = 3, main = "Palette Index")
```

**RGB-to-hex conversion logic** (for understanding):

$$
\text{hex} \;=\; \text{format}(\text{as.hexmode}(\text{round}(r \times 255)), \text{width} = 2)
\;\Vert\; \ldots
$$

where each channel $r, g, b \in [0, 1]$ is mapped to two hex digits in $[00, \text{FF}]$.

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use What

| Goal | Recommended Function | Type | Code |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Single numeric distribution | `hist()` | HL | `hist(x)` |
| 5-number summary + outliers | `boxplot()` | HL | `boxplot(x)` |
| Categorical proportions | `barplot()` / `pie()` | HL | `barplot(t)` |
| Two numeric variables | `plot(x, y)` | HL | `plot(x, y)` |
| Many numeric variables | `pairs(df)` | HL | `pairs(df)` |
| Normality check | `qqnorm()` + `qqline()` | HL + LL | as shown |
| Add regression line | `abline(lm(y~x))` | LL | `abline(fit)` |
| Add point labels | `text()` | LL | `text(x, y, labels)` |
| Subplot grid | `par(mfrow)` | Param | `par(mfrow=c(2,2))` |
| Variable-sized layout | `layout()` | Param | `layout(matrix(...))` |
| Export to file | `pdf()` / `png()` | Device | open + plot + `dev.off()` |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid: Conceptual Architecture of R Graphics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Issues R Command] --> B[Graphics Engine]
    B --> C{Device Open?}
    C -- No --> D[Open Default Screen Device]
    C -- Yes --> E[Current Device]
    D --> E
    E --> F[High Level Function Called]
    F --> G[Create New Plot Region]
    G --> H[Reset axes labels box]
    H --> I[Add Default Annotations]
    I --> J[Render to Current Device]
    J --> K[Low Level Function Called]
    K --> L[Modify Existing Plot]
    L --> M[Render Delta to Current Device]
    M --> N{More LL Calls?}
    N -- Yes --> L
    N -- No --> O[dev.copy or dev.off]
    O --> P[Output File or Screen Final]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid: High-Level vs. Low-Level Function Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HL[High Level Functions]
        H1[plot]
        H2[hist]
        H3[barplot]
        H4[boxplot]
        H5[pie]
        H6[pairs]
    end
    subgraph LL[Low Level Functions]
        L1[points]
        L2[lines]
        L3[abline]
        L4[text]
        L5[legend]
        L6[axis]
        L7[polygon]
        L8[segments]
        L9[arrows]
    end
    HL --> R[Active Plot Region]
    LL --> R
    R --> D[Graphics Device Screen PDF PNG]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid: Device Stack Management

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    D1 -->|dev.new| D2[Screen Device 2]
```
