# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
Differentiate between **high-level** and **low-level** plotting functions in R. Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Aspect | High-Level Functions | Low-Level Functions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Purpose | Create a *new* complete plot with axes, labels, title | *Annotate* an existing plot |
| State change | Initialize a fresh graphics region | Modify the currently active plot |
| Examples | `plot()`, `hist()`, `barplot()`, `boxplot()`, `pie()` | `points()`, `lines()`, `abline()`, `text()`, `legend()`, `axis()` |
| Dependency | Can be called standalone | Must follow a high-level call (otherwise no plot to modify) |

**(1 mark** for the purpose statement, **1 mark** for the state-change difference, **1 mark** for the two-example lists.)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Supplementary]**
What is the function of `par()` in R graphics? List any four graphical parameters controlled by it.

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
The `par()` function is the *master graphical parameter control* of the base R graphics system. It queries or sets default graphical parameters that affect all subsequent high-level plots until reset. Common parameters:

1. `pch` — plotting character/symbol.
2. `lty` — line type (solid, dashed, dotted, etc.).
3. `col` — plotting color.
4. `mfrow` — multiple-figure row/column layout.
5. `mar` — plot margins.
6. `cex` — character expansion factor.

**(1 mark** for defining `par()`, **1 mark** for naming the category it controls, **1 mark** for listing four valid parameters.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Solutions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4 Compulsory]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the *base R graphics system*, including the role of the graphics device, high-level functions, and low-level functions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write R code to:
1. Create a 2×2 multi-panel layout.
2. In the first panel, draw a histogram of 200 random normal variates with `mean = 10, sd = 3`.
3. In the second, draw a boxplot of the same data.
4. In the third, overlay the density curve on the histogram.
5. In the fourth, draw a Q-Q plot of the same data.
6. Save the final figure to a PDF file named `"diagnostics.pdf"`.
   *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to A(a) — 7 marks

The **base R graphics system** is the default visualization layer in R, implemented by the `graphics` package, included in every R installation.

**1. Graphics Device (2 marks):**
A *device* is the destination for graphical output. R maintains a *stack* of open devices; the *current* device receives all output. Devices can be:
- *Screen devices* (default on most platforms; opened automatically on first plot).
- *File devices*: `pdf()`, `png()`, `jpeg()`, `tiff()`, `bmp()`, `postscript()`.
- Each device has an internal *coordinate system* (user, plot, figure, device-inches).
- `dev.cur()`, `dev.list()`, `dev.set(n)`, `dev.off(n)` manipulate the device stack.

**2. High-Level Functions (2.5 marks):**
High-level functions such as `plot()`, `hist()`, `barplot()`, `boxplot()`, `pie()`, `pairs()`, and `qqnorm()` create a *new* plot region. They:
- Open a fresh plotting region.
- Compute and draw default axes, labels, and main title.
- Provide reasonable defaults for the *type* of plot.
- Are *self-contained*: callable without an existing plot.

**3. Low-Level Functions (2.5 marks):**
Low-level functions such as `points()`, `lines()`, `abline()`, `text()`, `legend()`, `axis()`, `title()`, `polygon()`, `segments()`, and `arrows()` add graphical primitives to an *existing* plot. They:
- Do not reset the current device or region.
- Operate in the user coordinate system established by the most recent high-level call.
- Allow fine-grained customization after the initial plot is created.
- The combined HL+LL pattern is the standard "build-then-decorate" workflow of base R graphics.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b) — 7 marks

```r
# (1) Set up 2x2 layout and store original parameters
oldpar <- par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1))

# (2) Generate data
set.seed(123)
x <- rnorm(200, mean = 10, sd = 3)

# (3) Panel 1: Histogram
hist(x, col = "lightblue", border = "white",
     main = "Histogram of X", xlab = "X")

# (4) Panel 2: Boxplot
boxplot(x, col = "lightgreen",
        main = "Boxplot of X", ylab = "X")

# (5) Panel 3: Histogram with density overlay
hist(x, prob = TRUE, col = "lavender", border = "white",
     main = "Histogram + Density", xlab = "X")
lines(density(x), col = "darkblue", lwd = 2)

# (6) Panel 4: Q-Q plot
qqnorm(x, pch = 19, col = "darkorange", main = "Q-Q Plot")
qqline(x, col = "red", lwd = 2)

# (7) Restore layout
par(oldpar)

# (8) Save to PDF
pdf("diagnostics.pdf", width = 8, height = 8)
par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1))
hist(x, col = "lightblue", main = "Histogram of X")
boxplot(x, col = "lightgreen", main = "Boxplot of X")
hist(x, prob = TRUE, col = "lavender", main = "Histogram + Density")
lines(density(x), col = "darkblue", lwd = 2)
qqnorm(x, pch = 19, col = "darkorange", main = "Q-Q Plot")
qqline(x, col = "red", lwd = 2)
dev.off()
```

**Mark Allocation**:
- (1) `par(mfrow)` correctly used: **1 mark**
- (2) Random data generation: **0.5 mark**
- (3) Histogram with custom color: **0.5 mark**
- (4) Boxplot: **0.5 mark**
- (5) Histogram + density overlay (`lines()`): **1 mark**
- (6) Q-Q plot + `qqline()`: **1 mark**
- (7) Outer title (optional but expected): **0.5 mark**
- (8) PDF device open/close pair: **1 mark**
- Variable naming, indentation, comments: **1 mark**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice Set A]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of `par()` in customizing R graphics. Explain any six graphical parameters with examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write R code to generate the following plot:
- A scatter plot of the `iris` dataset showing `Sepal.Length` (x-axis) vs `Petal.Length` (y-axis).
- Points colored according to the `Species` column.
- A legend in the top-left.
- A blue dashed regression line.
- Title: "Iris: Sepal vs Petal Length".
- Save the plot to a PNG file of size 800×600 pixels. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 marks

The `par()` function is the *central control panel* of base R graphics, used to query or permanently set graphical parameters. Arguments passed to `par()` affect all subsequent plotting operations within the same active device.

**Six Important Parameters (each 1 mark, plus 1 mark for overall explanation):**

1. **`pch` — Plotting character.** Specifies the symbol used for data points.
   - Example: `par(pch = 19)` sets filled circles as default.

2. **`lty` — Line type.** Determines dash pattern of lines.
   - Values: `1` solid, `2` dashed, `3` dotted, `4` dotdash, `5` longdash, `6` twodash.
   - Example: `par(lty = 2)` makes all lines dashed.

3. **`col` — Color.** Sets default color for symbols, lines, axes, and text.
   - Example: `par(col = "steelblue")`.

4. **`mfrow` — Multiple figure layout.** Splits the device into an $r \times c$ grid, filled row-wise.
   - Example: `par(mfrow = c(2, 2))` creates a 2×2 panel grid.

5. **`mar` — Plot margins in text lines.** Order: bottom, left, top, right.
   - Example: `par(mar = c(5, 4, 4, 2) + 0.1)`.

6. **`cex` — Character expansion factor.** Scales all text uniformly.
   - Example: `par(cex = 1.5)` makes all text 50% larger.

7. *(Bonus)* **`bty` — Box type around plot.** Controls whether a box is drawn: `"o"`, `"l"`, `"7"`, `"c"`, `"u"`, `"]"`, or `"n"` (no box).

> **Restoration Pattern** (1 mark for mentioning):
> ```r
> oldpar <- par(mfrow = c(2, 2), pch = 19)
> # ... plotting code ...
> par(oldpar)   # Restore defaults
> ```

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 marks

```r
# (1) Load data
data(iris)

# (2) Define color palette for species
species_levels <- levels(iris$Species)
species_colors <- c("setosa"     = "tomato",
                    "versicolor" = "steelblue",
                    "virginica"  = "darkgreen")

# (3) Initialize scatter plot with first species
plot(iris$Sepal.Length[iris$Species == "setosa"],
     iris$Petal.Length[iris$Species == "setosa"],
     col  = species_colors["setosa"],
     pch  = 19,
     xlab = "Sepal Length (cm)",
     ylab = "Petal Length (cm)",
     main = "Iris: Sepal vs Petal Length",
     xlim = range(iris$Sepal.Length),
     ylim = range(iris$Petal.Length))

# (4) Add remaining species points (low-level)
points(iris$Sepal.Length[iris$Species == "versicolor"],
       iris$Petal.Length[iris$Species == "versicolor"],
       col = species_colors["versicolor"], pch = 19)

points(iris$Sepal.Length[iris$Species == "virginica"],
       iris$Petal.Length[iris$Species == "virginica"],
       col = species_colors["virginica"], pch = 19)

# (5) Add blue dashed regression line (low-level)
fit <- lm(Petal.Length ~ Sepal.Length, data = iris)
abline(fit, col = "blue", lwd = 2, lty = 2)

# (6) Add legend
legend("topleft",
       legend = species_levels,
       col    = species_colors,
       pch    = 19,
       title  = "Species",
       bty    = "n")

# (7) Save to PNG
png("iris_plot.png", width = 800, height = 600, res = 100)
# Re-issue plot commands inside device:
plot(iris$Sepal.Length[iris$Species == "setosa"],
     iris$Petal.Length[iris$Species == "setosa"],
     col  = "tomato", pch = 19,
     xlab = "Sepal Length (cm)", ylab = "Petal Length (cm)",
     main = "Iris: Sepal vs Petal Length",
     xlim = range(iris$Sepal.Length),
     ylim = range(iris$Petal.Length))
points(iris$Sepal.Length[iris$Species == "versicolor"],
       iris$Petal.Length[iris$Species == "versicolor"],
       col = "steelblue", pch = 19)
points(iris$Sepal.Length[iris$Species == "virginica"],
       iris$Petal.Length[iris$Species == "virginica"],
       col = "darkgreen", pch = 19)
abline(fit, col = "blue", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
legend("topleft", legend = species_levels,
       col = species_colors, pch = 19, title = "Species", bty = "n")
dev.off()
```

**Mark Allocation for B(b):**
- Color-coding by species: **1.5 marks**
- `points()` low-level reuse: **1 mark**
- `abline()` for regression: **1.5 marks**
- `legend()` with title: **1.5 marks**
- `png()` device + `dev.off()` pair with correct resolution math: **1.5 marks**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (seen in 2023–2024 answer scripts):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `dev.off()` after opening a file device** → The PDF/PNG file remains locked and is never flushed. Loss: full 1.5 marks on the file-output sub-part.
> 2. **Calling low-level functions *before* a high-level function** → `lines()`, `points()`, `text()` produce an error because no plot region exists. Always verify a high-level call comes first.
> 3. **Confusing `mfrow` with `mfcol`** → `mfrow` fills row-wise (left-to-right, then top-to-bottom); `mfcol` fills column-wise (top-to-bottom, then left-to-right). Examiners test this distinction.
> 4. **Modifying `par()` without restoring via `par(oldpar)`** → Subsequent plots inherit the modified layout. *Always* save and restore.
> 5. **Writing `col = "redd"` or `"RGB(255,0,0)"` instead of `"red"` or `"#FF0000"`** → R throws an error. Valid color names are case-insensitive but must be exact.
> 6. **Confusing `pch = 16` (filled circle) with `pch = 19` (large filled circle)** — both are filled circles but with different visual sizes. KTU questions often specify *one* value.
> 7. **Forgetting `xlim`/`ylim` when overlaying groups** → The second species' points are clipped because the first species' plot has too-narrow axes. Always pre-set `xlim` and `ylim` to the global range.
> 8. **Using `type = "b"` (both) when `type = "o"` (overplotted) is intended** — KTU will mark distinction between `"p"`, `"l"`, `"b"`, `"o"`, `"h"`, `"s"`, `"n"`.
> 9. **Misnaming `boxplot` argument as `xlab` in `plot()` but `varwidth = TRUE` in `boxplot()`** — pay attention to which function accepts which argument.
> 10. **Not specifying `lty = 2` for the dashed line** — leaving it at the default solid line forfeits descriptive-accuracy marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print and keep before the exam)**

- [ ] R graphics is **device-based**; the *current device* receives all output.
- [ ] **High-level** functions: `plot`, `hist`, `barplot`, `boxplot`, `pie`, `pairs`, `qqnorm`, `coplot`, `image`, `contour`, `stripchart`, `mosaicplot`.
- [ ] **Low-level** functions: `points`, `lines`, `abline`, `text`, `mtext`, `legend`, `title`, `axis`, `polygon`, `rect`, `segments`, `arrows`, `symbols`, `rug`, `grid`.
- [ ] **`par()`** controls: `pch`, `lty`, `lwd`, `col`, `bg`, `fg`, `cex`, `font`, `family`, `mar`, `mai`, `mfrow`, `mfcol`, `las`, `bty`, `xaxt`, `yaxt`.
- [ ] **`plot()` `type` argument**: `"p"` points (default), `"l"` lines, `"b"` both, `"o"` overplotted, `"h"` histogram-like, `"s"` stair, `"n"` no plotting.
- [ ] **`abline(a, b)`** draws $y = a + b x$; **`abline(v = ...)`** is vertical; **`abline(h = ...)`** is horizontal; **`abline(lm(...))`** is the regression line.
- [ ] **Multi-panel**: `par(mfrow = c(r, c))` for equal-sized grid; `layout(matrix(...))` for variable-sized.
- [ ] **Save plot**: open device (`pdf()`, `png()`, `jpeg()`), plot, then **always** call `dev.off()`.
- [ ] **Color**: named (`"red"`), hex (`"#1F77B4"`), or `rgb(r, g, b, alpha)` with channels in $[0, 1]$.
- [ ] **Resolution math**: `pixels = inches × dpi`; for `res = 100` and `8 in × 6 in` you get an 800×600 image.
- [ ] **Best practice**: `oldpar <- par(...)` → plot → `par(oldpar)` to prevent parameter leakage.
- [ ] **`bty`** values: `"o"`, `"l"`, `"7"`, `"c"`, `"u"`, `"]"`, `"n"`.
- [ ] **`las`** values: `0` parallel, `1` horizontal, `2` perpendicular, `3` vertical.
- [ ] **Plotting character (`pch`)**: 0–25 are pre-defined glyphs; any single character is also valid (`pch = "A"`).
- [ ] **`legend()`** requires `legend`, `col`, `pch`/`lty`/`lwd` (matching the plot), and optionally `title`, `bty`, `bg`.
- [ ] **`qqnorm()` + `qqline()`** is the canonical normality check in base R.
- [ ] **`density()` returns an object** to be plotted with `plot()` (HL) or `lines()` (LL overlay).
- [ ] **Restoring `par()`** is a KTU-grademark item: forgetting it in a multi-part question is a common loss.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Customizing Charts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT631 - R-PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Customizing Charts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Customizing Charts** in R programming refers to the systematic modification of default graphical output parameters to enhance the **visual aesthetics, interpretability, and communicative efficacy** of data visualizations. In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, customization encompasses the modification of plot elements including *titles, axis labels, axis limits, colors, line types, point symbols, legends, annotations, fonts, margins, and multi-panel layouts* using both the traditional **base R graphics system** (`graphics` package) and the modern **Grammar of Graphics** framework (`ggplot2` package).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Customization is the bridge between *raw data plotting* and *publication-grade visualization*. The `par()` function in base R and the `theme()` / `labs()` / `scale_*()` functions in `ggplot2` are the **two primary customization engines** expected to be mastered.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Café Menu Board"

Imagine a chef who prepares excellent dishes (the data) but presents them on a plain white plate with no garnish. Now imagine a chef who adds a sprinkle of parsley, a colorful sauce drizzle, and arranges items aesthetically — the dish *tastes* the same but *communicates* the chef's intent far better.

| Element | Café Analogy | R Chart Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| The dish itself | Cooked food | Default `plot()` output |
| Plate choice | Color/shape of plate | `bg`, `pch`, plot region color |
| Garnish & sauce | Visual decoration | `col`, `lwd`, `lty` |
| Menu card description | Written explanation | `main`, `sub`, `xlab`, `ylab` |
| Allergen legend | Footnote details | `legend()` call |
| Multiple dishes on a tray | Combo meal | `par(mfrow)`, `layout()` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** Customization does *not* change the underlying data — it only changes *how the data is rendered*. This is analogous to changing the font of a document without changing its words.

## 1.3 The Two Customization Paradigms in R

1. **Base R Graphics (`graphics` package)** — *Imperative* style. You issue commands that directly modify the active device.
2. **ggplot2 (`ggplot2` package)** — *Declarative* style. You describe *what* the final plot should look like using layered grammar.

**Both paradigms are examinable** in the KTU 2024 ESE pattern.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The `par()` Function — The Master Controller

The `par()` function in base R sets or queries **graphical parameters** globally for the active plotting device. Every parameter set via `par()` persists for the rest of the session unless reset.

### Syntax

```r
par(parameter = value)
old_settings <- par()              # save current state
par(new_setting)                    # apply new setting
# ... your plotting code ...
par(old_settings)                   # restore (good practice)
```

## 2.2 Hierarchy of Chart Customization

Customization is applied in this layered order (outer to inner):

1. **Device-level** → `pdf()`, `png()`, `jpeg()` (controls file output, size, resolution).
2. **Global graphical parameters** → `par()` (affects all subsequent plots).
3. **Plot-level arguments** → arguments inside `plot()`, `barplot()`, `hist()`, etc.
4. **Annotation-level** → `text()`, `mtext()`, `legend()`, `abline()`, `points()` added *on top* of the plot.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** KTU examiners often test whether students know that *higher-priority* settings override *lower-priority* ones. Argument-level > par() level for the same parameter.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Graphical Parameters

| Parameter | Purpose | Common Values | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `col` | Color of points/lines/text | Named, hex, `rgb()` | `col = "red"`, `col = "#FF5733"` |
| `pch` | Plotting character (point symbol) | **0–25** integer codes | `pch = 16` (filled circle) |
| `lty` | Line type | **0=blank, 1=solid, 2=dashed, 3=dotted, 4=dotdash, 5=longdash, 6=twodash** | `lty = 2` |
| `lwd` | Line width (multiplier) | Numeric ≥ 1 | `lwd = 2` |
| `cex` | Character/text expansion | Numeric; default 1 | `cex = 1.5` |
| `main` | Main title | Character string | `main = "Sales 2024"` |
| `xlab` | X-axis label | Character string | `xlab = "Month"` |
| `ylab` | Y-axis label | Character string | `ylab = "Revenue"` |
| `xlim` | X-axis limits | Numeric vector of 2 | `xlim = c(0, 100)` |
| `ylim` | Y-axis limits | Numeric vector of 2 | `ylim = c(-5, 5)` |
| `bg` | Background color | Color spec | `bg = "lightyellow"` |
| `las` | Label orientation | **0=parallel, 1=horizontal, 2=perpendicular, 3=vertical** | `las = 1` |
| `mfrow` | Multiple plots by row | `c(nrow, ncol)` | `par(mfrow = c(2,2))` |
| `mfcol` | Multiple plots by column | `c(nrow, ncol)` | `par(mfcol = c(2,2))` |
| `mar` | Plot margins (in lines) | Numeric vector of 4 | `mar = c(5,4,4,2)` |
| `oma` | Outer margins | Numeric vector of 4 | `oma = c(0,0,2,0)` |
| `family` | Font family | `"serif"`, `"sans"`, `"mono"` | `family = "serif"` |
| `font` | Font style | **1=plain, 2=bold, 3=italic, 4=bold italic** | `font = 2` |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** When writing R color values like `"red"`, `"blue"`, or hex codes like `"#FF0000"`, always enclose them in **double quotes** — they are character strings, not R symbols. Forgetting quotes is a common KTU valuation error.

## 2.4 The `legend()` Function — Signature Reference

```r
legend(x, y,                 # position: "topright", coordinates, or "bottomleft"
       legend,               # vector of text labels
       col,                  # vector of colors
       pch,                  # point symbols (numeric vector)
       lty,                  # line types (numeric vector)
       lwd,                  # line widths
       cex,                  # text size
       bty,                  # "o"=box, "n"=no box
       title,                # legend title
       fill)                 # fill colors for boxes
```

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

Customization is not merely aesthetic — it is **functional engineering communication**:

- **Color-coding** categorical data in **medical imaging** (tumor vs. healthy tissue).
- **Log-scale axes** in **signal processing** plots (frequency response).
- **Multi-panel layouts** in **comparative analytics dashboards**.
- **Annotated thresholds** in **control systems** (warning limits on temperature plots).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Customization Walkthroughs (Analytical Matrix)

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Customizing a Scatter Plot (Base R)

**Task:** Plot `mtcars$wt` vs `mtcars$mpg` with full customization.

```r
# Step 1: Save default par settings (best practice)
oldpar <- par(no.readonly = TRUE)

# Step 2: Set global graphical parameters
par(
  bg       = "lightgray",         # light gray background
  fg       = "darkblue",          # foreground color
  las      = 1,                   # horizontal axis labels
  mar      = c(5, 5, 4, 2),       # generous margins for long labels
  cex.axis = 0.9                  # slightly smaller axis numbers
)

# Step 3: Create the customized plot
plot(
  x       = mtcars$wt,
  y       = mtcars$mpg,
  main    = "Vehicle Weight vs. Fuel Efficiency",
  sub     = "Source: mtcars dataset (R base)",
  xlab    = "Weight (1000 lbs)",
  ylab    = "Miles per Gallon",
  xlim    = c(1.5, 5.5),
  ylim    = c(10, 35),
  pch     = 16,                   # filled circles
  col     = "steelblue",
  cex     = 1.3,                  # larger points
  lwd     = 2                     # point border thickness
)

# Step 4: Overlay a regression line in red
abline(
  lm(mpg ~ wt, data = mtcars),
  col     = "red",
  lty     = 2,                    # dashed line
  lwd     = 2
)

# Step 5: Add a legend
legend(
  "topright",
  legend  = c("Data points", "Linear fit"),
  col     = c("steelblue", "red"),
  pch     = c(16, NA),
  lty     = c(NA, 2),
  lwd     = c(NA, 2),
  bty     = "n",
  cex     = 0.9
)

# Step 6: Add a text annotation
mtext(
  text   = "Lighter cars → better mileage",
  side   = 3,                     # top
  line   = 0.5,
  cex    = 0.85,
  col    = "darkred"
)

# Step 7: Restore defaults (always do this in scripts)
par(oldpar)
```

### Logical Deduction of Each Line

| Line / Argument | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| `bg = "lightgray"` | Reduces eye strain; standard in dashboards |
| `las = 1` | Horizontal labels are easier to read for long names |
| `mar = c(5,5,4,2)` | Order: *bottom, left, top, right* — extra space for title |
| `pch = 16` | **Filled circle** is the most visible symbol; pch 0-14 are outlines |
| `xlim`, `ylim` | Manual limits prevent auto-scaling from misleading the viewer |
| `abline()` overlay | Adds analytical value — viewer sees the *trend*, not just points |
| `legend()` | Critical for dual-symbol plots (points + line) |
| `mtext()` | Margin text is non-intrusive annotation |

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Multi-Panel Layout with `par(mfrow)`

**Task:** Place 4 plots in a 2×2 grid.

```r
# Setting up 2x2 grid
par(mfrow = c(2, 2),             # 2 rows, 2 columns
    mar   = c(4, 4, 3, 1),       # uniform margins
    bg    = "white")

# Panel 1: Histogram
hist(mtcars$mpg,
     col    = "skyblue",
     main   = "Histogram of MPG",
     xlab   = "Miles per Gallon",
     border = "white")

# Panel 2: Boxplot
boxplot(mtcars$hp,
        col  = "lightcoral",
        main = "Boxplot of Horsepower",
        ylab = "HP")

# Panel 3: Scatter
plot(mtcars$wt, mtcars$mpg,
     pch  = 17,                   # filled triangle
     col  = "darkgreen",
     main = "WT vs MPG",
     xlab = "Weight", ylab = "MPG")

# Panel 4: Barplot
barplot(table(mtcars$cyl),
        col   = c("gold", "silver", "brown"),
        main  = "Cylinder Count",
        xlab  = "Cylinders", ylab = "Frequency")
```

**Conceptual Flow:**

$$
\text{par(mfrow)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Fills row-by-row} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Plots placed in order: top-left, top-right, bottom-left, bottom-right}
$$

$$
\text{par(mfcol)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Fills column-by-column} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Plots placed in order: top-left, bottom-left, top-right, bottom-right}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** `mfrow` = "**row**-wise fill" → *read the W as W across rows*; `mfcol` = "**col**umn-wise fill".

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — ggplot2 Customization

**Task:** Reproduce the scatter plot using `ggplot2` grammar.

```r
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(data = mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 3, shape = 16) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_color_manual(
    name   = "Cylinders",
    values = c("4" = "steelblue", "6" = "darkgreen", "8" = "firebrick")
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(1.5, 5.5), name = "Weight (1000 lbs)") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(10, 35),  name = "Miles per Gallon") +
  labs(
    title    = "Vehicle Weight vs. Fuel Efficiency",
    subtitle = "Grouped by Number of Cylinders",
    caption  = "Source: mtcars dataset"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", color = "navy"),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
```

### Layer-wise Logic

| ggplot2 Layer | Function | Base R Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| `ggplot()` | Initializes the canvas with data & aesthetic mappings | `plot()` empty call |
| `geom_point()` | Adds the scatter geometry | `points()` after `plot()` |
| `geom_smooth()` | Adds the trend line with confidence band | `abline()` after `lm()` |
| `scale_color_manual()` | Maps discrete colors to factor levels | `col` vector in `plot()` |
| `scale_x/y_continuous()` | Controls continuous axis limits & labels | `xlim`, `ylim`, `xlab`, `ylab` |
| `labs()` | Sets title, subtitle, caption | `main`, `sub` |
| `theme_minimal()` | Applies a built-in visual theme | `par()` global styling |
| `theme()` | Fine-tunes individual theme elements | `par()` plus `mtext()` |

## 3.4 The Mathematical Construction of Color in R

R accepts three forms of color specification:

$$
\text{Color Specification} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Named color} & \rightarrow \texttt{"red", "steelblue", "darkgreen"} \\
\text{Hex code (RGB)} & \rightarrow \texttt{"\#FF5733"} = (255, 87, 51)_{10} \\
\text{rgb() function} & \rightarrow \texttt{rgb(1, 0.34, 0.20)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Hex → Decimal Conversion for `"#FF5733"`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{"FF"} &= 15 \times 16^1 + 15 \times 16^0 = 240 + 15 = 255 \\
\texttt{"57"} &= 5 \times 16^1 + 7 \times 16^0 = 80 + 7 = 87 \\
\texttt{"33"} &= 3 \times 16^1 + 3 \times 16^0 = 48 + 3 = 51
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, `"#FF5733"` corresponds to RGB (255, 87, 51), a vibrant orange-red. This is **exactly** the color *Pantone 1655 C* in design systems.

## 3.5 Quick-Reference Matrix — Customization Element → Method

| Customization Element | Base R Method | ggplot2 Method |
|---|---|---|
| Title | `main = "..."` | `labs(title = "...")` |
| Subtitle | `sub = "..."` | `labs(subtitle = "...")` |
| X-label | `xlab = "..."` | `xlab("...")` or `labs(x = "...")` |
| Y-label | `ylab = "..."` | `ylab("...")` or `labs(y = "...")` |
| X-limits | `xlim = c(a, b)` | `xlim(a, b)` or `scale_x_continuous(limits)` |
| Y-limits | `ylim = c(a, b)` | `ylim(a, b)` or `scale_y_continuous(limits)` |
| Color | `col = "red"` | `color = "red"` inside `aes()` |
| Point size | `cex = 1.5` | `size = 3` in `geom_point()` |
| Point shape | `pch = 16` | `shape = 16` in `geom_point()` |
| Line type | `lty = 2` | `linetype = "dashed"` |
| Line width | `lwd = 2` | `linewidth = 1` (ggplot2 ≥ 3.4) |
| Legend | `legend(...)` | `scale_color_manual()` + `labs(color)` |
| Background | `par(bg = ...)` | `theme(plot.background = ...)` |
| Grid | `par(...)` + `grid()` | `theme(panel.grid = ...)` |
| Multi-panel | `par(mfrow)`, `layout()` | `facet_wrap()`, `facet_grid()` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Explain the role of the `par()` function in R with any **four** commonly used graphical parameters.

**Model Answer:**

The `par()` function in R is the **primary mechanism** for setting global graphical parameters that control the appearance of plots. It allows the user to modify the default rendering behavior of base R graphics. Four commonly used parameters are:

1. **`col`** → Specifies color for points, lines, text, and borders. Accepts named colors (`"red"`), hex codes (`"#FF5733"`), or `rgb()` function calls.
2. **`pch`** → Plotting Character. Integer codes **0–25** select the symbol used to represent data points (e.g., `pch = 16` is a filled circle).
3. **`lty`** → Line type. Integer codes **0 (blank), 1 (solid), 2 (dashed), 3 (dotted), 4 (dotdash), 5 (longdash), 6 (twodash)**.
4. **`mfrow`** → Splits the plotting device into a matrix of panels filled **row-wise**, e.g., `par(mfrow = c(2,2))` creates a 2×2 grid.

Example call:
```r
par(col = "blue", pch = 16, lty = 2, mfrow = c(2, 2))
```

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Differentiate between `mfrow` and `mfcol` in R's `par()` function. Which one fills the plot region *row-wise*?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `par(mfrow)` | `par(mfcol)` |
|---|---|---|
| Fill direction | **Row-wise** (left→right, then next row) | **Column-wise** (top→bottom, then next column) |
| Order of placement | Panel 1: top-left; Panel 2: top-right; Panel 3: bottom-left | Panel 1: top-left; Panel 2: bottom-left; Panel 3: top-right |
| Syntax | `par(mfrow = c(2, 2))` | `par(mfcol = c(2, 2))` |

`par(mfrow = c(2, 2))` fills the plot region **row-wise**. This is the most commonly used in KTU examinations.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** Consider the built-in dataset `mtcars`. Write an R script to perform the following tasks:

**(a) [7 Marks]** Create a customized scatter plot of `wt` (x-axis) vs `mpg` (y-axis) with:
- Title: *"Weight vs Fuel Efficiency"*
- X-label: *"Weight (1000 lbs)"* and Y-label: *"Miles per Gallon"*
- Filled red circles (`pch = 16`, `col = "red"`) with point size `cex = 1.5`
- X-axis limits from 1.5 to 5.5; Y-axis limits from 10 to 35
- A light yellow plot background

**(b) [7 Marks]** On the same plot, overlay:
- A linear regression line in dashed blue (`lty = 2`, `col = "blue"`, `lwd = 2`)
- A legend at the top-right corner identifying the data points and the regression line
- A margin text at the bottom reading *"Trend: negative correlation"*

**Model Solution:**

```r
# Part (a) - Customized Scatter Plot
oldpar <- par(no.readonly = TRUE)     # save state

# Set background
par(bg = "lightyellow")

# Base scatter plot
plot(
  x    = mtcars$wt,
  y    = mtcars$mpg,
  main = "Weight vs Fuel Efficiency",
  xlab = "Weight (1000 lbs)",
  ylab = "Miles per Gallon",
  xlim = c(1.5, 5.5),
  ylim = c(10, 35),
  pch  = 16,
  col  = "red",
  cex  = 1.5
)

# Part (b) - Overlay regression, legend, annotation
# Linear regression line
abline(
  lm(mpg ~ wt, data = mtcars),
  col = "blue",
  lty = 2,
  lwd = 2
)

# Legend at top-right
legend(
  "topright",
  legend = c("Data points", "Linear fit"),
  col    = c("red", "blue"),
  pch    = c(16, NA),
  lty    = c(NA, 2),
  lwd    = c(NA, 2),
  bty    = "n",
  cex    = 0.9
)

# Margin text at bottom
mtext(
  text = "Trend: negative correlation",
  side = 1,            # 1 = bottom
  line = 3,
  cex  = 0.9,
  col  = "darkblue"
)

par(oldpar)           # restore defaults
```

**Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
- Correct `par()` setup → 1 mark
- Correct `plot()` arguments → 3 marks
- `abline()` overlay with correct style → 2 marks
- `legend()` with proper arguments → 2 marks
- `mtext()` with correct side/line → 2 marks

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]** Using the `ggplot2` package, reproduce the following customization tasks on the `iris` dataset:

**(a) [7 Marks]** Create a scatter plot of `Sepal.Length` (x) vs `Petal.Length` (y), with points colored by `Species`. Apply:
- A `theme_minimal()` base theme
- Title: *"Iris Morphology Comparison"* in bold
- X-label: *"Sepal Length (cm)"* and Y-label: *"Petal Length (cm)"*
- Manual color scale: `setosa = "orange"`, `versicolor = "purple"`, `virginica = "darkgreen"`

**(b) [7 Marks]** Add:
- A linear regression line (per species, no confidence band)
- Legend at the bottom titled *"Species"*
- A plot caption: *"Source: iris dataset"*

**Model Solution:**

```r
library(ggplot2)

# Part (a) - Base ggplot2 plot with customization
p <- ggplot(
  data = iris,
  aes(x = Sepal.Length, y = Petal.Length, color = Species)
) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  scale_color_manual(
    name = "Species",
    values = c(
      "setosa"     = "orange",
      "versicolor" = "purple",
      "virginica"  = "darkgreen"
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Iris Morphology Comparison",
    x     = "Sepal Length (cm)",
    y     = "Petal Length (cm)",
    caption = "Source: iris dataset"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    plot.caption     = element_text(face = "italic", color = "gray40")
  )

# Part (b) - Add regression line without confidence band
p + geom_smooth(
  method = "lm",
  se     = FALSE,
  linetype = "dashed"
)
```

**Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
- Correct `ggplot()` initialization with `aes()` mapping → 2 marks
- `geom_point()` with proper aesthetic → 1 mark
- `scale_color_manual()` with all three mappings → 2 marks
- `labs()` and `theme_minimal()` + `theme()` → 2 marks
- `geom_smooth()` with `se = FALSE` → 2 marks

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting quotes around color names:** `col = red` (ERROR) vs `col = "red"` (CORRECT). R treats unquoted `red` as a variable lookup.
> 2. **Mixing up `mfrow` and `mfcol`:** If asked *"draw 4 plots in a 2×2 grid row-wise"*, the answer is `par(mfrow = c(2,2))`, NOT `mfcol`.
> 3. **Forgetting `par(oldpar)` restoration:** In multi-plot scripts, failing to restore settings bleeds into subsequent plots — examiners deduct for this.
> 4. **Confusing `pch` and `shape`:** `pch` is for base R; `shape` is for ggplot2. They are NOT interchangeable in code.
> 5. **Wrong `side` argument in `mtext()`:** `side = 1` is BOTTOM, `side = 2` is LEFT, `side = 3` is TOP, `side = 4` is RIGHT. Many students guess.
> 6. **Hex code formatting:** Use uppercase or lowercase consistently. `"#FF5733"` and `"#ff5733"` are identical to R, but KTU expects *no trailing spaces* inside the quotes.
> 7. **In ggplot2, putting `color` outside `aes()`:** Inside `aes()` → maps to data; outside `aes()` → applies to ALL points statically.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — The 12-Point Customization Mantra:**
>
> - [ ] **1.** `par()` sets **global** graphical parameters; arguments inside `plot()` set **plot-specific** ones.
> - [ ] **2.** `pch` codes: **0–14** are open/outline shapes; **15–20** are solid; **21–25** are fillable with border.
> - [ ] **3.** `lty` codes: **1=solid, 2=dashed, 3=dotted, 4=dotdash, 5=longdash, 6=twodash**.
> - [ ] **4.** Colors accept **named strings, hex codes, or `rgb()` calls** — all in double quotes (as strings).
> - [ ] **5.** `mfrow` fills **row-wise**; `mfcol` fills **column-wise**.
> - [ ] **6.** `mar` order is `c(bottom, left, top, right)`; default is `c(5, 4, 4, 2)`.
> - [ ] **7.** `cex` is a **multiplier** for text/symbol size; `cex = 1.5` means 1.5× default.
> - [ ] **8.** `legend()` requires matching lengths for `legend`, `col`, `pch`, `lty` arguments.
> - [ ] **9.** `mtext(side = X)` where **1=bottom, 2=left, 3=top, 4=right**.
> - [ ] **10.** ggplot2 layers are added with `+`; aesthetics inside `aes()` map to data, outside are constants.
> - [ ] **11.** ggplot2 customization: **`labs()` for labels, `scale_*()` for axes/colors, `theme()` for non-data elements**.
> - [ ] **12.** Always **save and restore `par()`** in scripts using `oldpar <- par(no.readonly = TRUE)`.

**Engineering Design Principle:** A well-customized chart tells a **complete visual story** in *one glance* — the title answers *what*, the axes answer *how much*, the colors answer *which category*, and the legend anchors the *meaning* of every visual encoding.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Graphical Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT631 - R-PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Graphical Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **R programming**, **Graphical Parameters** are a comprehensive set of configurable attributes that govern the visual appearance, layout, dimensions, colors, text properties, and structural composition of graphical output. They are primarily manipulated using the high-level function **`par()`** (short for *parameters*), which retrieves and sets graphical defaults for the current device. Every plotting call in base R (e.g., `plot()`, `hist()`, `boxplot()`) ultimately respects these parameters unless explicitly overridden within the function call.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme R-Programming syllabus (Module 4)**, graphical parameters form the backbone of *custom data visualization* and are classified into multiple functional families: text-scaling parameters, color parameters, font parameters, line parameters, symbol parameters, margin parameters, and layout/figure-splitting parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The function `par()` is a *list*-returning function. Calling `par()` with no arguments returns the **current graphical parameter settings**. Calling `par(name = value)` sets parameters and invisibly returns the *previous* settings — a critical property used in KTU examination code snippets for "saving and restoring defaults".

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **professional photographer's studio**. The photographer does not just click the shutter; they control:
- **Aperture & ISO** (exposure → analogous to `cex` for character expansion)
- **Film grain & resolution** (sharpness → analogous to `lwd`, `lty` for line types)
- **Filters & color grading** (mood → analogous to `col`, `col.axis` for color)
- **Picture frame & matting** (composition → analogous to `mar`, `oma` for margins)
- **Picture arrangement on a wall** (layout → analogous to `mfrow`, `mfcol` for split screens)

In the same way, a programmer does not just plot data — they **fine-tune the visual aesthetics** using graphical parameters to produce publication-grade graphics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are Graphical Parameters Important in KTU Exams?**
> - Frequently tested as **3-mark direct questions** ("List the categories of graphical parameters").
> - Form the **foundation of 14-mark descriptive questions** on multi-panel plotting, custom legends, and margin control.
> - Common in **Part B coding questions** where students must write R code using `par()` to generate specific outputs.

### 1.3 Key Categories — At a Glance

The KTU syllabus broadly categorizes graphical parameters into **8 functional families**:

1. **Text Size Parameters** — `cex`, `cex.axis`, `cex.lab`, `cex.main`, `cex.sub`
2. **Color Parameters** — `col`, `col.axis`, `col.lab`, `col.main`, `col.sub`, `fg`, `bg`
3. **Font Parameters** — `font`, `font.axis`, `font.lab`, `font.main`, `font.sub`, `family`, `ps`
4. **Symbol & Point Parameters** — `pch`, `cex` (symbol size)
5. **Line Parameters** — `lty`, `lwd`, `lend`, `ljoin`, `lmitre`
6. **Axis & Tick Parameters** — `lab`, `las`, `tck`, `tcl`, `xaxp`, `yaxp`, `xaxt`, `yaxt`
7. **Margin Parameters** — `mai`, `mar`, `mex`, `mar`, `oma`, `omd`, `omi`
8. **Layout & Splitting Parameters** — `mfrow`, `mfcol`, `mfg`, `fig`, `fin`, `pin`, `plt`, `layout()`

> [!TIP]
> The **default numeric value of nearly every `cex.*` parameter is `1`**, meaning "no scaling". Values >1 enlarge, values <1 shrink. This is a frequently tested KTU fact.

---

### 1.4 Physical Metrics & Standard Defaults

| Parameter Family | Default Value | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| `mar` (margins in inches) | `c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1)` | Standard A4 page margins |
| `oma` (outer margins) | `c(0, 0, 0, 0)` | Bleed area on print |
| `pch` (plotting character) | `1` (open circle) | Calibrated marker |
| `lty` (line type) | `1` (solid) | Solid trace on oscilloscope |
| `lwd` (line width) | `1` | Standard trace width |
| `font` | `1` (plain) | Default typeface |
| `mfrow` | `c(1, 1)` | Single-frame display |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Walkthrough — The `par()` Function

The `par()` function has a dual nature that students must master:

```r
# Mode 1: Get current parameters
old_par <- par()              # Returns a NAMED LIST of all active settings

# Mode 2: Set parameters
par(col = "red", pch = 17)    # Sets color to red and symbol to filled triangle

# Mode 3: Multi-line setting
par(
  mfrow = c(2, 2),            # 2 rows x 2 columns of plots
  mar   = c(2, 2, 2, 2),      # Tight inner margins
  cex.main = 1.5              # Enlarged main title
)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why save old parameters?** Restoring defaults after a plot is mandatory in *publication-quality* R scripts and is a **valuation keyword** in KTU 14-mark answers. Pattern:
>
> ```r
> oldpar <- par(mfrow = c(2, 2))   # Save
> plot(1:10); plot(2:20); plot(3:30); plot(4:40)
> par(oldpar)                      # Restore — examiners love this
> ```

---

### 2.2 Family 1: Text Size Parameters (`cex.*`)

The `cex` parameter (Character EXpansion) is a **multiplicative scaling factor**.

| Parameter | Scope | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `cex` | Overall text in plot | 1 |
| `cex.axis` | Axis annotation numbers | 0.83 |
| `cex.lab` | X & Y axis labels | 1 |
| `cex.main` | Main title | 1.2 |
| `cex.sub` | Subtitle | 1 |

**Underlying Logic:**
$$\text{Effective font size} = \text{Base point size} \times cex \times cex.\text{scope}$$

For example, axis text size:
$$\text{axis\_text\_size} = ps \times cex \times cex.axis$$

---

### 2.3 Family 2: Color Parameters

Colors in R can be specified as:
- **Named colors:** `"red"`, `"blue"`, `"darkgreen"`
- **Hexadecimal:** `"#FF5733"`
- **Integer index:** `1` (black), `2` (red), `3` (green), `4` (blue), `5` (cyan), `6` (magenta), `7` (yellow)
- **Function-generated:** `rgb(0.5, 0.2, 0.8)`, `rainbow(n)`

| Parameter | Scope |
|---|---|
| `col` | Plotting color (points, lines) |
| `col.axis` | Axis tick labels |
| `col.lab` | Axis labels |
| `col.main` | Main title |
| `col.sub` | Subtitle |
| `fg` | Foreground (axes, box) |
| `bg` | Background (also symbol fill for `pch=21:25`) |

> [!NOTE]
> The function `colors()` in R returns a vector of **657 named colors**. KTU examiners often ask for a 1-line answer: "How many named colors does R support?"

---

### 2.4 Family 3: Font & Typeface Parameters

| Parameter | Allowed Values |
|---|---|
| `font` | 1=plain, 2=**bold**, 3=*italic*, 4=***bold italic***, 5=Cyrillic (Cyrillic) |
| `font.axis` | Same as `font`, applied to axis text |
| `font.lab` | Applied to axis labels |
| `font.main` | Applied to main title |
| `font.sub` | Applied to subtitle |
| `family` | Font family name, e.g., `"serif"`, `"sans"`, `"mono"`, `"Helvetica"` |
| `ps` | Point size of text (1/72 inch) — default `12` |

---

### 2.5 Family 4: Symbol Parameters (`pch`)

The `pch` (Plotting CHaracter) parameter accepts integers **1–25** for symbols, integers **32–127** for ASCII characters, and integer `NA` for no symbol.

| pch | Symbol | pch | Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ○ Open circle | 16 | ● Filled circle |
| 2 | △ Open triangle | 17 | ▲ Filled triangle |
| 3 | + Plus | 18 | ◆ Filled diamond |
| 4 | × Cross | 19 | ● Solid circle |
| 15 | ■ Filled square | 21 | ○ Circle with fill (use `bg`) |

> [!TIP]
> `pch` values **21–25** are the *only* symbols that respect the `bg` parameter for fill color. This is a **high-yield KTU point**.

---

### 2.6 Family 5: Line Parameters

| Parameter | Purpose | Allowed Values |
|---|---|---|
| `lty` | Line Type | 0=blank, 1=solid, 2=dashed, 3=dotted, 4=dotdash, 5=longdash, 6=twodash |
| `lwd` | Line Width (relative) | Numeric ≥ 0; default = 1 |
| `lend` | Line End style | 0=round, 1=butt, 2=square |
| `ljoin` | Line Join style | 0=round, 1=mitre, 2=bevel |
| `lmitre` | Mitre limit | Numeric > 1 |

---

### 2.7 Family 6: Axis & Tick Parameters

| Parameter | Purpose | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `lab` | Number of tick intervals on x & y | `c(5, 5, 7)` |
| `las` | Orientation of axis labels | 0 (parallel) |
| `tck` | Tick length as fraction of plot region | `NA` (uses `tcl`) |
| `tcl` | Tick length as fraction of line height | `-0.5` |
| `xaxp`, `yaxp` | Extreme tick positions | Computed |

`las` values: `0`=parallel to axis, `1`=horizontal, `2`=perpendicular, `3`=vertical.

---

### 2.8 Family 7: Margin Parameters

Margins are measured in **inches** (or `mex` lines).

| Parameter | Description | Default (in inches) |
|---|---|---|
| `mai` | Margin sizes (b, l, t, r) | `c(1.02, 0.82, 0.82, 0.42)` |
| `mar` | Same as `mai` but in **text lines** | `c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1)` |
| `mex` | Conversion factor: lines → inches | `1` |
| `oma` | Outer margins (b, l, t, r) | `c(0, 0, 0, 0)` |
| `omi` | Outer margins in **inches** | `c(0, 0, 0, 0)` |

**Conversion relationship:**
$$\text{mar (in lines)} \times mex = \text{mar (in inches)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> `mar` order is **(bottom, left, top, right)** — a *very* common KTU trap. Students often write it in the wrong order.

---

### 2.9 Family 8: Layout & Splitting Parameters

| Parameter | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `mfrow` | Splits device into rows × cols, **filled row-wise** |
| `mfcol` | Splits device into rows × cols, **filled column-wise** |
| `mfg` | Current figure location `c(row, col)` |
| `fig` | Figure region in NDC (0–1) `c(x1, x2, y1, y2)` |
| `fin` | Figure dimensions in inches `c(width, height)` |
| `pin` | Plot region (inner) in inches |
| `plt` | Plot region in NDC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Difference between `mfrow` and `mfcol`:**
> - `par(mfrow = c(2, 2))` → Plots fill left→right, then top→bottom
> - `par(mfcol = c(2, 2))` → Plots fill top→bottom, then left→right

---

### 2.10 Complete KTU Formula / Cheat-Sheet Table

| # | Parameter | Domain | Default | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `cex` | numeric | 1 | Overall character expansion |
| 2 | `cex.axis` | numeric | 0.83 | Axis label size |
| 3 | `cex.lab` | numeric | 1 | Axis title size |
| 4 | `cex.main` | numeric | 1.2 | Main title size |
| 5 | `cex.sub` | numeric | 1 | Subtitle size |
| 6 | `col` | string/int | `"black"` | Plot color |
| 7 | `col.axis` | string/int | — | Axis tick label color |
| 8 | `col.lab` | string/int | — | Axis title color |
| 9 | `col.main` | string/int | — | Main title color |
| 10 | `col.sub` | string/int | — | Subtitle color |
| 11 | `fg` | string/int | `"black"` | Foreground color |
| 12 | `bg` | string/int | `"white"` | Background color |
| 13 | `pch` | int 1–25 / char | 1 | Plotting symbol |
| 14 | `lty` | int 0–6 | 1 | Line type |
| 15 | `lwd` | numeric | 1 | Line width |
| 16 | `lend` | int 0–2 | 1 | Line end style |
| 17 | `ljoin` | int 0–2 | 1 | Line join style |
| 18 | `font` | int 1–5 | 1 | Font face |
| 19 | `font.axis` | int 1–5 | 1 | Axis font |
| 20 | `font.lab` | int 1–5 | 1 | Label font |
| 21 | `font.main` | int 1–5 | 2 | Title font |
| 22 | `font.sub` | int 1–5 | 1 | Subtitle font |
| 23 | `family` | string | `""` | Font family |
| 24 | `ps` | numeric | 12 | Text point size |
| 25 | `lab` | int c(3) | `c(5,5,7)` | Tick intervals |
| 26 | `las` | int 0–3 | 0 | Axis label orientation |
| 27 | `tck` | numeric | NA | Tick length (NDC) |
| 28 | `tcl` | numeric | -0.5 | Tick length (lines) |
| 29 | `mar` | numeric c(4) | `c(5.1,4.1,4.1,2.1)` | Inner margins (lines) |
| 30 | `mai` | numeric c(4) | `c(1.02,0.82,0.82,0.42)` | Inner margins (inches) |
| 31 | `oma` | numeric c(4) | `c(0,0,0,0)` | Outer margins (lines) |
| 32 | `omi` | numeric c(4) | `c(0,0,0,0)` | Outer margins (inches) |
| 33 | `mfrow` | int c(2) | `c(1,1)` | Multi-frame row-wise |
| 34 | `mfcol` | int c(2) | `c(1,1)` | Multi-frame col-wise |
| 35 | `mfg` | int c(4) | `c(1,1,1,1)` | Current figure location |
| 36 | `fig` | num c(4) | computed | Figure region (NDC) |
| 37 | `fin` | num c(2) | computed | Figure size (inches) |
| 38 | `pin` | num c(2) | computed | Plot region (inches) |
| 39 | `plt` | num c(4) | computed | Plot region (NDC) |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Text Size Calculation

When R renders any text element, the final on-screen font size is computed by multiplying **four multiplicative factors**:

$$
\text{Effective Size} = ps \times cex \times cex.\text{scope} \times \text{device-scaling}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For standard screen devices, the device-scaling factor is 1, so we ignore it in KTU problems.

**Worked Example (KTU-typical):**

Suppose `ps = 14`, `cex = 1.2`, and we are rendering the **main title** so `cex.main = 1.5`.

**Step 1:** Identify the relevant factors.
- Base point size: $ps = 14$
- Global cex: $cex = 1.2$
- Title-specific cex: $cex.main = 1.5$

**Step 2:** Apply the formula.

$$
\text{title\_size} = 14 \times 1.2 \times 1.5
$$

**Step 3:** Compute intermediate products.

$$
14 \times 1.2 = 16.8
$$

**Step 4:** Final multiplication.

$$
16.8 \times 1.5 = 25.2
$$

**Result:** The main title is rendered at approximately **25.2 points**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Margin Conversion (`mar` ↔ `mai`)

R maintains two equivalent margin vectors — one in *text lines* (`mar`) and one in *inches* (`mai`). The conversion uses the `mex` parameter, which is the height (in inches) of one text line at the current point size.

$$
mai_i = \frac{mar_i \times ps}{72} \times mex
$$

Or equivalently, since `mex = 1` by default and `1 inch = 72 points`:

$$
mai_i = \frac{mar_i \times ps}{72}
$$

**Worked Example:**

Default: `mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1)` and `ps = 12`, `mex = 1`.

**Step 1:** Calculate height of one line.

$$
\text{one line (in)} = \frac{12}{72} = 0.1667 \text{ inch}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply to bottom margin.

$$
mai_{\text{bottom}} = 5.1 \times 0.1667 = 0.85
$$

> *(The actual default is 1.02 because the calculation uses R's internal character height constant, not just point size.)*

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: `mfrow` vs `mfcol` Plot Filling Order

**Given:** `par(mfrow = c(2, 2))` and 4 plots A, B, C, D.

| Call # | `mfrow` (row-wise) | `mfcol` (col-wise) |
|---|---|---|
| 1st call | A (top-left) | A (top-left) |
| 2nd call | B (top-right) | C (bottom-left) |
| 3rd call | C (bottom-left) | B (top-right) |
| 4th call | D (bottom-right) | D (bottom-right) |

**Step-by-step for `mfrow`:**
1. Cursor at `(1,1)` → Plot A
2. Cursor advances to `(1,2)` → Plot B
3. Cursor advances to `(2,1)` → Plot C
4. Cursor advances to `(2,2)` → Plot D

**Step-by-step for `mfcol`:**
1. Cursor at `(1,1)` → Plot A
2. Cursor advances to `(2,1)` → Plot C
3. Cursor advances to `(1,2)` → Plot B
4. Cursor advances to `(2,2)` → Plot D

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This difference is a **classic 7-mark KTU question**: "Differentiate between mfrow and mfcol with a 2×2 layout example."

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Restoring Par Defaults (Standard KTU Pattern)

```r
# Step 1: Save current state
oldpar <- par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(3, 3, 2, 1))

# Step 2: Generate four plots
plot(1:10, main = "Plot 1", col = "red", pch = 16)
plot(1:10, main = "Plot 2", col = "blue", pch = 17)
plot(1:10, main = "Plot 3", col = "green", pch = 18)
plot(1:10, main = "Plot 4", col = "purple", pch = 19)

# Step 3: RESTORE — Critical for examination
par(oldpar)
```

**Logic of restoration:**
- `par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(...))` *invisibly returns* the previous values as a list.
- Assigning this list to `oldpar` captures the snapshot.
- `par(oldpar)` re-applies those values, restoring the device to its original state.

---

### 3.5 Complete Multi-Parameter Setup: A Production-Quality Example

```r
# 1. Save defaults
op <- par(
  mfrow    = c(2, 2),         # 2x2 grid
  mar      = c(4, 4, 3, 1),   # Bottom, Left, Top, Right
  oma      = c(2, 2, 2, 2),   # Outer margin for main title
  bg       = "lightyellow",   # Background fill
  fg       = "darkblue",      # Foreground
  col      = "steelblue",     # Default plotting color
  col.main = "darkred",       # Title color
  cex.main = 1.3,             # Enlarge title
  font.main= 2,               # Bold title
  pch      = 16,              # Filled circle
  cex      = 0.9              # Slightly smaller text globally
)

# 2. Generate plots
x <- 1:10
plot(x, x,    main = "Linear",     col = "red",    type = "b", lty = 1)
plot(x, x^2,  main = "Quadratic",  col = "blue",   type = "b", lty = 2)
plot(x, sqrt(x), main = "Square Root", col = "green", type = "b", lty = 3)
plot(x, log(x), main = "Logarithmic", col = "purple", type = "b", lty = 4)

# 3. Add an outer title spanning all plots
mtext("Comprehensive Visualization", outer = TRUE, cex = 1.5, font = 2)

# 4. Restore
par(op)
```

**Parameter-by-Parameter Analysis:**

| Line | Parameter(s) | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `mfrow = c(2,2)` | Layout | 2×2 grid |
| `mar = c(4,4,3,1)` | Margins | Generous bottom & left, narrow right |
| `oma = c(2,2,2,2)` | Outer | Space for global title |
| `bg = "lightyellow"` | Background | Soft cream canvas |
| `fg = "darkblue"` | Foreground | Axes & box in navy |
| `col = "steelblue"` | Default | All unspecificed plots in steel blue |
| `col.main = "darkred"` | Title color | Red titles |
| `cex.main = 1.3` | Title size | 30% larger |
| `font.main = 2` | Title weight | Bold |
| `pch = 16` | Symbol | Filled circles |
| `cex = 0.9` | Global | Slight downsizing of all text |

---

### 3.6 The `layout()` Function (Advanced)

While `mfrow` produces an *equally-sized* grid, `layout()` allows **irregular splits** of the device.

```r
# 3 plots: 1 tall on left, 2 stacked on right
layout(matrix(c(1, 1, 2, 3), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE))
plot(1:10, main = "Wide (top)")     # Spans top
plot(1:10, main = "Tall-left (1)")  # Bottom-left
plot(1:10, main = "Tall-right (2)") # Bottom-right
```

**Matrix interpretation:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 1 \\
2 & 3
\end{bmatrix}
$$

- Row 1, Col 1 = `1` → Plot 1
- Row 1, Col 2 = `1` → Plot 1 (so Plot 1 spans both columns)
- Row 2, Col 1 = `2` → Plot 2
- Row 2, Col 2 = `3` → Plot 3
