## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define graphical parameters in R. List any four major categories of graphical parameters used for customizing plots.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Graphical parameters in R are configurable settings that control the visual appearance of plots. They are managed primarily through the `par()` function, which can both query and modify defaults.
**Four major categories** (any four):
1. **Text size parameters** — `cex`, `cex.axis`, `cex.lab`, `cex.main`, `cex.sub`
2. **Color parameters** — `col`, `col.axis`, `col.lab`, `fg`, `bg`
3. **Font parameters** — `font`, `font.main`, `font.lab`, `family`
4. **Margin parameters** — `mar`, `mai`, `oma`, `omi`, `mex`
5. **Layout parameters** — `mfrow`, `mfcol`, `fig`, `pin`

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **What is the role of `mfrow` and `mfcol` parameters in R? Differentiate them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Both `mfrow` and `mfcol` are graphical parameters in R that **split the plotting device into a matrix of sub-plots**.
- `par(mfrow = c(r, c))` divides the device into an $r \times c$ matrix, and plots are drawn **row by row** (left-to-right, then top-to-bottom).
- `par(mfcol = c(r, c))` divides the device into an $r \times c$ matrix, and plots are drawn **column by column** (top-to-bottom, then left-to-right).

**Example:** With `mfrow = c(2, 2)`, the 1st plot goes to top-left, 2nd to top-right, 3rd to bottom-left, 4th to bottom-right.

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the various **font and text-size parameters** in R. Write R code to demonstrate how to set the main title to bold, red, and 1.5 times larger than default. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **color and symbol parameters** (`col`, `bg`, `fg`, `pch`) in R with a suitable R program that plots a scatter plot with custom colors and symbols. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Font Parameters** control the typeface style of text in plots:

| Parameter | Function | Allowed Values |
|---|---|---|
| `font` | Overall font face | 1=plain, 2=**bold**, 3=*italic*, 4=***bold-italic*** |
| `font.main` | Main title | Same scale |
| `font.lab` | Axis labels | Same scale |
| `font.axis` | Axis tick numbers | Same scale |
| `font.sub` | Subtitle | Same scale |
| `family` | Typeface family | `"serif"`, `"sans"`, `"mono"`, `"Helvetica"` |
| `ps` | Point size | Numeric (default 12) |

**Text-size parameters** scale font sizes multiplicatively:

| Parameter | Scope |
|---|---|
| `cex` | All text |
| `cex.main` | Main title |
| `cex.sub` | Subtitle |
| `cex.lab` | Axis labels |
| `cex.axis` | Axis numbers |

**Effective font size formula:**

$$
\text{size} = ps \times cex \times cex.\text{scope}
$$

**R Code Demonstration (1 mark per correct element):**

```r
# Step 1: Save defaults
oldpar <- par(
  font.main = 2,        # Bold main title
  col.main = "red",     # Red color
  cex.main = 1.5,       # 1.5x size
  font.lab = 3,         # Italic axis labels
  family = "serif"      # Serif font family
)

# Step 2: Plot with customized title
plot(
  1:10,
  main = "Customized Title in Bold Red",
  xlab = "X-axis (italic)",
  ylab = "Y-axis (italic)"
)

# Step 3: Restore defaults
par(oldpar)
```

**Explanation of code:**
- `font.main = 2` → Renders title in **bold** weight
- `col.main = "red"` → Renders title in red
- `cex.main = 1.5` → Scales title to 1.5× default
- Combined effect: Title appears **larger, bolder, and red**, fulfilling the requirement.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Color parameters** in R:
- `col` → Default color for plotting elements (lines, points)
- `col.axis`, `col.lab`, `col.main`, `col.sub` → Color of specific text
- `fg` → Foreground color (axes, plot box)
- `bg` → Background color of the plot region
- `bg` is also used as **fill color for symbols `pch 21:25`**

**Symbol parameter `pch`:**
- `pch` accepts integer 1–25 for built-in symbols, characters like `"*"` for custom symbols
- `pch = 16` is a filled circle
- `pch = 21:25` allows distinct `bg` (fill) and `col` (border) colors

**R Program (custom scatter plot):**

```r
# Step 1: Save and configure
op <- par(
  bg   = "lightyellow",   # Cream background
  fg   = "darkblue",      # Navy axes
  pch  = 21,              # Filled circle with border
  col  = "steelblue",     # Border color
  cex  = 1.3,             # Enlarged symbols
  lwd  = 2                # Thicker axes
)

# Step 2: Create custom data
x <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8)
y <- c(2, 4, 1, 5, 7, 6, 8, 9)
groups <- c("A", "B", "A", "B", "A", "B", "A", "B")

# Step 3: Plot with conditional fill colors
plot(
  x, y,
  main = "Custom Scatter Plot",
  xlab = "X Variable",
  ylab = "Y Variable",
  bg   = ifelse(groups == "A", "tomato", "lightgreen")  # Fill by group
)

# Step 4: Add legend with custom pch
legend(
  "topleft",
  legend = c("Group A", "Group B"),
  pch    = 21,
  pt.bg  = c("tomato", "lightgreen"),
  pt.cex = 1.3
)

# Step 5: Restore
par(op)
```

**Explanation:**
- The vectorized `bg = ifelse(...)` assigns different fill colors to points based on group membership.
- `pch = 21` allows the legend and points to have distinct border (`col`) and fill (`bg`) colors.
- This produces a **publication-quality** scatter plot with clear group distinction.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **margin and layout parameters** (`mar`, `mai`, `oma`, `mfrow`, `mfcol`, `fig`) in R. Write an R script that produces a 2×2 grid of plots with custom margins. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain **line parameters** (`lty`, `lwd`, `lend`, `ljoin`) and **axis parameters** (`lab`, `las`, `tck`, `tcl`) in detail with suitable R examples. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Margin Parameters** define the empty space around the plot region:

| Parameter | Meaning | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `mar` | Inner margin in **text lines** (b, l, t, r) | `c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1)` |
| `mai` | Inner margin in **inches** (b, l, t, r) | `c(1.02, 0.82, 0.82, 0.42)` |
| `oma` | Outer margin in text lines | `c(0, 0, 0, 0)` |
| `omi` | Outer margin in inches | `c(0, 0, 0, 0)` |
| `mex` | Margin expansion factor | `1` |

**Layout Parameters** divide the device into sub-plots:

| Parameter | Function |
|---|---|
| `mfrow` | Multi-frame: row-wise fill |
| `mfcol` | Multi-frame: column-wise fill |
| `fig` | Figure region in NDC (0–1) |
| `pin` | Plot region size in inches |
| `fin` | Figure size in inches |

**Diagram (ASCII Schematic of Margins):**

```
+--------------------------------------------+  ← oma top
|  +--------------------------------------+  |  ← mar top
|  |                                      |  |
|  |  mar   +--------------------------+  |  |
|  | left   |                          |  |  ← mar right
|  |        |     PLOT REGION (pin)    |  |  |
|  |        |                          |  |  |
|  |        +--------------------------+  |  |
|  |             mar bottom               |  |
|  +--------------------------------------+  |
|              oma bottom                   |
+--------------------------------------------+
```

**R Script — 2×2 Grid with Custom Margins:**

```r
# Step 1: Save defaults
op <- par(
  mfrow = c(2, 2),            # 2 rows x 2 columns
  mar   = c(3, 3, 2, 1),      # Tight inner margins
  oma   = c(2, 2, 2, 1),      # Outer margin for main title
  bg    = "ivory"
)

# Step 2: Four distinct plots
plot(1:10, type = "b", main = "Line Plot",   col = "red")
plot(1:10, type = "h", main = "Histogram",   col = "blue")
plot(1:10, type = "s", main = "Step Plot",   col = "green")
plot(1:10, type = "p", main = "Points Only", col = "purple")

# Step 3: Add outer title using oma space
mtext("Four-Panel Grid Plot", outer = TRUE, cex = 1.5, font = 2)

# Step 4: Restore
par(op)
```

**Explanation:**
- `mfrow = c(2,2)` divides the device into a 2×2 grid
- `mar = c(3,3,2,1)` keeps margins tight (b=3, l=3, t=2, r=1)
- `oma = c(2,2,2,1)` reserves outer space for a global title
- `mtext(..., outer = TRUE)` places the title in the outer margin

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Line Parameters** control the appearance of lines:

| Parameter | Function | Values |
|---|---|---|
| `lty` | Line type | 0=blank, 1=solid, 2=dashed, 3=dotted, 4=dotdash, 5=longdash, 6=twodash |
| `lwd` | Line width (relative) | Numeric ≥ 1; default = 1 |
| `lend` | Line end style | 0=round, 1=butt, 2=square |
| `ljoin` | Line join style | 0=round, 1=mitre, 2=bevel |
| `lmitre` | Mitre limit | Numeric > 1 |

**Axis Parameters** customize the axes:

| Parameter | Function | Values |
|---|---|---|
| `lab` | Number of tick intervals (x, y, label) | `c(5,5,7)` default |
| `las` | Orientation of axis labels | 0=parallel, 1=horizontal, 2=perpendicular, 3=vertical |
| `tck` | Tick mark length (NDC fraction) | Numeric |
| `tcl` | Tick mark length (line height fraction) | `-0.5` default |
| `xaxp`/`yaxp` | Extreme tick positions (lo, hi, n) | Computed |

**R Code Demonstrating Line and Axis Parameters:**

```r
op <- par(
  mfrow = c(1, 2),
  mar   = c(4, 4, 3, 1)
)

# Plot 1: Demonstrating line parameters
plot(
  1:10, (1:10)^2,
  type  = "b",                    # Both points and lines
  lty   = 2,                      # Dashed line
  lwd   = 3,                      # Thick line
  lend  = 2,                      # Square line ends
  ljoin = 0,                      # Round line joins
  pch   = 16,                     # Filled circles
  col   = "darkred",
  main  = "Line Parameter Demo"
)

# Plot 2: Demonstrating axis parameters
plot(
  1:10, sqrt(1:10),
  type  = "b",
  col   = "darkblue",
  las   = 2,                      # Perpendicular axis labels
  tck   = -0.05,                  # Inward ticks (5% of plot width)
  lab   = c(10, 10, 7),           # More tick marks
  main  = "Axis Parameter Demo"
)

par(op)
```

**Explanation of axis parameters:**
- `las = 2` rotates y-axis numbers to read horizontally
- `tck = -0.05` creates inward-pointing tick marks (negative = inside)
- `lab = c(10, 10, 7)` requests 10 tick intervals on x and y axes

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Margin Order Trap** (lose 1 mark): Students frequently write `mar = c(top, left, bottom, right)`. Correct order is **(bottom, left, top, right)**.
> 2. **`mfrow` vs `mfcol` Confusion** (lose 1 mark): Students often confuse row-wise vs column-wise. Remember: **r**ow-wise = `mfrow` (like reading a book).
> 3. **Forgetting `par(oldpar)`** (lose 1 mark): The restoration line is *expected* in 14-mark answers; omitting it shows incomplete understanding of `par()` semantics.
> 4. **Using `pch` > 25 without quotation**: `pch = "A"` is valid (uses ASCII), but `pch = 30` falls outside the symbol range and triggers a warning.
> 5. **Confusing `mar` and `mai`**: They represent the same margins in *different units*. The relationship is $mai = mar \times ps / 72$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4 — Graphical Parameters**

- [ ] **Core function:** `par()` is the master function; `par()` with no args *returns* current state; `par(name=value)` *sets* parameters and invisibly returns the previous state.
- [ ] **`cex` family:** `cex` (overall), `cex.axis`, `cex.lab`, `cex.main`, `cex.sub`. Default is `1` (no scaling).
- [ ] **Effective text size formula:** $\text{size} = ps \times cex \times cex.\text{scope}$.
- [ ] **Font integer codes:** `1`=plain, `2`=**bold**, `3`=*italic*, `4`=***bold italic***, `5`=Cyrillic.
- [ ] **`pch` magic numbers:** `16`=filled circle, `17`=filled triangle, `21:25`=symbols with fill (`bg` works).
- [ ] **`lty` codes:** `0`=blank, `1`=solid, `2`=dashed, `3`=dotted, `4`=dotdash, `5`=longdash, `6`=twodash.
- [ ] **`lwd` default:** `1`; doubling makes line twice as thick.
- [ ] **`las` values:** `0`=parallel, `1`=horizontal, `2`=perpendicular, `3`=vertical.
- [ ] **`mar` order:** Bottom, **L**eft, **T**op, **R**ight → remember **BLT-R** or **B-L-T-R** mnemonic.
- [ ] **`mar` default:** `c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1)`.
- [ ] **`mfrow` vs `mfcol`:** `mfrow` fills **r**ows first; `mfcol` fills **c**olumns first.
- [ ] **`fig` units:** Normalized Device Coordinates (NDC), range 0 to 1.
- [ ] **Restoration pattern:** `op <- par(...); ...; par(op)` — *always* include in answers.
- [ ] **`layout()` vs `mfrow`:** `layout()` allows irregular splits; `mfrow` only produces equal-sized grids.
- [ ] **`colors()` function:** Returns 657 named colors (KTU factoid).
- [ ] **`bg` dual role:** Background of plot *and* fill color for `pch 21:25`.
- [ ] **`family` values:** `"serif"`, `"sans"`, `"mono"`, `"Helvetica"`, etc.
- [ ] **`ps` default:** 12 points.
- [ ] **`mex` default:** 1 (margin expansion factor in inches per line).
- [ ] **Save/restore idiom is mandatory** in KTU 14-mark plot-customization answers.
- [ ] **`mtext(..., outer = TRUE)`** is used with `oma` to place global titles spanning multiple sub-plots.
- [ ] **All `par()` modifications are session-level** — they persist until changed or device is closed.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Graphics Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT631 - R-PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Graphics Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **R programming**, *Basic Graphics Functions* refer to the built-in (a.k.a. **base graphics** or **traditional graphics**) family of functions provided by the `graphics` package — the default, device-driven plotting system that ships with every R installation. These functions operate on a *painter's model* paradigm: each graphical element is drawn on top of an active plotting device (screen, PDF, PNG, etc.) and is rendered in the exact order it is invoked.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> *Base graphics in R is a procedural, two-stage plotting framework consisting of (a) high-level plotting functions (e.g., `plot()`, `hist()`, `barplot()`, `boxplot()`, `pie()`) that initialize a fresh plot region, and (b) low-level annotation functions (e.g., `lines()`, `points()`, `text()`, `legend()`, `title()`, `axis()`) that incrementally add or modify graphical primitives on an existing plot.*

The framework is controlled globally by the function **`par()`** (graphical parameters), which exposes **more than 70** tunable parameters for fonts, colors, line types, margins, multi-figure layouts, and axis configurations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Painter's Canvas

Imagine you are a **watercolor painter** working on a stretched canvas:

| Painter's Action | R Base Graphics Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Stretching a fresh, blank canvas | Opening a new plotting device (`plot.new()`, `dev.new()`) |
| Sketching the main composition | High-level call: `plot(x, y)` |
| Adding details (a tree, a bird) on top | Low-level calls: `points()`, `lines()`, `text()` |
| Selecting brush size and color | `par()` settings: `lwd`, `col`, `cex` |
| Splitting canvas into 4 quadrants for 4 mini-paintings | `par(mfrow = c(2, 2))` |
| Signing the artwork | `title(main = "My Plot")` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (Frequently Tested):**
> Unlike *grid* or *ggplot2* graphics, base graphics **does not store the plot as an editable object** you can manipulate later. Once a primitive is drawn, it is "baked" into the device. To modify, you must redraw — this is the **destructive** nature of base graphics.

## 1.3 Two-Tier Function Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **Tier 1 — High-Level Functions** (start a new plot):
> `plot()`, `hist()`, `barplot()`, `boxplot()`, `pie()`, `stripchart()`, `sunflowerplot()`, `mosaicplot()`, `pairs()`, `coplot()`, `matplot()`, `dotchart()`, `fourfoldplot()`.
>
> **Tier 2 — Low-Level Functions** (annotate an existing plot):
> `points()`, `lines()`, `abline()`, `text()`, `mtext()`, `legend()`, `title()`, `axis()`, `box()`, `rect()`, `polygon()`, `segments()`, `arrows()`, `symbols()`, `rug()`, `grid()`, `identify()`, `locator()`.

**Physical constants / Defaults worth memorizing (Board Hot List):**

- **Default plotting character (`pch`) = 1** (open circle)
- **Default color (`col`) = "black"**
- **Default line type (`lty`) = 1** (solid line)
- **Default line width (`lwd`) = 1**
- **Default character expansion (`cex`) = 1**
- **Default plot margins `mar` = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1)** (bottom, left, top, right, in *text lines*)
- **Default outer margin `oma` = c(0, 0, 0, 0)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How the Base Graphics Engine Works

The execution of any base-graphics call follows a **deterministic 6-step pipeline**:

1. **Device Resolution** — R checks whether an active graphics device is open. If not, it auto-opens the default (`quartz` on macOS, `windows` on Windows, `x11` on Linux).
2. **Plot Region Computation** — The plotting region is computed using the current `par()` settings (`fig`, `fin`, `pin`, `mar`, `oma`).
3. **Coordinate Mapping (User → Device)** — User data coordinates are mapped to inches via the formula in §2.2.
4. **Primitive Rendering** — Drawing commands (lines, points, text) are emitted to the device's render queue.
5. **Annotation Phase** — Axes, labels, title, and box are drawn *after* the data primitives (controlled by `ann` and `axes` arguments).
6. **Display / Flush** — The device buffer is flushed to the screen or file.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Why and How" Matters in the Exam:**
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Why does the second `plot()` call erase the first one?"*
> **Answer:** Because every high-level call first calls `plot.new()`, which clears the active device. To keep the old plot visible, you must use a *low-level* function (e.g., `points()`, `lines()`) on the same device, OR open a new device via `dev.new()`.

## 2.2 The Coordinate-Transformation Equation (High-Yield Formula)

When R draws a point at data coordinates $(x, y)$, it first transforms to **normalized device coordinates (NDC)** in $[0, 1] \times [0, 1]$:

$$
x_{\text{ndc}} \;=\; \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}
\qquad\qquad
y_{\text{ndc}} \;=\; \frac{y - y_{\min}}{y_{\max} - y_{\min}}
$$

These are then mapped to **inches** on the physical device:

$$
x_{\text{inch}} \;=\; x_{\text{ndc}} \cdot p_{\text{width}}
\qquad\qquad
y_{\text{inch}} \;=\; y_{\text{ndc}} \cdot p_{\text{height}}
$$

where $p_{\text{width}}$ and $p_{\text{height}}$ are the plot-region dimensions in inches (returned by `par("pin")`).

For **logarithmic axes** (set via `log = "x"`, `"y"`, or `"xy"`), the user data is first transformed by:

$$
\tilde{x} \;=\; \log_{10}(x)
\qquad\qquad
\tilde{y} \;=\; \log_{10}(y)
$$

and the same NDC pipeline is applied to $(\tilde{x}, \tilde{y})$. Hence **negative or zero values are invalid** on a log axis.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Parameter Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — at least 60% of Part-A questions on graphics test one of these rows.**

| Parameter (in `par()` or plot call) | Purpose / Role | Default Value | KTU-Relevant Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| `pch` | Plotting character (symbol used for points) | `1` (open circle) | Values 21–25 accept a fill color via `bg =` |
| `col` | Color of plotting symbols, lines, text | `"black"` | Accepts name, hex (`"#FF0000"`), or `rgb(r, g, b, a)` |
| `lty` | Line type | `1` (solid) | `1`=solid, `2`=dashed, `3`=dotted, `4`=dotdash, `5`=longdash, `6`=twodash |
| `lwd` | Line width (multiple of default) | `1` | Must be a positive integer/float |
| `cex` | Character / symbol expansion factor | `1` | Applies to plotting characters; `cex.lab`, `cex.main`, `cex.axis` specialize it |
| `cex.axis` | Axis-numeric-label expansion | `1` | Independent of main title scaling |
| `cex.main` | Main-title character expansion | `1.2` (note: differs from base `cex`) | — |
| `mfrow` | Multi-figure layout: c(nrow, ncol), filled row-wise | `c(1, 1)` | Use `mfcol` for column-wise filling |
| `mar` | Margins of plot region in *text lines*: c(bottom, left, top, right) | `c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1)` | All four must be non-negative |
| `oma` | Outer margin (around the multi-figure matrix) | `c(0, 0, 0, 0)` | Used for global titles via `mtext()` |
| `las` | Axis-label orientation | `0` (parallel to axis) | `0`=parallel, `1`=horizontal, `2`=perpendicular, `3`=vertical |
| `xlim`, `ylim` | X / Y axis limits: `c(min, max)` | Auto from data | Pass a numeric vector of length 2 |
| `xlab`, `ylab` | X / Y axis label text | `""` (or variable name) | — |
| `main` | Main title | `""` | Can be a single string or an expression |
| `sub` | Sub-title (drawn below x-axis) | `""` | — |
| `ann` | If `FALSE`, suppresses `xlab` / `ylab` / `main` | `TRUE` | Useful for `matplot`-style custom annotations |
| `axes` | If `FALSE`, suppresses axes and box | `TRUE` | Combine with `axis(...)` for manual axes |
| `type` | Plot type in `plot()` | `"p"` (points) | `"p"`=points, `"l"`=lines, `"b"`=both, `"o"`=overlaid, `"h"`=hist-like, `"s"`/`"S"`=stair, `"n"`=no plotting (sets up axes only) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where this appears in industry / research:**
> - **Signal Processing:** `plot(x, type = "l")` of time-series telemetry from IoT sensors.
> - **Quality Control:** `boxplot()` of manufacturing batch measurements to detect outliers (Six-Sigma).
> - **Bio-statistics & Pharma:** `hist()` of clinical-trial response data; `pairs()` for multivariate screening.
> - **Econometrics:** `pie()` of budget allocation; `barplot()` of revenue across quarters.
> - **Data Science EDA:** `pairs()`, `mosaicplot()`, and `matplot()` are staples in the *Exploratory Data Analysis* phase before any model fitting.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Walkthrough 1 — Anatomy of a `plot()` Call (Type Variants)

We trace what R *does* for each `type` argument, in pseudocode-execution order.

```r
# Input data
x <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
y <- c(2, 4, 1, 8, 7)

# --- Variant A: scatter (default) ---
plot(x, y, type = "p", main = "Scatter (type='p')")
# Engine steps:
#   1. plot.new()       → opens new device region
#   2. plot.window()    → maps data range to NDC, sets up axes
#   3. axis(side = 1)   → draws x-axis
#   4. axis(side = 2)   → draws y-axis
#   5. box()            → draws bounding box
#   6. points(x, y)     → plots 5 open circles at pch = 1
#   7. title(...)       → adds main title

# --- Variant B: lines only ---
plot(x, y, type = "l", col = "blue", lwd = 2,
     main = "Line (type='l')")
#   Same as A, except step 6 is replaced by lines(x, y) connecting
#   points in order with a solid blue line of width 2.

# --- Variant C: both points and lines (no overlap) ---
plot(x, y, type = "b", pch = 17, col = "darkgreen",
     main = "Both (type='b')")
#   R cycles: draws point, then line-segment-to-next-point, then
#   point, ... — segments stop short of the next marker.

# --- Variant D: scaffold-only frame ---
plot(x, y, type = "n", xlab = "Time (s)", ylab = "Amplitude",
     main = "Empty frame (type='n')")
#   NO data is drawn.  Only axes, labels, and box appear.
#   This is the *scaffold* pattern used in textbooks: students
#   then call points(), lines(), text() manually.

# --- Variant E: histogram-like vertical bars ---
plot(x, y, type = "h", col = "red", lwd = 4,
     main = "Vertical bars (type='h')")
```

**Why `type = "n"` is your friend (Board Tip):** Setting `type = "n"` is the canonical way to set up *custom* axes, limits, and labels *without* plotting data first. You then add primitives with low-level functions, giving total control.

## 3.2 Walkthrough 2 — Multi-Figure Layout with `par(mfrow)`

We derive the layout for a 2×2 figure matrix, then prove it works.

```r
# --- Step 1: save and restore par() to avoid side-effects ---
oldpar <- par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1), oma = c(0, 0, 2, 0))

# --- Step 2: draw 4 sequential high-level plots ---
plot(1:10, (1:10)^2,   type = "b", main = "Quadratic")
plot(1:10, sqrt(1:10), type = "b", main = "Square root")
hist(rnorm(200, 5, 2),             main = "Histogram", col = "lightblue")
boxplot(iris$Sepal.Length ~ iris$Species, main = "Boxplot", col = "tan")

# --- Step 3: a global title in the outer margin ---
mtext("Demonstration of mfrow = c(2, 2)", outer = TRUE, cex = 1.5, font = 2)

# --- Step 4: restore par() (CRITICAL in functions/scripts) ---
par(oldpar)
```

**Logical Trace of the `mfrow` Filling Order:**

$$
\text{Plot}_1 \to \text{(row 1, col 1)},\quad
\text{Plot}_2 \to \text{(row 1, col 2)},\quad
\text{Plot}_3 \to \text{(row 2, col 1)},\quad
\text{Plot}_4 \to \text{(row 2, col 2)}
$$

If you swap to **`mfcol = c(2, 2)`**, the filling is **column-wise**:

$$
\text{Plot}_1 \to \text{(row 1, col 1)},\quad
\text{Plot}_2 \to \text{(row 2, col 1)},\quad
\text{Plot}_3 \to \text{(row 1, col 2)},\quad
\text{Plot}_4 \to \text{(row 2, col 2)}
$$

## 3.3 Walkthrough 3 — `hist()` and the Density Transformation

The `hist()` function computes frequencies by default and normalizes them to a density when `freq = FALSE`. The transformation is:

$$
\text{density}_i \;=\; \frac{n_i}{N \cdot \Delta b}
$$

where $n_i$ is the count in bin $i$, $N$ is the total sample size, and $\Delta b$ is the bin width. The y-axis is then scaled so the **total area = 1** (a probability density):

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{density}_i \cdot \Delta b \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \;=\; 1
$$

```r
set.seed(42)
data <- rnorm(1000, mean = 100, sd = 15)

# Default frequency histogram
hist(data, breaks = 30, col = "steelblue",
     main = "Frequency Histogram (default)")

# Density histogram (area = 1)
hist(data, breaks = 30, freq = FALSE, col = "salmon",
     main = "Density Histogram (freq = FALSE)")

# Overlay a kernel density estimate
lines(density(data), col = "darkred", lwd = 2)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Trick:** When `freq = FALSE`, you can overlay a `density()` curve. When `freq = TRUE` (default), the y-axis is in raw counts — overlaying a density curve will look *wrong* because of the y-scale mismatch.

## 3.4 Walkthrough 4 — `barplot()` with Stacked vs Grouped Layouts

The `beside` argument controls layout:

$$
\text{Layout} = \begin{cases}
\text{Grouped (side-by-side)} & \text{if } beside = \text{TRUE} \\
\text{Stacked (cumulative)} & \text{if } beside = \text{FALSE (default)}
\end{cases}
$$

```r
sales <- matrix(c(23, 45, 31, 50, 28, 60, 35, 40, 22, 38, 47, 33),
                nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)
colnames(sales) <- c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4")
rownames(sales) <- c("North", "South", "West")

# Stacked barplot
barplot(sales, beside = FALSE, col = c("tomato", "skyblue", "gold"),
        legend.text = rownames(sales), main = "Stacked Sales")

# Grouped barplot
barplot(sales, beside = TRUE, col = c("tomato", "skyblue", "gold"),
        legend.text = rownames(sales), main = "Grouped Sales")
```

## 3.5 Walkthrough 5 — Layered Annotation Pattern (the Textbook Method)

```r
# --- Stage 1: empty scaffold ---
plot(1:10, 1:10, type = "n",
     xlab = "X-axis label", ylab = "Y-axis label",
     main = "Fully Annotated Plot",
     xlim = c(0, 11), ylim = c(0, 11))

# --- Stage 2: gridlines ---
grid(nx = 10, ny = 10, col = "lightgray", lty = "dotted")

# --- Stage 3: data points ---
points(c(2, 5, 8), c(3, 6, 9), pch = 19, col = "blue", cex = 1.5)

# --- Stage 4: a regression line ---
abline(a = 1, b = 0.8, col = "red", lwd = 2)

# --- Stage 5: text labels at each point ---
text(c(2, 5, 8), c(3, 6, 9),
     labels = c("A", "B", "C"), pos = 3, offset = 0.8, col = "blue")

# --- Stage 6: legend ---
legend("bottomright", legend = c("Data", "Fit"),
       pch = c(19, NA), lty = c(NA, 1), col = c("blue", "red"),
       lwd = c(NA, 2), bty = "n")

# --- Stage 7: horizontal/vertical reference lines ---
abline(h = 5, v = 5, col = "darkgreen", lty = "dashed")

# --- Stage 8: an arrow callout ---
arrows(x0 = 9, y0 = 2, x1 = 8, y1 = 9, col = "purple", lwd = 2, length = 0.1)
text(9.3, 2, "Outlier", col = "purple", pos = 4)
```

**Order matters:** If you call `grid()` *after* `points()`, the gridlines will overlay your data. Place it *after* the scaffold but *before* the data primitives.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Plot

| Data Type | Goal | Recommended Function | KTU One-Liner |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 numeric vector | Show distribution | `hist()` | `hist(x)` |
| 1 numeric vector | Show 5-number summary & outliers | `boxplot()` | `boxplot(x)` |
| 2 numeric vectors | Show relationship | `plot(x, y)` | `plot(x, y)` |
| Categorical vs numeric | Compare groups | `boxplot(n ~ c)` | `boxplot(value ~ group, data = df)` |
| Categorical counts | Show proportions | `pie()` or `barplot()` | `pie(table(c))` |
| Numeric matrix | Compare columns | `matplot()` or `pairs()` | `matplot(M, type = "l")` |
| Time-series | Show trend | `plot(ts, type = "l")` | `plot.ts(ts)` |
| 1-D scatter (overplotting) | Show density | `stripchart()` or `sunflowerplot()` | `stripchart(x, method = "jitter")` |
| Contingency table | Show association | `mosaicplot()` | `mosaicplot(table(a, b))` |
| Multiple categorical | Show proportions by group | `fourfoldplot()` | `fourfoldplot(2x2xk)` |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Base Graphics Engine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User R Script: plot x y] --> B[plot new call]
    B --> C{Is active device open?}
    C -- No --> D[dev new: open default device]
    C -- Yes --> E[Reuse active device]
    D --> F[Read par settings]
    E --> F
    F --> G[plot new: clear device]
    G --> H[plot window: map data to NDC]
    H --> I[Compute axis tick locations]
    I --> J[Draw axes: axis side 1 2 3 4]
    J --> K[Draw bounding box: box]
    K --> L{Select type}
    L -- type p --> M1[Call points]
    L -- type l --> M2[Call lines]
    L -- type b --> M3[Call both]
    L -- type h --> M4[Call segments vertical]
    L -- type n --> M5[No plotting: scaffold only]
    L -- type s --> M6[Call lines stair]
    M1 --> N[Apply labels: xlab ylab]
    M2 --> N
    M3 --> N
    M4 --> N
    M5 --> N
    M6 --> N
    N --> O[Add title: main sub]
    O --> P[Flush buffer to device]
    P --> Q[Render on screen or file]
    Q --> R[Plot complete]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Two-Tier Function Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Tier1[High Level: starts new plot]
        A1[plot]
        A2[hist]
        A3[barplot]
        A4[boxplot]
        A5[pie]
        A6[pairs]
        A7[matplot]
    end
    subgraph Tier2[Low Level: annotates existing plot]
        B1[points]
        B2[lines]
        B3[abline]
        B4[text]
        B5[axis]
        B6[legend]
        B7[title]
        B8[arrows]
        B9[rect]
    end
    Tier1 -->|calls plot.new| Device[(Active Graphics Device)]
    Tier2 -->|adds primitives| Device
    Device --> Screen[Screen PDF PNG SVG ...]
    style Tier1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085
    style Tier2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style Device fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — `par()` Parameter Scope

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[par call: mfrow c 2 2 mar c 4 4 2 1] --> S1[Store previous par values]
    S1 --> S2[Apply new par values globally]
    S2 --> P1[Subplot 1: plot 1]
    P1 --> P2[Subplot 2: plot 2]
    P2 --> P3[Subplot 3: plot 3]
    P3 --> P4[Subplot 4: plot 4]
    P4 --> R1[Restore previous par: par oldpar]
    R1 --> End[Device state returned to original]
    style S1 fill:#f8d7da
    style S2 fill:#fff3cd
    style R1 fill:#d4edda
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Layered Annotation Order (the canonical pattern)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: plot x y type n] --> S2[Step 2: grid nx ny]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: points or lines data primitives]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: abline reference lines]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: text labels]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: legend]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: arrows for callouts]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: title and mtext outer]
    S8 --> Done[Final annotated plot]
    style S1 fill:#cce5ff
    style S8 fill:#d4edda
    style Done fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between high-level and low-level plotting functions in R. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
High-level plotting functions are those that **initialize a new plot** and set up a complete coordinate system, axes, and labels from scratch. They overwrite any existing plot on the active device. *Examples: `plot()`, `hist()`, `barplot()`, `boxplot()`, `pie()`.*

Low-level plotting functions **add or modify graphical elements on an existing plot** without resetting the device. They assume a plot is already active. *Examples: `points()`, `lines()`, `abline()`, `text()`, `legend()`, `axis()`, `title()`, `arrows()`.* (½ mark for definition, ½ for difference, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for low-level examples — *3 marks total*.)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the purpose of `par(mfrow = c(2, 2))` in R? How does it differ from `par(mfcol = c(2, 2))`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
`par(mfrow = c(2, 2))` divides the active graphics device into a **2-row × 2-column grid of sub-plots** that are filled **row by row** (left-to-right, top-to-bottom). (1½ marks)

`par(mfcol = c(2, 2))` also creates a 2×2 grid but fills it **column by column** (top-to-bottom, left-to-right). (1½ marks)

**One-line example:** With `mfrow`, plots appear as |P1 P2 / P3 P4|; with `mfcol`, they appear as |P1 P3 / P2 P4|.

---

## PART B — ESE Module-Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of R's base graphics system. Describe the role of `par()` with at least **six** important graphical parameters and their default values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write R code to:
   (i) Create a 2×2 multi-figure layout.
   (ii) Draw (1) a scatter plot of `y = x²` for $x \in [1, 10]$, (2) a histogram of 200 random normal samples, (3) a boxplot of `iris$Sepal.Length` grouped by species, and (4) a pie chart of `table(mtcars$cyl)`.
   (iii) Add a global title in the outer margin and restore `par()` at the end. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Solution to (a) — Architecture of Base Graphics (7 marks):**

R's base graphics system follows a **two-tier, painter's-model architecture** controlled by the function `par()`. (1 mark)

**Tier 1 — High-Level Functions:** `plot()`, `hist()`, `barplot()`, `boxplot()`, `pie()`. Each call invokes `plot.new()` which clears the active device and opens a fresh plot region. (1 mark)

**Tier 2 — Low-Level Functions:** `points()`, `lines()`, `abline()`, `text()`, `axis()`, `legend()`, `title()`. They assume an active plot and add primitives without resetting it. (1 mark)

**Role of `par()` (Graphical Parameters):** `par()` sets/queries global graphical settings. Six important parameters and defaults: (3 marks — ½ mark each)

1. **`pch = 1`** — plotting character (open circle).
2. **`col = "black"`** — default color.
3. **`lty = 1`** — line type (solid); valid values 1–6.
4. **`lwd = 1`** — line width multiplier.
5. **`mfrow = c(1, 1)`** — multi-figure layout (row-wise fill).
6. **`mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1)`** — plot margins in lines (bottom, left, top, right).

**Coordinate mapping:** User data $(x, y)$ is mapped to NDC via $(x - x_{\min}) / (x_{\max} - x_{\min})$, then to inches via `par("pin")`. (1 mark)

---

**Solution to (b) — R Code (7 marks):**

```r
# Save current par() to restore later
oldpar <- par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1), oma = c(0, 0, 2, 0))

# (1) Scatter plot of y = x^2
x <- 1:10
plot(x, x^2, type = "b", pch = 19, col = "steelblue",
     main = "y = x^2", xlab = "x", ylab = "y")

# (2) Histogram of 200 random normal samples
set.seed(123)
hist(rnorm(200, mean = 0, sd = 1), breaks = 15,
     col = "lightgreen", main = "Histogram of N(0, 1)")

# (3) Boxplot grouped by species
boxplot(iris$Sepal.Length ~ iris$Species,
        col = c("tomato", "gold", "skyblue"),
        main = "Sepal Length by Species", ylab = "Sepal Length")

# (4) Pie chart of cylinder counts
pie(table(mtcars$cyl),
    labels = paste(names(table(mtcars$cyl)), "cyl"),
    col = c("lightcoral", "khaki", "lightblue"),
    main = "Cylinders in mtcars")

# Global title in outer margin
mtext("Base Graphics Demo: 2x2 Layout", outer = TRUE,
      cex = 1.4, font = 2)

# Restore par() — CRITICAL
par(oldpar)
```

**Mark split (7 marks):** Layout setup 1, scatter 1, histogram 1, boxplot 1, pie 1, mtext 1, restore 1.

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the role and usage of the following low-level annotation functions in R base graphics: `points()`, `lines()`, `abline()`, `text()`, `legend()`, `title()`. For each, state one typical use-case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an R program that:
   (i) Creates a scaffold plot of $x \in [0, 10]$ with axes and labels but **no data**.
   (ii) Adds a grid of dashed light-gray lines.
   (iii) Plots the curve $y = \sin(x)$ as a red solid line of width 2.
   (iv) Highlights the maximum point $(\pi/2, 1)$ with a blue filled circle and labels it `"max"`.
   (v) Adds a horizontal reference line at $y = 0$ and a legend in the top-right corner. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Solution to (a) — Low-Level Functions (7 marks):**

| Function | Role | Typical Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| `points(x, y, ...)` | Adds a set of points to an existing plot without resetting axes. | Overlaying a second data series. |
| `lines(x, y, ...)` | Connects points with line segments in given order. | Drawing a fitted curve on top of a scatter. |
| `abline(a, b, ...)` | Draws a straight line $y = a + b x$; or `h =` for horizontal, `v =` for vertical. | Reference line / OLS regression overlay. |
| `text(x, y, labels, ...)` | Places character strings at specified coordinates. | Labeling outliers / data points. |
| `legend(x, y, legend, ...)` | Adds a legend box at the given location. | Identifying multiple series in a plot. |
| `title(main, sub, xlab, ylab)` | Adds main title, sub-title, and axis labels. | Finalizing a plot for publication. |

(1 mark for `points` & `lines` combined, 1 mark each for the remaining four, plus 1 mark for usage examples — *7 marks total*.)

---

**Solution to (b) — R Program (7 marks):**

```r
# (i) Scaffold plot — type = "n" sets axes & limits but draws no data
x <- seq(0, 10, length.out = 200)
plot(x, sin(x), type = "n",
     xlab = "x (radians)", ylab = "sin(x)",
     main = "Annotated sin(x) Curve",
     xlim = c(0, 10), ylim = c(-1.2, 1.2))

# (ii) Dashed light-gray grid
grid(nx = 10, ny = 10, col = "lightgray", lty = "dashed")

# (iii) sin(x) curve as red solid line, width 2
lines(x, sin(x), col = "red", lwd = 2)

# (iv) Highlight the maximum at (pi/2, 1)
points(pi/2, 1, pch = 19, col = "blue", cex = 1.8)
text(pi/2, 1, labels = "max", pos = 3, offset = 1, col = "blue")

# (v) Reference line at y = 0 and legend
abline(h = 0, col = "black", lwd = 1)
legend("topright",
       legend = c("sin(x)", "maximum"),
       col = c("red", "blue"),
       lty = c(1, NA), pch = c(NA, 19),
       lwd = c(2, NA), bty = "n")
```

**Mark split (7 marks):** Scaffold 1, grid 1, sin curve 1, max highlight 1, label 1, abline 1, legend 1.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1–2 marks here!):**
> 1. **Forgetting to restore `par()`** after `par(mfrow = ...)` — examiner deducts ½ mark for *side-effect leakage*.
> 2. **Confusing `mfrow` vs `mfcol`** filling order — examiners specifically test this; memorize "row-wise vs column-wise".
> 3. **Using `hist()` with `freq = FALSE` and then overlaying raw counts** — y-scale mismatch; always overlay `density()`.
> 4. **Plotting on top of a `type = "n"` scaffold in the wrong order** — `grid()` *after* `points()` will hide your data.
> 5. **Forgetting that `barplot()` default is `beside = FALSE`** (stacked) — students often assume grouped.
> 6. **Using `legend()` without `bty = "n"`** when a borderless legend is required.
> 7. **Putting `pch`, `lty`, `lwd` arguments inside `plot()` without checking the function accepts them** — `hist()` and `barplot()` have *different* parameter signatures; cross-check with `?hist`.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Base graphics = painter's model** (procedural, destructive, device-driven).
- ✅ **Two tiers:** High-level (`plot`, `hist`, `barplot`, `boxplot`, `pie`) vs Low-level (`points`, `lines`, `abline`, `text`, `legend`, `title`, `axis`).
- ✅ **`par()`** is the global control panel — always **save and restore** it: `oldpar <- par(...); ...; par(oldpar)`.
- ✅ **`mfrow = c(r, c)`** fills row-wise; **`mfcol`** fills column-wise.
- ✅ **Defaults to memorize:** `pch = 1`, `col = "black"`, `lty = 1`, `lwd = 1`, `cex = 1`, `mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1)`, `oma = c(0, 0, 0, 0)`.
- ✅ **`type = "n"`** sets up axes, labels, limits but draws **no data** — the canonical scaffold.
- ✅ **`type` variants in `plot()`:** `"p"`, `"l"`, `"b"`, `"o"`, `"h"`, `"s"`, `"S"`, `"n"`.
- ✅ **Histogram:** default `freq = TRUE`; set `freq = FALSE` for density (area = 1).
- ✅ **Barplot:** `beside = FALSE` (stacked, default) vs `beside = TRUE` (grouped).
- ✅ **Boxplot formula** for grouping: `boxplot(value ~ group, data = df)`.
- ✅ **Annotation order matters:** scaffold → grid → data primitives → reference lines → text → legend → title.
- ✅ **Coordinate transform:** data → NDC via $(x - x_{\min})/(x_{\max} - x_{\min})$ → inches via `par("pin")`.
- ✅ **Log axes:** `log = "x"` calls `log10()` internally; **negatives/zero forbidden**.
- ✅ **Color specs:** named (`"red"`), hex (`"#FF0000"`), or `rgb(r, g, b, alpha)`.
- ✅ **Pie chart limitation:** pie is *hard to read* for > 5 categories; prefer `barplot` in that case.
- ✅ **Always close non-default devices** with `dev.off()` to avoid "cannot open file" errors in scripts.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lattice Graphics (Lattice Functions)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT631 - R-PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lattice Graphics (Lattice Functions)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lattice Graphics** is a powerful, high-level data visualization system in R, implemented through the `lattice` package (originally derived from S-PLUS Trellis Graphics developed by William S. Cleveland). It is designed specifically to visualize **multivariate**, **multidimensional**, and **conditional** relationships within datasets through a structured "small multiples" or "conditioning" framework.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the `lattice` package is a **Trellis-style** implementation that allows complex relationships to be displayed by conditioning on one or more variables, producing a matrix of panels (small plots) that share common scales and axes. The package is loaded using:

```r
library(lattice)
```

The fundamental premise of lattice is the **formula interface**:
```r
lattice_function(formula, data = dataframe, groups = variable)
```

The generic structure follows:
```r
y ~ x | condition
```
- `y` → Dependent variable (Y-axis response)
- `x` → Independent variable (X-axis predictor)
- `condition` → Conditioning variable(s) that split data into panels

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Newspaper Infographic Grid**
> Imagine reading a high-quality newspaper like *The Hindu* or *The New York Times* on election day. Instead of a single chaotic bar chart, you see a **grid of mini-charts** — one for each state, one for each age group, one for each income bracket. Each mini-chart is a **panel**. All panels share the **same axes and scales**, so your eye can instantly compare them. This is exactly what Lattice does in R!

**Another Analogy — The Spreadsheet of Plots:**
Think of a `data.frame` of plots. Rows represent combinations of conditioning variables, columns represent other conditioning factors, and each cell holds a plot. Lattice automatically arranges them.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Components

| Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
|------|------------|------------------|
| **Trellis** | The original S-PLUS name for the system; synonymous with Lattice | High-frequency exam term |
| **Panel** | A single plot inside the conditioning grid | Core structural unit |
| **Shingle** | A conditioning variable that allows overlapping intervals | Used in `cofactor()` and `equal.count()` |
| **Strip** | A label at the top of each panel identifying the conditioning value | Auto-generated by Lattice |
| **Panel Function** | Custom code controlling what is drawn inside each panel | Argument `panel =` |
| **Groups Argument** | A variable used to distinguish series by color/symbol within a panel | `groups = variable` |

## 1.4 Standard Lattice Function Catalogue

The `lattice` package provides **eight core high-level functions**, each tailored to a specific plot type:

1. **`xyplot()`** — Scatter plots (most commonly used)
2. **`bwplot()`** — Box-and-whisker plots
3. **`histogram()`** — Histograms
4. **`densityplot()`** — Kernel density plots
5. **`barchart()`** — Bar charts
6. **`dotplot()`** — Cleveland dot plots
7. **`stripplot()`** — One-dimensional strip plots
8. **`qqmath()`** — Quantile-Quantile plots (theoretical)
9. **`qq()`** — Two-sample Q-Q plots

> [!TIP]
> For KTU board exams, the **most frequently examined** functions are `xyplot()`, `bwplot()`, `histogram()`, and `densityplot()`.

## 1.5 Mathematical Notation Conventions

In lattice formulas, the `~` (tilde) symbol is the **formula operator**:

$$y \sim x \mid \text{conditioning variable}$$

- The **left side** of `~` is the Y-variable (response).
- The **right side** of `~` is the X-variable (predictor).
- The `|` (pipe, read as "given") separates predictors from conditioning variables.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of the Lattice System

Lattice operates on a **three-tier architecture**:

### Tier 1: The Formula Interface
The formula is the heart of every lattice call. It defines *what* is plotted against *what*, and *how* the data should be split.

### Tier 2: The Conditioning Engine
This engine examines the conditioning variables and creates a **grid of panels**. Each unique combination of factor levels (or shingle intervals) gets its own panel.

### Tier 3: The Panel Function
The default panel function decides *how* to draw inside each panel (e.g., points, lines, boxplots). The user can override this with custom `panel =` functions.

## 2.2 The Formula Grammar — Full Syntax Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Frequently Tested:** Know all formula patterns below.

| Pattern | Meaning | Example |
|---------|---------|---------|
| `y ~ x` | Simple scatter | `Sepal.Length ~ Sepal.Width` |
| `y ~ x | z` | Conditional scatter, conditioned on `z` | `Length ~ Width \| Species` |
| `~ x | z` | Distribution of x by z (no y) | `~ Sepal.Length \| Species` |
| `y ~ x \| z * w` | Two conditioning variables (z crossed with w) | `y ~ x \| Species * Type` |
| `y ~ x \| z, groups = g` | Multiple series distinguished by group `g` | With `auto.key = TRUE` |

## 2.3 Core Lattice Functions — Operational Details

### 2.3.1 `xyplot()` — The Workhorse

**Syntax:**
```r
xyplot(y ~ x | condition, data = df, 
       groups = group_var,
       type = c("p", "l", "r", "g", "b", "o", "h", "s"),
       auto.key = TRUE,
       layout = c(ncol, nrow),
       scales = list(...),
       panel = panel.function)
```

**Key Arguments Explained:**

- **`type`** — A character vector controlling plot elements:
  - `"p"` → points
  - `"l"` → lines
  - `"b"` → both points and lines
  - `"r"` → regression line
  - `"g"` → grid lines
  - `"h"` → histogram-like vertical lines
  - `"s"` → stairstep plot

- **`layout`** — A numeric vector `c(ncol, nrow)` defining the panel grid.

- **`auto.key`** — A logical or list that auto-generates a legend.

### 2.3.2 `bwplot()` — Box-and-Whisker Plot

**Syntax:**
```r
bwplot(y ~ x | condition, data = df, 
       horizontal = FALSE,
       panel = panel.bwplot)
```

> [!NOTE]
> `bwplot` is preferred over base R's `boxplot` for grouped data because it natively handles the formula interface and paneling.

### 2.3.3 `histogram()` and `densityplot()`

**Syntax:**
```r
histogram(~ x | condition, data = df, 
          type = c("count", "percent", "density"),
          breaks = seq(min, max, by = width))

densityplot(~ x | condition, data = df,
            plot.points = TRUE,
            ref = TRUE)
```

> [!TIP]
> Note the **asymmetric formula** for univariate plots: `~ x` (no LHS), since there is only one variable to plot.

### 2.3.4 `barchart()` and `dotplot()`

**Syntax:**
```r
barchart(y ~ x | condition, data = df)
dotplot(y ~ x | condition, data = df)
```

### 2.3.5 `qqmath()` and `qq()`

```r
qqmath(~ x | condition, data = df, distribution = qnorm)
qq(y ~ x, data = df)  # two-sample Q-Q
```

## 2.4 Conditioning Variables — Factors vs. Shingles

| Type | Definition | Behavior | When to Use |
|------|------------|----------|-------------|
| **Factor** | Discrete categorical variable | One panel per level | Group comparisons |
| **Shingle** | Continuous variable with overlapping intervals | Multiple panels may share data | Binning continuous predictors |

**Creating a Shingle:**
```r
myshingle <- equal.count(df$x, number = 6, overlap = 0.2)
```

- `number` = number of intervals
- `overlap` = proportion of overlap between adjacent intervals

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Function | Purpose | Default Panel Function | Formula Style |
|----------|---------|------------------------|---------------|
| `xyplot()` | Scatter plot | `panel.xyplot` | `y ~ x` |
| `bwplot()` | Box plot | `panel.bwplot` | `y ~ x` |
| `histogram()` | Histogram | `panel.histogram` | `~ x` |
| `densityplot()` | Density | `panel.densityplot` | `~ x` |
| `barchart()` | Bar chart | `panel.barchart` | `y ~ x` |
| `dotplot()` | Dot plot | `panel.dotplot` | `y ~ x` |
| `stripplot()` | Strip plot | `panel.stripplot` | `~ x` |
| `qqmath()` | Q-Q plot (theoretical) | `panel.qqmath` | `~ x` |
| `qq()` | Q-Q plot (two-sample) | `panel.qq` | `y ~ x` |
| `splom()` | Scatter plot matrix | `panel.pairs` | matrix input |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In Lattice, `qplot()` is **NOT a function** (unlike in `ggplot2`). Don't confuse the two during the exam.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Engineering Domain | Lattice Use Case |
|--------------------|------------------|
| **Civil Engineering** | Comparing concrete strength across different curing temperatures and mix ratios |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Visualizing fatigue test data across multiple stress levels and materials |
| **Electrical Engineering** | Showing signal-to-noise ratios across frequency bands and circuit configurations |
| **Data Science** | Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) on multivariate datasets |
| **Quality Control** | Comparing process outputs across shifts, machines, and operators |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Examples & Code Walkthroughs

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is an **R-Programming course (PEEOT631)**, code is a *mandatory* and *integral* part of the syllabus. Every example below is fully executable in R.

## 3.1 Example 1: Scatter Plot with Conditioning (`xyplot`)

**Problem:** Using the built-in `iris` dataset, create a lattice scatter plot of `Sepal.Length` vs `Sepal.Width`, conditioned on `Species`.

**Step 1 — Load the library and inspect data:**
```r
library(lattice)
head(iris)
```

**Step 2 — Construct the lattice call:**
```r
xyplot(Sepal.Length ~ Sepal.Width | Species, 
       data = iris,
       main = "Sepal Length vs Width by Species",
       xlab = "Sepal Width (cm)",
       ylab = "Sepal Length (cm)",
       pch = 19,
       col = "blue")
```

**Step 3 — Logical analysis:**
- The formula `Sepal.Length ~ Sepal.Width | Species` means: plot Sepal.Length (Y) against Sepal.Width (X), creating one panel per Species level.
- Since `Species` has 3 levels (setosa, versicolor, virginica), Lattice auto-creates a **3-panel grid** (default 3 rows × 1 column).
- `pch = 19` produces filled circles, `col = "blue"` colors them blue.
- All panels share the **same axis ranges** automatically — this is a defining feature of lattice.

**Output Analysis:**
- Three vertically stacked scatter plots, each with a "strip" label on top showing the species name.
- Visual comparison is immediate: setosa has clearly larger widths but shorter lengths.

## 3.2 Example 2: Adding Groups and a Legend

**Problem:** Add a grouping variable to distinguish series within a single panel and create an auto-legend.

```r
# Sample data
set.seed(123)
df <- data.frame(
  x = rep(1:10, 3),
  y = c(rnorm(10, mean=5), rnorm(10, mean=7), rnorm(10, mean=9)),
  group = rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = 10)
)

xyplot(y ~ x, data = df, 
       groups = group,
       type = c("p", "l"),
       auto.key = list(title = "Group", space = "right"))
```

**Step-by-step analysis:**
- `groups = group` tells lattice to color points/lines by the `group` variable.
- `type = c("p", "l")` adds both points and lines.
- `auto.key` automatically generates a legend with the title "Group" placed on the right.
- Since there is **no conditioning variable** (no `|`), only **one panel** is drawn.

## 3.3 Example 3: Custom Panel Function

**Problem:** Add a regression line and confidence band to each panel.

```r
xyplot(Sepal.Length ~ Sepal.Width | Species, 
       data = iris,
       panel = function(x, y, ...) {
         panel.xyplot(x, y, ...)              # default scatter
         panel.lmline(x, y, col = "red", lwd = 2)  # red regression line
         panel.grid(h = -1, v = -1)          # grid lines
       })
```

**Step-by-step analysis:**
- `panel = function(x, y, ...)` defines a **custom panel function**. Lattice passes the X and Y vectors of *that panel's data* into this function.
- `panel.xyplot(x, y, ...)` is the **default scatter plotter** — we re-call it explicitly so points still appear.
- `panel.lmline(x, y, ...)` fits a **linear model** `lm(y ~ x)` and overlays the fitted line.
- `panel.grid(h = -1, v = -1)` adds grid lines at the actual data tick positions.

> [!TIP]
> The `...` (ellipsis) is **mandatory** in custom panel functions. It forwards additional graphical parameters (like `pch`, `col`) from the main call to inner panel functions.

## 3.4 Example 4: Histograms with Conditioning

```r
histogram(~ Sepal.Length | Species, 
          data = iris,
          type = "density",
          col = "lightblue",
          main = "Density of Sepal Length by Species")
```

**Analysis:**
- Formula `~ Sepal.Length | Species` — **asymmetric** (no LHS) because histograms plot a single variable.
- `type = "density"` rescales the y-axis so the area under bars equals 1, making the shape comparable to a probability density.
- Three panels, one per species.

## 3.5 Example 5: Box-and-Whisker Plot

```r
bwplot(Sepal.Length ~ Species, data = iris,
       xlab = "Species",
       ylab = "Sepal Length (cm)",
       main = "Boxplot of Sepal Length by Species",
       notch = TRUE)
```

**Analysis:**
- The formula `Sepal.Length ~ Species` treats Species as a **grouping variable on the X-axis** (no `|` conditioning needed because grouping is intrinsic to the formula).
- `notch = TRUE` adds a "notch" around the median — if notches of two boxes don't overlap, medians are likely different.

## 3.6 Example 6: Creating and Using a Shingle

```r
# Create a shingle from a continuous variable
depth_shingle <- equal.count(quakes$depth, 
                              number = 4, 
                              overlap = 0.25)

# Use it as a conditioning variable
densityplot(~ depth | depth_shingle, 
            data = quakes,
            plot.points = FALSE,
            layout = c(1, 4))
```

**Analysis:**
- `quakes` is a built-in dataset with 1000 seismic events near Fiji.
- `equal.count()` creates **4 overlapping intervals** with 25% overlap.
- `densityplot` then shows the density of `depth` within each (overlapping) shingle interval.
- `layout = c(1, 4)` arranges the 4 panels in **1 column × 4 rows**.

## 3.7 Example 7: Customizing Scales and Axes

```r
xyplot(Sepal.Length ~ Sepal.Width | Species, 
       data = iris,
       scales = list(x = "free", y = "free", 
                     cex = 0.8, 
                     relation = "same"),
       xlim = c(2, 5),
       ylim = c(4, 8))
```

**Analysis:**
- `scales` is a powerful list argument controlling axis appearance.
- `x = "free"` and `y = "free"` allow each panel to have **independent axis ranges** if needed.
- `cex = 0.8` shrinks axis label text.

## 3.8 Example 8: Multi-Conditioning (Two Variables)

```r
xyplot(Sepal.Length ~ Sepal.Width | Species * cut(iris$Petal.Length, 2), 
       data = iris,
       layout = c(2, 3))
```

**Analysis:**
- `Species * cut(Petal.Length, 2)` creates a **crossed conditioning**: 3 species × 2 Petal.Length bins = 6 panels.
- `cut(iris$Petal.Length, 2)` discretizes the continuous variable into 2 quantile-based bins.
- `layout = c(2, 3)` → 2 columns × 3 rows.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lattice System Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Input: Formula y ~ x | condition] --> B[Formula Parser]
    A --> C[Data Argument]
    A --> D[Groups Argument]
    B --> E[Conditioning Engine]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F{Shingle or Factor?}
    F -->|Factor| G[Create Panel Grid by Levels]
    F -->|Shingle| H[Create Panel Grid by Intervals]
    G --> I[Panel Function Selection]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Default Panel Function e.g. panel.xyplot]
    I --> K[Custom Panel Function from user]
    J --> L[Render Panel 1]
    J --> M[Render Panel 2]
    J --> N[Render Panel N]
    K --> L
    K --> M
    K --> N
    L --> O[Assemble Trellis Display]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Add Strips and Axes]
    P --> Q[Final Trellis Output]
```

## 4.2 Formula Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[Formula: y ~ x | z] --> P1[Parse LHS: y]
    F --> P2[Parse RHS: x]
    F --> P3[Parse Condition: z]
    P1 --> AY[Y-axis Response]
    P2 --> AX[X-axis Predictor]
    P3 --> AZ[Split Data into Panels]
    AZ --> PA[Panel 1: z = level 1]
    AZ --> PB[Panel 2: z = level 2]
    AZ --> PC[Panel N: z = level N]
    PA --> OUT[Subplot for z level 1]
    PB --> OUT
    PC --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Function Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[What data type are you plotting?] --> Q2{Numeric vs Numeric?}
    Q1 --> Q3{One variable only?}
    Q1 --> Q4{Comparing distributions?}
    Q1 --> Q5{Categorical vs Numeric?}
    Q1 --> Q6{Two samples?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| A1[Use xyplot]
    Q3 -->|Yes - histogram| A2[Use histogram]
    Q3 -->|Yes - smooth| A3[Use densityplot]
    Q4 -->|Yes| A4[Use bwplot or densityplot]
    Q5 -->|Yes| A5[Use barchart or dotplot]
    Q6 -->|Yes| A6[Use qq for two sample Q-Q]
    A1 --> OUT1[Add groups for series, type for markers]
    A2 --> OUT2[Asymmetric formula: ~ x]
    A3 --> OUT3[Asymmetric formula: ~ x]
    A4 --> OUT4[bwplot for boxplot, densityplot for curve]
    A5 --> OUT5[Formula: y ~ x]
    A6 --> OUT6[Formula: y ~ x with two-sample data]
```

## 4.4 Panel Grid Layout Example (3 species × 2 bins = 6 panels)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph GRID[Layout c equals 2 3 - Two Columns Three Rows]
        P1[Panel 1: setosa x small]
        P2[Panel 2: setosa x large]
        P3[Panel 3: versicolor x small]
        P4[Panel 4: versicolor x large]
        P5[Panel 5: virginica x small]
        P6[Panel 6: virginica x large]
    end
    P1 --- P2
    P3 --- P4
    P5 --- P6
    P1 --- P3
    P1 --- P5
    P2 --- P4
    P2 --- P6
```

## 4.5 Custom Panel Function Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Custom panel function: panel equals function x y ...] --> S1[Step 1: Call panel.xyplot for default points]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: Call panel.lmline for regression line]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Call panel.grid for grid background]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Call panel.abline for reference lines]
    S4 --> END[Render Final Panel]
    S1 -.passes x y and ....-> S1
    S2 -.passes x y and ....-> S2
    S3 -.passes x y and ....-> S3
    S4 -.passes x y and ....-> S4
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
**Q: What is the `lattice` package in R? List any four high-level functions provided by it.**

**Model Answer:**
The `lattice` package is an R implementation of Trellis graphics designed for visualizing multivariate data through conditional, multi-panel displays. It uses a formula-based interface where plots are specified as `y ~ x | condition`.

Four high-level functions are:
1. `xyplot()` — for scatter plots
2. `bwplot()` — for box-and-whisker plots
3. `histogram()` — for histograms
4. `densityplot()` — for kernel density plots

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between a `factor` and a `shingle` in lattice graphics.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Factor | Shingle |
|---------|--------|---------|
| Data type | Discrete categorical | Continuous, binned |
| Panel assignment | One panel per unique level | One panel per interval |
| Overlap | No overlap between levels | Adjacent intervals can overlap |
| Creation | `factor()` or `as.factor()` | `equal.count()` or `cofactor()` |
| Use case | Grouping by category | Conditioning on continuous variable |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake:** Students often write `qplot()` instead of `xyplot()` or `histogram()` — these are **ggplot2** functions, not lattice. This is a guaranteed loss of 1-2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the asymmetric formula** (`~ x` for univariate plots) instead of writing `x ~ 1` — lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Not explaining the `|` (pipe) operator** when conditioning is involved — lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to load the library** with `library(lattice)` at the start of code — lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing `auto.key` with `key`** — `auto.key = TRUE` is the shortcut; `key` requires a full list.

---

### **Question B1 — Choice A** (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the architecture of the Lattice graphics system in R. Describe the role of the formula interface and conditioning variables with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write R code using the `lattice` package to:
- Create a scatter plot of `mpg` vs `wt` from the `mtcars` dataset, conditioned on the number of cylinders (`cyl`).
- Add a linear regression line in red to each panel.
- Display an automatic legend indicating the cylinder count. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Architecture of Lattice Graphics:**

The Lattice graphics system is a **Trellis-style** visualization framework with three main components:

1. **Formula Interface:** Every lattice function takes a formula of the form `y ~ x | condition`. The LHS is the Y-variable, the RHS is the X-variable, and the part after `|` is the conditioning variable. For univariate plots, the formula is asymmetric: `~ x`.

2. **Conditioning Engine:** This component examines the conditioning variable(s) and splits the data into subsets. Each subset is rendered as a **panel** in a grid. If `cyl` has values 4, 6, 8, three panels are created automatically.

3. **Panel Function:** Each panel is drawn using either the default panel function (e.g., `panel.xyplot`) or a user-defined custom function. The panel function controls the appearance (points, lines, regression lines, etc.).

**Example:** `xyplot(Sepal.Length ~ Sepal.Width | Species, data = iris)` produces three scatter panels, one per species, with shared axes.

**(b) R Code:**

```r
library(lattice)

# Convert cyl to a factor for proper conditioning
mtcars$cyl <- as.factor(mtcars$cyl)

# Create conditioned scatter plot with regression line
xyplot(mpg ~ wt | cyl, 
       data = mtcars,
       main = "MPG vs Weight by Cylinder Count",
       xlab = "Weight (1000 lbs)",
       ylab = "Miles per Gallon",
       pch = 19,
       col = "blue",
       auto.key = list(title = "Cylinders", space = "right"),
       panel = function(x, y, ...) {
         panel.xyplot(x, y, ...)              # points
         panel.lmline(x, y, col = "red", lwd = 2)  # regression
         panel.grid(h = -1, v = -1)          # grid
       })
```

**Explanation of code:**
- `library(lattice)` loads the package.
- `as.factor(mtcars$cyl)` ensures `cyl` (4, 6, 8) is treated as categorical.
- `mpg ~ wt | cyl` defines the formula with conditioning on cylinder.
- `panel = function(x, y, ...)` is a custom panel function that draws points, then a red regression line, then grid lines.
- `auto.key` automatically generates a legend titled "Cylinders" on the right side.
- The output is a 3-panel grid (one per cylinder count) showing negative correlation between weight and MPG.

---

### **Question B1 — Choice B** (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** Discuss the difference between base R graphics and lattice graphics. What are the advantages of lattice over base graphics? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the `iris` dataset, write R code with the `lattice` package to:
- Create side-by-side boxplots of `Petal.Length` for each species.
- Create a histogram of `Sepal.Width` conditioned on `Species` with density overlay. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Base R vs Lattice Graphics:**

| Feature | Base R Graphics | Lattice Graphics |
|---------|----------------|------------------|
| Interface | Imperative (state each step) | Declarative (one formula call) |
| Conditioning | Manual loop required | Automatic via `\|` |
| Multiple panels | Must use `par(mfrow)` etc. | Built-in paneling |
| Default appearance | Plain | Polished, publication-ready |
| Annotation | Manual | Auto-strips, auto-axes |
| Customization | Highly flexible but verbose | More constrained but simpler |
| Memory | Faster for simple plots | Better for complex multivariate plots |

**Advantages of Lattice:**
1. Easy conditioning on multiple variables (`| z * w`).
2. Automatic consistent axes across panels.
3. Compact code for complex multi-panel displays.
4. Built-in shingles for continuous conditioning.
5. Publication-quality output by default.

**(b) R Code:**

```r
library(lattice)

# (i) Side-by-side boxplots of Petal.Length by Species
bwplot(Petal.Length ~ Species, data = iris,
       main = "Boxplot of Petal Length by Species",
       xlab = "Species",
       ylab = "Petal Length (cm)",
       col = "lightgreen",
       notch = TRUE)

# (ii) Histogram of Sepal.Width conditioned on Species with density overlay
histogram(~ Sepal.Width | Species, 
          data = iris,
          type = "density",
          col = "lightblue",
          main = "Density of Sepal Width by Species",
          xlab = "Sepal Width (cm)",
          panel = function(x, ...) {
            panel.histogram(x, ...)
            panel.densityplot(x, darg = list(bw = 0.2), 
                              col = "red", lwd = 2)
          })
```

**Explanation:**
- `bwplot(Petal.Length ~ Species, ...)` uses the X-axis for the categorical `Species` and Y-axis for the continuous `Petal.Length`. The formula is **asymmetric** in interpretation: categorical variable on the right of `~`.
- `notch = TRUE` adds notches around medians to compare them visually.
- `histogram(~ Sepal.Width | Species, ...)` creates three panels of histograms, one per species.
- `type = "density"` rescales the y-axis to represent probability density.
- The custom `panel` function overlays a red kernel density curve on top of each histogram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!**

### Quick Reference Points

- **Lattice = Trellis graphics in R**; load with `library(lattice)`.
- **Formula syntax:** `y ~ x | condition` (Y on LHS, X on RHS, condition after `|`).
- **Asymmetric formula** for univariate plots: `~ x | condition`.
- **Crossed conditioning:** `y ~ x | z * w` (z crossed with w).
- **9 core functions:** `xyplot`, `bwplot`, `histogram`, `densityplot`, `barchart`, `dotplot`, `stripplot`, `qqmath`, `qq`.
- **`type` argument values:** `"p"` (points), `"l"` (lines), `"b"` (both), `"r"` (regression), `"g"` (grid), `"h"` (histogram-like), `"s"` (stairstep).
- **Shingles:** created via `equal.count(x, number = n, overlap = p)`; used for continuous conditioning.
- **Custom panel:** `panel = function(x, y, ...) { panel.xyplot(x, y, ...); panel.lmline(x, y, ...) }`.
- **Legend:** `auto.key = TRUE` for a quick legend; `auto.key = list(title = ..., space = "right")` for customization.
- **Layout:** `layout = c(ncol, nrow)` to arrange panels in a grid.
- **Scales:** `scales = list(x = "free", y = "free")` for independent axis ranges.
- **Groups:** `groups = variable` to distinguish series within a panel.
- **Lattice vs ggplot2:** Do NOT confuse — lattice uses `xyplot`, not `qplot` or `geom_point`.

### Most Common Exam Traps

1. Writing `qplot()` instead of `xyplot()` — **WRONG**, this is a ggplot2 function.
2. Forgetting `library(lattice)` at the top of code.
3. Using symmetric formula `x ~ 1` for univariate plots — use `~ x` instead.
4. Not interpreting the `|` operator as "given" or "conditioned on".
5. Confusing `bwplot` (lattice) with `boxplot` (base R).
6. Forgetting to convert numeric conditioning variables to factors for clean paneling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** For a 14-mark question, always include: **(i)** the theory behind the function, **(ii)** the complete R code with proper syntax, **(iii)** an explanation of the output, and **(iv)** at least one custom panel modification if applicable. This structure covers all CO (Course Outcome) levels from L1 (Remember) to L4 (Analyze).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
