topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT631 - R-PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Customizing Lattice Graphics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Lattice Graphics** is an advanced, high-level data visualization system in R, formally inspired by the *Trellis Graphics* paradigm developed by William S. Cleveland at Bell Labs. The Lattice package, authored by **Deepayan Sarkar**, provides a framework for creating *conditioned* multi-panel plots that are governed by the principles of "small multiples" — a structured grid of plots where each panel shows a different subset of the data, making complex multivariate relationships easy to compare.

**Customizing Lattice Graphics** refers to the systematic modification of the *default visual vocabulary* of lattice — i.e., the symbols, lines, colors, fonts, axes, strips, and legends — by leveraging:

1. **Custom Panel Functions** (`panel.function` arguments)
2. **Graphical Parameter Overrides** (`trellis.par.set()`, `par.settings`)
3. **Custom Strip Functions** (for panel headers/labels)
4. **Axis Component Customization** (`axis.components`, `scales`)
5. **Arbitrary Annotations & Geometries** (`panel.text`, `panel.abline`, `panel.segments`, `panel.polygon`, `panel.arrows`)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:**
> The default `lattice` plot is a *Trellis object* (a `trellis` class list) — NOT a base R plot. Customization therefore operates on **components of an S4-style object**, which is a high-weight concept in board examinations.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you walk into a **custom car showroom**. The base model (a standard lattice plot) leaves the factory with default paint, default upholstery, and a standard dashboard. *Customizing* the lattice graphics is like:

- **Painting the body** → Modifying `trellis.par.set()` (changing colors, line types, plotting symbols).
- **Replacing the dashboard** → Writing a **custom panel function** (deciding exactly what shapes, lines, or text appear inside every panel).
- **Renaming the panel labels** → Crafting a **custom strip function** (controlling how the conditioning variable labels appear above each panel).
- **Adding neon underglow** → Overlaying **arbitrary annotations** like `panel.abline`, `panel.text`, or `panel.segments` for reference marks and annotations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this so important?**
> Default lattice plots are *publication-naïve* — they look acceptable for exploration but are rarely publication-ready. Customization is the bridge between *raw statistical output* and *Kerala University / IEEE-quality figures*.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Defaults (Must Memorize)

| Default Element | Standard Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `plot.symbol` | `pch = 16` (filled circle) | Default point marker |
| `plot.line` | `lwd = 1` | Default line width |
| `add.line` | `lwd = 1, lty = 3` | Reference line style |
| `background` | `"white"` | Panel background |
| `par.strip.text` | Bold font | Strip label default |
| `axis.text` | `cex = 0.8` | Axis tick label size |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Lattice Customization

### Pillar 1: `trellis.par.set()` — *Theme Engineering*
This is the **lowest level** of customization. It modifies the *global* theme settings that control the look of subsequent lattice plots until the session is reset.

**Operational Logic:**
- `trellis.par.get()` → Returns the current settings as a list.
- `trellis.par.set(name = value)` → Modifies a setting in the current device.

The `name` argument is a **dotted name** like `plot.symbol`, `plot.line`, `add.text`, `axis.text`, `par.main.text`, etc. Each name corresponds to a structured `gpar` object containing graphical parameters.

### Pillar 2: Custom Panel Functions — *Logic Engineering*
Every high-level lattice function (`xyplot`, `bwplot`, `densityplot`, `histogram`, etc.) accepts a `panel` argument. The default value (e.g., `panel.xyplot`) is itself a function. To customize, you write a function with **two mandatory arguments**:

```r
panel.custom <- function(x, y, ...) {
   # your code
}
```

- `x, y` → The data for the current panel subset.
- `...` → Other arguments (subscripts, groups, etc.) forwarded by the high-level call.

Inside the function, you typically call **panel helper functions** like `panel.xyplot()`, `panel.grid()`, `panel.abline()`, `panel.loess()`, `panel.text()`.

> [!TIP]
> **The Golden Rule:** Always call `panel.grid()` or other helpers *first*, then your custom additions. The **order of calls determines the drawing order** (z-axis of the plot).

### Pillar 3: `par.settings` — *Scoped Engineering*
Unlike `trellis.par.set()` (global), `par.settings` is passed **directly to a single high-level call**. It is *scoped* to that plot only. This is the *preferred* approach in reports and functions.

**Syntax:**
```r
xyplot(y ~ x | g, data = d,
       par.settings = list(plot.symbol = list(pch = 17, col = "red")))
```

## 2.2 Custom Strip Functions

The *strip* is the small header box above each panel that displays the level of the conditioning variable. Customization is done via the `strip` argument:

```r
strip = strip.custom(
   factor.labels = c("Group A", "Group B"),
   bg = "lightblue",
   par.strip.text = list(col = "white", font = 2)
)
```

> [!NOTE]
> `font = 2` is the standard code for **bold** in R base graphics.

## 2.3 Custom Axis Components

`axis.components` (passed inside `par.settings`) controls the four sides of every panel's axes. Common sub-components:
- `bottom` (x-axis ticks/labels)
- `left` (y-axis ticks/labels)
- `top`, `right` (rarely used)

Each side accepts a list specifying `ticks`, `at` (positions), `labels`, and `components`.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Function Cheat Sheet

| Function | Purpose | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| `trellis.par.get()` | Retrieve current lattice theme | Inspect defaults |
| `trellis.par.set()` | Modify global theme | Session-wide changes |
| `par.settings = list(...)` | Per-plot theme | Best practice (scoped) |
| `panel = function(x, y, ...)` | Custom panel logic | Conditional overlays |
| `panel.abline(a, b, ...)` | Add straight reference line | Mean/median lines |
| `panel.segments(x1, y1, x2, y2, ...)` | Draw line segments | Connect pairs |
| `panel.text(x, y, labels, ...)` | Add text annotations | Label outliers |
| `panel.arrows(x0, y0, x1, y1, ...)` | Add directional arrows | Vector fields |
| `panel.polygon(x, y, ...)` | Draw filled polygon | Shaded confidence bands |
| `panel.rect(x1, y1, x2, y2, ...)` | Draw rectangles | Highlight regions |
| `panel.grid(h, v, ...)` | Draw grid lines | Background grid |
| `panel.loess(x, y, ...)` | Add LOESS smoother | Trend line |
| `panel.lmline(x, y, ...)` | Add linear fit | Regression line |
| `panel.curve(expr, from, to, ...)` | Plot a function | f(x) overlays |
| `panel.mathdensity(dfun, ...)` | Theoretical density | Normal overlay |
| `strip = strip.custom(...)` | Custom panel headers | Renaming groups |
| `axis.components = list(...)` | Custom axis components | Tick formatting |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **engineering statistics** (a common KTU application), customizing lattice graphics is essential for:

- **Quality Control**: Overlaying specification limits (`panel.abline`) on Shewhart control charts.
- **Signal Processing**: Showing confidence bands (`panel.polygon`) around a smoothed spectrum.
- **Reliability Analysis**: Marking failure points with custom symbols and labels.
- **Comparative Studies**: Using `par.settings` to enforce a corporate-style theme across all figures in a thesis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs

> [!NOTE]
> Since the topic is *R Programming*, this section replaces algebraic derivations with **exhaustive code walkthroughs**. Each line of R code is annotated, mirroring the depth required in derivations.

## 3.1 Walkthrough A: Modifying Point Symbols & Colors with `par.settings`

**Problem:** Plot Sepal.Length vs Sepal.Width from the `iris` dataset, conditioned by Species. Mark each species with a different symbol and color using `par.settings` (no `trellis.par.set()`).

**Step 1 — Load the library and inspect data:**

```r
library(lattice)
data(iris)
head(iris, 3)
```

**Step 2 — Call `xyplot()` with `par.settings`:**

```r
xyplot(Sepal.Length ~ Sepal.Width | Species,
       data = iris,
       par.settings = list(
         plot.symbol = list(pch = 17,        # 17 = filled triangle
                            col = "darkred",
                            cex = 1.2),
         plot.line   = list(col = "blue",
                            lwd = 2)
       ),
       main = "Iris: Petal Geometry by Species")
```

**Step 3 — Verify the customization:**

The output will be a 1×3 grid of panels. Each panel contains **dark-red filled triangles** (instead of the default black filled circles). The *global* lattice theme is *untouched*, so any other plot called after this one will still use defaults.

**Why this works:**

$$
\text{par.settings} = \text{list}\big( \text{plot.symbol} = \text{list}(pch=17, col, cex) \big)
$$

The dotted name `plot.symbol` traverses the `gpar` hierarchy stored inside the lattice theme object. By overriding only the *leaf node* (`pch`, `col`, `cex`), other defaults (like `lwd` for line width) remain untouched.

## 3.2 Walkthrough B: Writing a Custom Panel Function with `panel.grid`, `panel.xyplot`, and `panel.abline`

**Problem:** Add a horizontal reference line (the overall mean of y) and a vertical reference line (the overall mean of x) to every panel of a conditioned scatterplot.

**Step 1 — Build the conditioned plot:**

```r
xyplot(yield ~ temp | catalyst,
       data = chemical_experiment,
       panel = function(x, y, ...) {
         # Step 1.1: Draw a faint background grid
         panel.grid(h = -1, v = -1, col = "grey80", lty = 3)

         # Step 1.2: Draw the default scatter points
         panel.xyplot(x, y, ...)

         # Step 1.3: Add a horizontal line at the mean of y
         panel.abline(h = mean(y),
                      lty = 2,        # dashed
                      col = "red",
                      lwd = 2)

         # Step 1.4: Add a vertical line at the mean of x
         panel.abline(v = mean(x),
                      lty = 2,
                      col = "blue",
                      lwd = 2)
       })
```

**Step 2 — Step-by-step logic:**

$$
\text{Custom panel} = f(x, y, \ldots) \rightarrow \big[ \text{grid} \rightarrow \text{points} \rightarrow \text{ref-lines} \big]
$$

The drawing order is **bottom-to-top** (z-order). If we had called `panel.xyplot` *before* `panel.grid`, the grid would overlay the points. The convention in R is *background first, then foreground overlays*.

**Step 3 — Argument forwarding (`...`):**

The `...` argument in the custom panel is **not optional**: it captures forwarding parameters like `groups`, `subscripts`, `col`, `pch` from the high-level call. Without it, your customization will *break* the original behavior.

## 3.3 Walkthrough C: Adding a Shaded Confidence Band with `panel.polygon`

**Problem:** Visualize uncertainty around a LOESS smoother using a 95% confidence band.

```r
xyplot(pressure ~ temperature, data = pressure,
       panel = function(x, y, ...) {
         # Step 1: Fit LOESS smoother and get standard errors
         fit    <- loess(y ~ x)
         pred   <- predict(fit, se = TRUE)
         yhat   <- pred$fit
         se     <- pred$se.fit

         # Step 2: Build a polygon spanning ±1.96*SE
         xx     <- c(x, rev(x))
         yy     <- c(yhat - 1.96 * se, rev(yhat + 1.96 * se))

         # Step 3: Draw the polygon FIRST (background)
         panel.polygon(xx, yy,
                       col = "lightyellow",
                       border = NA)

         # Step 4: Draw grid
         panel.grid()

         # Step 5: Draw the smoother curve
         panel.lines(x, yhat, col = "red", lwd = 2)

         # Step 6: Draw original points ON TOP
         panel.xyplot(x, y, ...)
       })
```

**Analytical step-by-step:**

$$
y_{\text{lower}}(x) = \hat{f}(x) - 1.96 \cdot \widehat{SE}(x)
$$

$$
y_{\text{upper}}(x) = \hat{f}(x) + 1.96 \cdot \widehat{SE}(x)
$$

$$
\text{polygon} = \big\{ (x, y_{\text{lower}}), (x_{\text{rev}}, y_{\text{upper}}) \big\}
$$

The polygon is closed by *reversing* the x-coordinates, so the first half is traced left-to-right along the lower bound, and the second half is traced right-to-left along the upper bound — forming a continuous closed shape.

## 3.4 Walkthrough D: Custom Strip Function

**Problem:** Replace the automatic conditioning labels with custom text and change the strip's background color.

```r
xyplot(mpg ~ wt | cyl,
       data = mtcars,
       strip = strip.custom(
         factor.labels = c("4-Cylinder",
                            "6-Cylinder",
                            "8-Cylinder"),
         bg = c("lightblue", "lightgreen", "lightpink"),
         par.strip.text = list(col = "black",
                               font = 2,     # bold
                               cex = 1.1)
       ))
```

**Why `factor.labels` works:**

Lattice internally stores conditioning levels as a factor. `strip.custom` reads those factor levels in their natural order. The vector `c("4-Cylinder", "6-Cylinder", "8-Cylinder")` is recycled 1-to-1 with the levels. If you provide *fewer* names than levels, R will recycle and **issue a warning** (commonly lost marks in the KTU exam).

## 3.5 Walkthrough E: Combining `trellis.par.set()` with Custom Panels

```r
# Step 1: Modify the GLOBAL theme
trellis.par.set(
  plot.symbol  = list(pch = 19, col = "purple", cex = 0.9),
  plot.line    = list(col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2),
  background   = list(col = "ivory"),
  par.strip.text = list(col = "white", font = 2),
  strip.background = list(col = "grey30")
)

# Step 2: Plot — the new theme is now applied
xyplot(Sepal.Length ~ Sepal.Width | Species, data = iris)

# Step 3: Reset if needed
trellis.par.set(theme = standard.theme())
```

**Important contrast:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{trellis.par.set()} &\rightarrow \text{GLOBAL, side-effect, lasts session} \\
\text{par.settings}     &\rightarrow \text{LOCAL, scoped, only this plot} \\
\end{aligned}
}
$$

This is one of the most commonly asked *difference* questions in the KTU viva and 3-mark section.

## 3.6 Walkthrough F: Plotting a Mathematical Function with `panel.curve`

```r
xyplot(1:100 ~ 1:100,
       xlab = "x", ylab = "f(x)",
       panel = function(x, y, ...) {
         panel.grid()
         panel.curve(0.05 * x^2 - 2 * x + 5,
                     from = 0, to = 100,
                     col = "red", lwd = 2)
         panel.curve(sin(x / 10) * 30 + 50,
                     from = 0, to = 100,
                     col = "blue", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
       })
```

The `panel.curve` function accepts an *unevaluated expression* as its first argument and evaluates it on the fly. This is the lattice equivalent of `curve()` in base R, but with proper axis alignment.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Customization Hierarchy in Lattice

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lattice Customization Entry Point] --> B{Global or Scoped?}
    B -- Global Session-wide --> C1[trellis.par.set]
    B -- Scoped Single plot --> C2[par.settings argument]
    C1 --> D1[Modify gpar objects via dotted names]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2[plot.symbol]
    D1 --> D3[plot.line]
    D1 --> D4[add.line]
    D1 --> D5[par.strip.text]
    D1 --> D6[axis.text]
    A --> E[Custom Panel Function]
    E --> F1[panel.grid]
    E --> F2[panel.xyplot]
    E --> F3[panel.abline]
    E --> F4[panel.text]
    E --> F5[panel.segments]
    E --> F6[panel.polygon]
    E --> F7[panel.loess]
    A --> G[strip argument]
    G --> H1[strip.custom]
    H1 --> I1[factor.labels]
    H1 --> I2[bg]
    H1 --> I3[par.strip.text]
    A --> J[axis.components]
    J --> K1[bottom]
    J --> K2[left]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of a Custom Panel Execution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[High-level call e.g. xyplot] --> B0[Data split by conditioning]
    B0 --> C0[For each panel subset]
    C0 --> D0[Invoke custom panel function with x y and ellipsis]
    D0 --> E0[Step 1 Draw background grid]
    D0 --> E1[Step 2 Draw data points]
    D0 --> E2[Step 3 Add reference lines]
    D0 --> E3[Step 4 Add text annotations]
    D0 --> E4[Step 5 Add shaded regions]
    E0 --> F0[Render panel to device]
    E1 --> F0
    E2 --> F0
    E3 --> F0
    E4 --> F0
    F0 --> G0[Repeat for next panel]
    G0 --> C0
```

## 4.3 Architecture: Dotted-Name Resolution in `trellis.par.set`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0["trellis.par.set plot.symbol pch = 19"] --> B0[Parse dotted name]
    B0 --> C0[Traverse nested list in theme]
    C0 --> D0[Locate plot.symbol node]
    D0 --> E0[Replace or merge gpar entries]
    E0 --> F0[Update in-memory theme object]
    F0 --> G0[Subsequent plots use new theme]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing `trellis.par.set()` and `par.settings`** — most common 3-mark error. Remember: the former is *global*, the latter is *local*.
> 2. **Forgetting `...` in the custom panel** — the high-level call will *silently* fail to pass groups, subscripts, and styling, producing empty plots.
> 3. **Wrong drawing order** — putting `panel.grid` *after* `panel.xyplot` will hide your data points.
> 4. **Using `pch = 0` to `pch = 25`** — students often write `pch = "triangle"`, which is base R only (not lattice). Use *numeric* codes in lattice.
> 5. **Strip custom with mismatched labels** — `length(factor.labels)` must equal the number of conditioning levels, or R recycles with a warning (often penalized).

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Differentiate between `trellis.par.set()` and `par.settings` in lattice graphics. When would you prefer one over the other?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `trellis.par.set()` | `par.settings` |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Global — affects all subsequent lattice plots in the session | Local — affects only the single plot being drawn |
| **Side effects** | Yes, mutates the active theme object | No, scoped to the call |
| **Preferred use** | One-time setup in scripts, demonstrations | Reproducible reports, functions, RMarkdown documents |
| **Reversibility** | Manual reset using `trellis.par.set(theme = standard.theme())` | Automatic — leaves no trace |

**Prefer `par.settings`** in RMarkdown / Shiny / production code where reproducibility matters. **Prefer `trellis.par.set()`** in interactive exploratory sessions.

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the role of the `panel` argument in lattice high-level functions. What is the significance of the `...` argument inside a custom panel function?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `panel` argument accepts a function `(x, y, ...)` that controls what is drawn inside each conditioning panel. It replaces the default behavior (e.g., `panel.xyplot`) and allows the user to layer multiple `panel.*` helpers — `panel.grid`, `panel.abline`, `panel.text`, etc.

The `...` argument is **mandatory**: it captures the auxiliary parameters forwarded by the high-level call (such as `groups`, `subscripts`, `col`, `pch`, `lty`). Without it, the custom panel cannot honor these arguments and may produce incomplete plots (e.g., a grouped plot where only one group is drawn).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Write R code to construct a conditioned scatterplot of `Sepal.Length` vs `Sepal.Width` from the `iris` dataset, with `Species` as the conditioning variable. Customize the plot using `par.settings` so that:
- Points are **filled red triangles** (`pch = 17`).
- A **blue dashed regression line** is drawn in each panel.
- Panel strip backgrounds are **light grey**.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write R code to add to the plot in (a):
- A **horizontal reference line** at the global mean of `Sepal.Length`.
- A **text annotation** "Mean" at the right edge of each panel.
- A **shaded rectangle** covering the region where `Sepal.Width` lies between 2.5 and 3.5.

**(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Solution (7 marks):**

```r
library(lattice)
data(iris)

# Compute global mean for later use
global_mean <- mean(iris$Sepal.Length)

xyplot(Sepal.Length ~ Sepal.Width | Species,
       data = iris,
       par.settings = list(
         plot.symbol = list(pch = 17,        # filled triangle
                            col = "red",
                            cex = 1.1),
         plot.line   = list(col = "blue",
                            lwd = 2,
                            lty = 2),       # dashed
         strip.background = list(col = "lightgrey")
       ),
       panel = function(x, y, ...) {
         panel.grid(h = -1, v = -1, col = "grey85", lty = 3)
         panel.xyplot(x, y, ...)
         panel.lmline(x, y, col = "blue", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
       },
       main = "Iris — Customized Lattice Plot (a)")
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**
- `par.settings` overrides only the **gpar leaves** `pch`, `col`, `cex` under `plot.symbol`, and the line styling under `plot.line`.
- `panel.lmline` is the lattice helper that fits a simple linear regression in each panel and draws the line.
- `strip.background` paints the strip header boxes.
- `panel.grid` is drawn *first*, then the data — preserving z-order.

**Part (b) — Solution (7 marks):**

```r
xyplot(Sepal.Length ~ Sepal.Width | Species,
       data = iris,
       par.settings = list(
         plot.symbol = list(pch = 17, col = "red", cex = 1.1),
         strip.background = list(col = "lightgrey")
       ),
       panel = function(x, y, ...) {
         # (i) Background grid
         panel.grid(h = -1, v = -1, col = "grey85", lty = 3)

         # (ii) Shaded rectangle for 2.5 < Sepal.Width < 3.5
         panel.rect(xleft = 2.5, ybottom = -Inf,
                    xright = 3.5, ytop = Inf,
                    col = "lightyellow", border = NA)

         # (iii) Horizontal reference line at global mean
         panel.abline(h = global_mean,
                      col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2, lty = 2)

         # (iv) Scatter points
         panel.xyplot(x, y, ...)

         # (v) 'Mean' text annotation on the right edge
         panel.text(x = max(x),                       # rightmost x
                    y = global_mean,
                    labels = "Mean",
                    pos = 4,                          # right of point
                    col = "darkgreen", font = 2)
       },
       main = "Iris — Customized Lattice Plot (b)")
```

**Analytical notes:**
- The shaded rectangle uses `panel.rect` with `ybottom = -Inf` and `ytop = Inf` so it spans the *entire vertical extent* of the panel regardless of data range.
- The text annotation uses `panel.text` with `pos = 4` (to the right of the coordinates), placing "Mean" just *outside* the data area, near the right axis.
- The global mean is computed *once* outside the panel function to avoid recomputation per panel.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative]

**(a)** Explain the concept of a **custom strip function** in lattice graphics. Write R code to rename the conditioning labels of an `xyplot(mpg ~ wt | cyl, data = mtcars)` plot to *"4-Cylinder", "6-Cylinder", "8-Cylinder"* and color the strip backgrounds as lightblue, lightgreen, and lightpink respectively. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Demonstrate with R code how `trellis.par.set()` can be used to change the **global theme** such that all subsequent lattice plots display points as **purple filled circles of size 1.2**, lines as **darkgreen with width 2**, and panel backgrounds as **ivory**. Show the R code to reset the theme. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Solution (7 marks):**

```r
library(lattice)
data(mtcars)
mtcars$cyl <- factor(mtcars$cyl)

xyplot(mpg ~ wt | cyl,
       data = mtcars,
       strip = strip.custom(
         factor.labels = c("4-Cylinder",
                            "6-Cylinder",
                            "8-Cylinder"),
         bg = c("lightblue",
                "lightgreen",
                "lightpink"),
         par.strip.text = list(col = "black",
                               font = 2,        # bold
                               cex = 1.1)
       ),
       main = "MPG vs Weight by Cylinder Count")
```

**Explanation:**
- `factor.labels` overrides the default strip text (which would be `"4"`, `"6"`, `"8"`).
- `bg` accepts a *vector* of colors, one per level — recycled in the order of the factor.
- `par.strip.text` controls font properties of the strip text *inside* the strip box.

**Part (b) — Solution (7 marks):**

```r
# (i) Modify the GLOBAL lattice theme
trellis.par.set(
  plot.symbol  = list(pch = 19,        # filled circle
                      col = "purple",
                      cex = 1.2),
  plot.line    = list(col = "darkgreen",
                      lwd = 2),
  background   = list(col = "ivory")
)

# (ii) Verify the change
show.settings()         # opens a diagnostic display

# (iii) Draw a plot — new theme is in effect
xyplot(mpg ~ wt | cyl, data = mtcars)

# (iv) Reset to defaults
trellis.par.set(theme = standard.theme())
```

**Explanation:**
- `trellis.par.set(name = list(...))` walks the dotted name path inside the global theme object.
- `standard.theme()` is a function that returns R's default lattice theme.
- Calling `trellis.par.set(theme = ...)` *replaces* the entire current theme — this is the cleanest way to reset.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Lattice** = Trellis-style conditioned multi-panel graphics in R.
- **`trellis.par.set()`** = global theme modifier; `**par.settings**` = per-plot modifier — *never confuse them*.
- **Custom panel function** = `panel = function(x, y, ...) { ... }` — `...` is **not optional**.
- **Drawing order** = `panel.grid` → `panel.xyplot` → `panel.abline` → `panel.text` (background first).
- **`panel.abline`** adds reference lines (use `h =` or `v =`).
- **`panel.segments`** draws line segments between pairs of coordinates.
- **`panel.text`** adds text annotations with a `labels` argument and `pos` for positioning.
- **`panel.rect`** draws filled rectangles (use `col =` and `border = NA` to suppress outline).
- **`panel.polygon`** draws filled polygons (used for confidence bands).
- **`panel.curve(expr, from, to)`** plots a mathematical function overlay.
- **`strip.custom`** controls strip text, background colors, and fonts.
- **`axis.components`** inside `par.settings` allows per-side axis customization.
- **`show.settings()`** displays the current lattice theme as a reference grid — *very useful for viva*.
- **Reset theme** with `trellis.par.set(theme = standard.theme())`.
- For **board answers**, always show: (1) the data split concept, (2) the panel function body, (3) the explanation of z-order, (4) at least one example of `par.settings`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ggplot2
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT631 - R-PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ggplot2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is ggplot2?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> `ggplot2` is an open-source, declarative, and grammar-of-graphics-based data visualization package for the R programming language, developed by **Hadley Wickham** as an implementation of Leland Wilkinson's *Grammar of Graphics*. It builds plots through the incremental addition of independent layers (data, aesthetics, geometric objects, statistical transformations, scales, coordinates, facets, and themes).

It is the most widely used extension package of the `tidyverse` suite and forms the **de-facto standard** for producing publication-quality static graphics in R. Unlike base R plotting (`plot()`, `hist()`, `boxplot()`), which paints pixel-by-pixel, `ggplot2` allows the user to declare **what** the plot should look like rather than **how** to draw it line-by-line.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Layered Sandwich" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:**
> Think of building a `ggplot2` plot exactly like preparing a **layered sandwich** at a Subway counter:
>
> 1. **The Plate (Data Layer):** You pick the bread base — this is your raw data frame.
> 2. **The Sauce & Spreads (Aesthetic Mappings - `aes()`):** You decide *which* variables map to the x-axis, y-axis, color, size, or shape. This is invisible until you actually add the topping.
> 3. **The Toppings (Geometric Objects - `geom_*()`):** You add cheese, olives, or lettuce — these are your bars, points, or lines.
> 4. **The Cooking Style (Statistical Transformations - `stat_*()`):** You may want the cheese melted (smoothing) or the veggies chopped (binning). These are your statistical summaries.
> 5. **The Coordinate System (Scales & Coordinates):** You serve it on a square plate or a round one — this controls how the axes are drawn (linear, log, polar).
> 6. **The Restaurant Layout (Facets - `facet_*()`):** You may want to repeat the same sandwich for each customer — this splits the plot into small multiples.
> 7. **The Decor (Themes - `theme_*()`):** The background color, font, grid lines, and borders — the visual styling.

## 1.3 The Grammar of Graphics (Foundational Philosophy)

Wilkinson's Grammar of Graphics (2005) treats every statistical graph as a composition of **independent grammatical components**. `ggplot2` faithfully implements this by allowing components to be combined using the `+` operator.

| Grammar Component | `ggplot2` Function Family | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Data | `ggplot(data = df)` | The raw dataset |
| Aesthetic Mappings | `aes(x, y, ...)` | Variable-to-visual mapping |
| Geometric Object | `geom_point()`, `geom_bar()` | Type of plot (point, bar, line) |
| Statistical Transform | `stat_smooth()`, `stat_summary()` | Compute summary statistics |
| Scales | `scale_x_continuous()`, `scale_color_manual()` | Control axis/legend mapping |
| Coordinate System | `coord_flip()`, `coord_polar()` | Cartesian, polar, flipped axes |
| Facets | `facet_wrap()`, `facet_grid()` | Small multiples / paneling |
| Theme | `theme_minimal()`, `theme()` | Non-data visual styling |

## 1.4 Installation & Loading

```r
# Install once
install.packages("ggplot2")

# Load in every session
library(ggplot2)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Note:** The package name on CRAN is `ggplot2`, but it is *always* loaded as `library(ggplot2)`. The primary function is written exactly as `ggplot()` (no capital G, no underscore). This is one of the most common syntax errors reported in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Mandatory Components of Every ggplot

A complete `ggplot2` call must include (at minimum) these three elements:

1. **`ggplot(data = dataframe, aes(x = var1, y = var2))`** — Initializes the plot canvas and defines global aesthetic mappings. If aesthetics are placed here, they are **inherited by every layer** that follows.
2. **`geom_*(...)`** — Adds at least one geometric layer (e.g., `geom_point()`, `geom_line()`, `geom_col()`). Without a geom, the plot is **blank/empty**.
3. **Aesthetic arguments inside `aes()`** — Variables must be wrapped in `aes()` to be mapped to visual properties. Arguments *outside* `aes()` are fixed static properties.

## 2.2 Aesthetic Mappings — `aes()`

> [!NOTE]
> The `aes()` function (aesthetic mapping) is the **soul** of `ggplot2`. It tells the plot: *"this variable should drive that visual property."*

### 2.2.1 Common Aesthetic Arguments

| Argument | Visual Property Controlled | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `x` | Horizontal position | `aes(x = mpg)` |
| `y` | Vertical position | `aes(y = horsepower)` |
| `color` / `colour` | Border/outline color (continuous or discrete) | `aes(color = cyl)` |
| `fill` | Interior fill color | `aes(fill = gear)` |
| `size` | Point size or line thickness | `aes(size = wt)` |
| `shape` | Point shape (discrete) | `aes(shape = factor(am))` |
| `alpha` | Transparency (0 to 1) | `aes(alpha = qsec)` |
| `linetype` | Solid/dashed/dotted line | `aes(linetype = vs)` |
| `group` | Grouping for lines/paths | `aes(group = Subject)` |

### 2.2.2 The `aes()` vs Outside-`aes()` Rule

```r
# CORRECT: Mapping a variable to color (inside aes)
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point()

# CORRECT: Fixing color to a constant (outside aes)
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 3, shape = 17)

# WRONG: Forcing a constant inside aes() creates a legend
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = "blue")) + geom_point()
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Examiners frequently deduct **2 marks** when students write `color = "blue"` *inside* `aes()` thinking it will color the points blue. Instead, it treats the string as a categorical variable and creates a useless legend titled "blue".

## 2.3 Geometric Objects (Geoms) — The Workhorses

Each `geom_*` function takes a dataset (defaults to the global data) and renders a specific shape. They are the **only** layer that actually draws marks on the canvas.

| Geom | Plot Type | Default Stat |
|---|---|---|
| `geom_point()` | Scatter plot | identity |
| `geom_line()` | Line plot (ordered by x) | identity |
| `geom_path()` | Line plot (in row order) | identity |
| `geom_bar()` | Bar chart (counts) | count |
| `geom_col()` | Bar chart (values supplied) | identity |
| `geom_histogram()` | Histogram | bin |
| `geom_density()` | Density / KDE plot | density |
| `geom_boxplot()` | Box-and-whisker plot | boxplot |
| `geom_violin()` | Violin plot | ydensity |
| `geom_smooth()` | Fitted regression line | smooth |
| `geom_text()` / `geom_label()` | Text annotations | identity |
| `geom_jitter()` | Jittered points (avoid overplotting) | identity |
| `geom_area()` | Area chart | identity |
| `geom_tile()` / `geom_raster()` | Heatmap tiles | identity |

## 2.4 Statistical Transformations — `stat_*()`

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In `ggplot2`, every geom has an associated **stat** (statistical transformation), and every stat has an associated geom. You can use either spelling. For example, `geom_histogram()` is equivalent to `stat_bin() + geom_bar()`.

The `stat_smooth()` function fits a smooth line, defaulting to LOESS (for n < 1000) or GAM (for n ≥ 1000). The `method` argument allows you to force a specific model:

```r
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "red")
```

The `se = TRUE` argument adds a **shaded confidence interval** band (95% by default).

## 2.5 Scales — Controlling the Axes & Legends

Scales translate data values into visual values. The naming convention is `scale_<aesthetic>_<type>()`.

| Scale Function | Effect |
|---|---|
| `scale_x_continuous()` | Numeric continuous x-axis |
| `scale_x_log10()` | Log10-transformed x-axis |
| `scale_x_discrete()` | Discrete/factor x-axis |
| `scale_y_reverse()` | Reversed y-axis |
| `scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1")` | ColorBrewer qualitative palette |
| `scale_color_manual(values = c("red","blue"))` | Manual color choice |
| `scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "red")` | Continuous fill gradient |
| `scale_size_continuous(range = c(1, 10))` | Point/line size range |
| `labs(title = "...", x = "...", y = "...")` | Axis labels and title |

## 2.6 Facets — Small Multiples

Facets create **separate panels** of the same plot for different subsets of the data. Two variants exist:

* **`facet_wrap(~ variable)`** — Wraps panels into a 2D grid (best for one faceting variable).
* **`facet_grid(rows = ~ variable, cols = ~ variable)`** — Forces a row/column layout (best for two faceting variables).

The tilde `~` is read as **"as a function of"** (a formula).

```r
# Wrap panels by cylinder count
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~ cyl)

# 2D grid: gear across columns, cyl down rows
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_grid(rows = vars(cyl), cols = vars(gear))
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip (2024 Scheme):** When using `facet_grid`, the modern argument is `vars()` (introduced in ggplot2 3.0+). The older syntax `cyl ~ gear` still works but is deprecated. Prefer `vars()` in exams for full marks.

## 2.7 Coordinate Systems

| Function | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `coord_cartesian()` | Default Cartesian (xlim, ylim zoom) |
| `coord_flip()` | Swap x and y axes |
| `coord_polar()` | Polar (pie, radar) coordinates |
| `coord_fixed(ratio = 1)` | Equal aspect ratio |

**Critical Distinction:** `coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 5))` **zooms** without dropping data. `scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 5))` **clips/removes** data outside the range. Examiners often test this nuance.

## 2.8 Themes — Visual Styling

Themes control non-data elements (backgrounds, gridlines, fonts). `ggplot2` provides several built-ins:

| Theme | Description |
|---|---|
| `theme_gray()` | Default (light gray background, white grid) |
| `theme_bw()` | Black-and-white |
| `theme_minimal()` | No background, minimal grid |
| `theme_classic()` | Looks like base R / journal style |
| `theme_void()` | Empty canvas (for maps, network plots) |
| `theme_light()` | Light gray gridlines on white |
| `theme_dark()` | Dark mode |

The `theme()` function provides **fine-grained** control over individual elements:

```r
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16, color = "navy"),
    axis.text  = element_text(size = 10, angle = 45),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "lightyellow"),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )
```

The four key element functions are:
* `element_text()` — text styling (size, font, angle, color)
* `element_line()` — line styling (color, size, linetype)
* `element_rect()` — rectangle styling (fill, border)
* `element_blank()` — removes the element entirely

## 2.9 Position Adjustments

When categories overlap, `position` arguments prevent stacking confusion:

| Position | Use Case |
|---|---|
| `"stack"` (default for bars) | Vertical stacking |
| `"dodge"` | Side-by-side bars |
| `"fill"` | Normalized to 100% (proportions) |
| `"jitter"` | Random scatter to avoid overplotting |
| `"identity"` | Plot as-is (causes overlap) |

```r
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = class, fill = drv)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill")   # 100% stacked proportion bar chart
```

## 2.10 The Complete Layer Syntax

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical ggplot2 expression:**
> `ggplot(data, aes(...)) + geom_*(...) + scale_*(...) + facet_*(...) + coord_*(...) + theme_*(...) + labs(...)`

The `+` operator **must appear at the end of each line** (never at the start of the next), or R will throw a syntax error.

## 2.11 High-Yield Cheat Sheet Table

| Concept | Function | Exam-Important Fact |
|---|---|---|
| Initialize plot | `ggplot(df, aes(x, y))` | At least one geom is **required** |
| Map variable to color | `aes(color = var)` | Inside `aes()` only |
| Fixed color | `geom_point(color = "red")` | Outside `aes()` only |
| Bar chart (counts) | `geom_bar(stat = "count")` | Default stat is `"count"` |
| Bar chart (values) | `geom_col(stat = "identity")` | Default stat is `"identity"` |
| Histogram | `geom_histogram(bins = 30)` | Default stat is `"bin"` |
| Regression line | `geom_smooth(method = "lm")` | Default method = LOESS |
| Log axis | `scale_y_log10()` | Shortcut for continuous + log transform |
| Manual colors | `scale_color_manual(values = c(...))` | Vector of same length as levels |
| Wrap facets | `facet_wrap(~ var)` | One faceting variable |
| Grid facets | `facet_grid(rows = vars(v1), cols = vars(v2))` | Two faceting variables |
| 100% stacked bars | `geom_bar(position = "fill")` | Proportions, not counts |
| Pie chart | `coord_polar(theta = "y")` | Layer over a stacked bar |
| Save plot | `ggsave("plot.png", width = 6, height = 4, dpi = 300)` | Saves the **last** plot |
| Set theme globally | `theme_set(theme_minimal())` | Affects all subsequent plots |

## 2.12 Real-World Engineering & Data Science Utility

* **EDA (Exploratory Data Analysis):** Quickly visualizing distributions, correlations, and outliers in any tabular dataset.
* **Publication-quality journals:** The default 300+ dpi output meets IEEE, Springer, and Elsevier figure specifications.
* **Statistical reporting:** `geom_smooth()` produces reproducible regression plots used in research papers.
* **Dashboard prototyping:** Although not interactive (for that use `plotly` or `shiny`), it forms the static backbone.
* **Reproducible research:** Since plots are declared as code, the exact same figure can be regenerated from the data — critical for the KTU project phase.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Building a Scatter Plot Incrementally

> [!NOTE]
> This walkthrough demonstrates the **layer-by-layer construction** that examiners love to test in the 14-mark questions. We use the inbuilt `mtcars` dataset.

**Step 1 — Initialize with data only (produces a blank gray panel):**
```r
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(data = mtcars)
```
*Result:* A blank gray canvas with no axes. This proves that `ggplot()` alone does not draw anything.

**Step 2 — Add the data–aesthetic mapping:**
```r
ggplot(data = mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg))
```
*Result:* Axes appear (wt on x: 1.5–5.5, mpg on y: 10–35) but still no points, because no geom has been added.

**Step 3 — Add the geometric layer:**
```r
ggplot(data = mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point()
```
*Result:* Black scatter points appear. The mapping declared in `ggplot()` is inherited.

**Step 4 — Add a color aesthetic mapping:**
```r
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 3)
```
*Result:* Points are colored by cylinder count. A discrete legend appears on the right.

**Step 5 — Add a regression line per group:**
```r
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)
```
*Result:* Three colored regression lines, one per cylinder group. `se = FALSE` hides the confidence ribbon.

**Step 6 — Add faceting, labels, and a clean theme:**
```r
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ am) +
  labs(
    title    = "Fuel Efficiency vs Weight by Cylinder Count",
    subtitle = "Faceted by Transmission Type",
    x        = "Weight (1000 lbs)",
    y        = "Miles per Gallon",
    color    = "Cylinders"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Conceptual Takeaway:** This six-step build-up is the **canonical template** for the KTU "construct a multi-layered plot" question. Memorize the order: **Data → Aesthetics → Geom → Stat → Scale → Facet → Coordinate → Theme → Labels**.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Histogram with Density Overlay

```r
# Histogram with custom bins and a KDE overlay
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = mpg)) +
  geom_histogram(
    aes(y = after_stat(density)),  # critical: same scale for histogram + density
    bins = 15,
    fill = "skyblue",
    color = "black"
  ) +
  geom_density(color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of MPG", x = "Miles per Gallon", y = "Density") +
  theme_bw()
```

**Analytical Explanation:**
* `after_stat(density)` is a `ggplot2 3.0+` feature that pulls the computed density from the histogram's stat — this ensures the histogram and density curve are on the **same y-axis scale**.
* `linewidth` (note: replaces the deprecated `size` argument for lines in ggplot2 ≥ 3.4).
* `theme_bw()` gives the classic black-and-white journal look.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Boxplot Group Comparison

```r
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = class, y = displ, fill = drv)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(
    title = "Engine Displacement by Vehicle Class",
    x = "Vehicle Class",
    y = "Engine Displacement (Liters)",
    fill = "Drive Type"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

**Analytical Notes:**
* `coord_flip()` swaps x and y — useful when category labels are long.
* `scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2")` applies a ColorBrewer qualitative palette (great for ordinal/categorical fills).
* The `fill = drv` mapping inside `aes()` produces side-by-side boxes per drive type within each class.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Faceted Time Series

```r
library(dplyr)
economics_long <- economics

ggplot(economics, aes(x = date, y = unemploy)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = TRUE, color = "darkred") +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "%Y", date_breaks = "5 years") +
  labs(title = "US Unemployment 1967–2015", x = "Year", y = "Thousands Unemployed") +
  theme_light()
```

**Analytical Notes:**
* `economics` is a built-in `ggplot2` dataset with monthly US economic indicators.
* `scale_x_date()` automatically formats date axes.
* `geom_smooth()` with default `loess` produces a smooth trend curve with a confidence band.

## 3.5 Worked Example 5: Heatmap with `geom_tile()`

```r
# Correlation heatmap
cor_mat <- cor(mtcars)
cor_df <- as.data.frame(as.table(cor_mat))
names(cor_df) <- c("Var1", "Var2", "Correlation")

ggplot(cor_df, aes(x = Var1, y = Var2, fill = Correlation)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "blue", mid = "white", high = "red",
    midpoint = 0, limit = c(-1, 1)
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)) +
  labs(title = "Correlation Matrix of mtcars Variables")
```

**Analytical Notes:**
* `as.table()` converts the matrix to long format with columns `Var1`, `Var2`, `Freq` (we rename to `Correlation`).
* `scale_fill_gradient2()` creates a **diverging** color scale (negative → neutral → positive).
* The white grid lines between tiles are produced by `color = "white"` in `geom_tile()`.

## 3.6 The Analytical Decision Matrix

| Student Goal | Recommended Geom | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Show relationship between 2 continuous variables | `geom_point()` | Reveals correlation, clusters, outliers |
| Show distribution of 1 continuous variable | `geom_histogram()` / `geom_density()` | Reveals shape, modality, skew |
| Compare distributions across groups | `geom_boxplot()` / `geom_violin()` | Summarizes median, IQR, spread |
| Compare counts across categories | `geom_bar()` (stat = "count") | Default behavior counts rows |
| Show trend over time | `geom_line()` | Ordered x-axis connection |
| Show composition | `geom_bar(position = "fill")` or `geom_col()` | Proportions or absolute values |
| Show uncertainty | `geom_errorbar()` / `geom_smooth(se=TRUE)` | Confidence intervals |
| Show correlation matrix | `geom_tile()` | Encodes 3rd dimension via fill |
| Show 2D density | `geom_density_2d()` / `geom_bin_2d()` | Hexbin/2D KDE |

## 3.7 Saving Plots — `ggsave()`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> `ggsave()` is a convenience function that **saves the most recently displayed plot**.

```r
p <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) + geom_point()

ggsave(
  filename = "myplot.png",
  plot     = p,            # if omitted, saves last plot
  width    = 8,            # inches
  height   = 5,            # inches
  units    = "in",
  dpi      = 300           # publication quality
)
```

Supported formats: `.png`, `.pdf`, `.svg`, `.jpg`, `.tiff`. The `dpi` argument is the **resolution**; 300 is the academic publication standard.

## 3.8 Common Errors & Their Fixes (Analytical Resolution Table)

| Error Message | Likely Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `object 'X' not found` | Variable not in dataframe or typo | Use `dataframe$variable` to debug |
| `Discrete value supplied to continuous scale` | Numeric var passed where factor expected | Wrap with `factor()` |
| `Aesthetics must be either length 1 or same as data` | `color = c("red","blue")` outside `aes()` for many rows | Move into `aes()` or use a single value |
| `Removed N rows containing missing values` | `NA` present in x or y | Add `na.rm = TRUE` or filter with `drop_na()` |
| `Don't know how to automatically pick scale` | Need explicit scale for new variable | Add `scale_color_manual()` etc. |
| Plot looks blank | Forgot to add a `geom_*()` | Add a layer |

    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI

    nodeP1A --> nodeP1B{Categorical}
    nodeP1B -->|Yes| nodeGeom1[geom bar]
    nodeP1B -->|No| nodeGeom2[geom histogram or geom density]

    nodeQ1 -->|Two variables| nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 -->|Both continuous| nodeGeom3[geom point or geom smooth]

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the Grammar of Graphics as implemented in `ggplot2`. List its main components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Grammar of Graphics, proposed by Leland Wilkinson and implemented in R by Hadley Wickham's `ggplot2` package, is a theoretical framework that decomposes any statistical graph into a set of **independent semantic components** that can be composed like grammatical rules. Its main components are:

1. **Data** — the input data frame
2. **Aesthetic Mappings** (`aes()`) — link variables to visual properties
3. **Geometric Objects** (`geom_*()`) — the actual marks drawn
4. **Statistical Transformations** (`stat_*()`) — summary computations
5. **Scales** — mapping from data to aesthetic values
6. **Coordinate System** — the plane on which marks are placed
7. **Facets** — small-multiple sub-plots
8. **Theme** — non-data visual styling

These components are added sequentially using the `+` operator. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between `geom_bar()` and `geom_col()` in `ggplot2` with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `geom_bar()` | `geom_col()` |
|---|---|---|
| Default stat | `count` (counts rows) | `identity` (uses supplied y) |
| Y aesthetic | Not required | Required |
| Typical use | Frequency of categories | Pre-computed bar heights |

```r
# geom_bar: counts number of rows per cyl
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = factor(cyl))) + geom_bar()

# geom_col: uses supplied y values
df <- data.frame(gear = c(3,4,5), avg_mpg = c(16,21,21))
ggplot(df, aes(x = factor(gear), y = avg_mpg)) + geom_col()
```
**(3 marks)**

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU pattern provides **two full 14-mark alternatives**. Solve **ONE** of them. Each contains two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the layered architecture of the Grammar of Graphics in `ggplot2`. List the minimum mandatory components required to render a plot. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

The Grammar of Graphics treats every plot as a composition of **independent, layered grammatical components**. In `ggplot2`, these components are added sequentially using the `+` operator, building from the data layer up to the rendered output.

**Layered Architecture (5 components per layer):**

1. **Data Layer** — `ggplot(data = df)` provides the canvas.
2. **Aesthetic Mappings** — `aes(x, y, color, fill, ...)` maps variables to visual properties.
3. **Geometric Layer** — `geom_point()`, `geom_bar()`, etc. draws the marks.
4. **Statistical Transformation** — `stat_smooth()`, `stat_bin()`, etc. computes summaries.
5. **Scales & Coordinates** — `scale_x_continuous()`, `coord_flip()` control axes.
6. **Facets** — `facet_wrap()` creates sub-panels.
7. **Theme** — `theme_minimal()` controls non-data appearance.

**Minimum Mandatory Components to Render a Plot:**

```r
ggplot(data = mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) + geom_point()
```

Three things are **mandatory**:
* **Data** (`mtcars`)
* **Aesthetic mapping** (`aes(x, y)`)
* **At least one geometric layer** (`geom_point()`)

Without a geom, the plot is blank. Without data, `ggplot()` throws an error. Without aesthetics, geoms cannot position marks. **(7 marks — 3 for diagram/explanation, 2 for listing, 2 for example)**

---

**(b)** Using the `mtcars` dataset, write complete R code using `ggplot2` to produce a **scatter plot of `mpg` vs `wt`**, with points colored by `cyl`, a separate linear regression line for each cylinder group (without confidence band), faceted by `am`, and a publication-quality `theme_bw()` appearance. Add appropriate title and axis labels. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

```r
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(data = mtcars,
       aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ am) +
  labs(
    title = "Fuel Efficiency vs Weight by Cylinder Count",
    subtitle = "Faceted by Transmission (0 = Automatic, 1 = Manual)",
    x = "Weight (1000 lbs)",
    y = "Miles per Gallon",
    color = "Cylinders"
  ) +
  theme_bw()
```

**Step-by-step Marking Scheme:**

* (1 mark) `library(ggplot2)` and data argument correctly specified.
* (1 mark) `aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = factor(cyl))` — correct mapping.
* (1 mark) `geom_point()` added with non-default `size`.
* (1 mark) `geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)` — correct method and CI handling.
* (1 mark) `facet_wrap(~ am)` — correct faceting syntax.
* (1 mark) `labs()` with title, axis labels, and legend title.
* (1 mark) `theme_bw()` applied. **(7 marks total)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly lose **2 marks** by:
> * Forgetting to wrap `cyl` in `factor()` — causes a continuous color scale and a poor legend.
> * Placing the `+` at the start of the next line instead of the end of the previous line — causes a syntax error.
> * Using `fill = factor(cyl)` instead of `color = factor(cyl)` for points (fill is for shapes with interiors like bars; color is for points and lines).

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the role of **aesthetic mappings (`aes()`)** in `ggplot2`. Clearly differentiate between arguments placed **inside** `aes()` and those placed **outside** `aes()`, with one example of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

The `aes()` function is the **declarative mapping engine** of `ggplot2`. It tells the plotting system: *"this column of the data should drive this visual property."* Without `aes()`, no variable-to-visual linkage is possible.

**Arguments inside `aes()` (variable-driven, data-dependent):**
* Must reference **column names** of the data.
* Cause `ggplot2` to **build a scale and a legend** automatically.
* Value of the aesthetic changes per observation.

```r
# Inside aes(): color varies by cyl
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point()
```
A legend titled "factor(cyl)" with three color swatches is auto-generated. **(3 marks)**

**Arguments outside `aes()` (constant, fixed for all points):**
* Must be **literal values** (numbers, quoted strings).
* Do **not** create a legend.
* Apply the same value to every observation.

```r
# Outside aes(): all points are blue, size 4
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point(color = "blue", size = 4)
```
No legend is produced. **(3 marks)**

**Rule of Thumb:** "If it changes with the data → `aes()`. If it's a constant → outside `aes()`." **(1 mark for the rule)**

---

**(b)** Using the `mpg` dataset from `ggplot2`, write complete R code to produce a **boxplot of `displ` (engine displacement) across `class` (vehicle type)**, with the boxes filled by `drv` (drive type) using a ColorBrewer palette, flipped coordinates, percentage-stacked bars alternative for proportion comparison, and a minimal theme. Provide both versions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Version 1: Boxplot with flipped coordinates and ColorBrewer fill**

```r
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(mpg, aes(x = class, y = displ, fill = drv)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(
    title = "Engine Displacement by Vehicle Class",
    x = "Vehicle Class",
    y = "Engine Displacement (Liters)",
    fill = "Drive Type"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```
*(3 marks for this version — 1 for correct geom + aes, 1 for coord_flip + scale_fill_brewer, 1 for labs + theme)*

**Version 2: Proportion-style comparison using stacked bar**

```r
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = class, fill = drv)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(
    title = "Proportion of Drive Types per Vehicle Class",
    x = "Vehicle Class",
    y = "Proportion (%)",
    fill = "Drive Type"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  coord_flip()
```
*(3 marks for this version — 1 for geom_bar with position fill, 1 for percent scale, 1 for proper labels)*

*(1 mark for the comparative analysis: boxplot reveals distribution shape (median, IQR, outliers), while the 100% stacked bar reveals the relative share of drive types within each class.)* **(7 marks total)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake is to use `geom_bar()` (with default stat = "count") but expect it to plot a supplied `y` value. Students should remember: for heights from a column, use `geom_col()`; for counts, use `geom_bar()`. Mixing these loses 2 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 / ggplot2**

- ✅ `ggplot2` is a **declarative** package implementing Wilkinson's **Grammar of Graphics**.
- ✅ The function is `ggplot()` (lowercase g, no underscore) — calling `ggplot` as a variable name creates a conflict.
- ✅ The **three mandatory components** for any plot: `data`, `aes()` mapping, and at least one `geom_*()`.
- ✅ Layers are combined with the **`+` operator**, which must be at the **end of the line**, not the start of the next.
- ✅ `aes()` is for **variable mappings** (creates legends); constants go **outside** `aes()`.
- ✅ `geom_bar()` **counts** rows; `geom_col()` **plots supplied y values**.
- ✅ `geom_histogram()` defaults to **30 bins**; always tune `bins` or `binwidth`.
- ✅ `geom_smooth()` defaults to **LOESS** (n<1000) or **GAM** (n≥1000); use `method = "lm"` for linear regression.
- ✅ `stat_smooth()` and `geom_smooth()` are **equivalent** — one is just a stat, the other a geom with a stat.
- ✅ `facet_wrap(~ var)` — **one faceting variable**, wraps into a grid.
- ✅ `facet_grid(rows = vars(v1), cols = vars(v2))` — **two faceting variables**, structured layout.
- ✅ Use modern `vars()` syntax in `facet_grid` (preferred over deprecated `cyl ~ gear`).
- ✅ `scale_x_log10()` is a shortcut; for custom logs use `scale_x_continuous(trans = "log10")`.
- ✅ `scale_color_*` controls point/line **outlines**; `scale_fill_*` controls **interior fills**.
- ✅ `coord_cartesian(xlim = ...)` **zooms** without dropping data; `scale_x_continuous(limits = ...)` **drops** out-of-range rows.
- ✅ `coord_flip()` swaps x and y axes (useful for long category labels).
- ✅ `coord_polar(theta = "y")` converts a stacked bar into a **pie chart**.
- ✅ Position adjustments: `"stack"` (default), `"dodge"` (side-by-side), `"fill"` (100% proportions), `"jitter"` (random offset).
- ✅ `theme_minimal()`, `theme_bw()`, `theme_classic()`, `theme_void()` are built-in themes.
- ✅ Use `element_text()`, `element_line()`, `element_rect()`, `element_blank()` inside `theme()` for fine control.
- ✅ `ggsave("file.png", width = 8, height = 5, dpi = 300)` saves the **last plot** at publication quality.
- ✅ For a 3-variable heatmap, use `geom_tile(aes(fill = z))` with a `scale_fill_gradient2()` diverging scale.
- ✅ The pipe-friendly companion `aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = factor(cyl))` is the most exam-tested pattern.
- ✅ Common error: writing `color = "red"` inside `aes()` — produces a useless legend instead of a fixed color.
- ✅ Common error: forgetting to add any `geom_*()` — produces a blank panel with axes but no marks.
- ✅ Common error: `+` at the **start of a new line** instead of the end of the previous line — R syntax error.
- ✅ KTU exam focus for 14-mark Q: layered scatter plot + regression + facet + theme + labels.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Notes — ggplot2 (Module 4, PEEOT631, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> These notes are aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**, **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (L2–L4)**, and the **typical ESE question paper pattern** (2 × 3 marks in Part A + 14-mark choice question in Part B). For further practice, the student is encouraged to replicate the worked examples using the `mtcars`, `mpg`, `economics`, and `diamonds` inbuilt datasets.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Regression Models: Building Linear Models (Model Fitting)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT631 - R-PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Regression Models: Building Linear Models (Model Fitting)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Linear Regression** is a supervised machine learning and classical statistical modeling technique used to model the relationship between a scalar dependent (response) variable and one or more explanatory (independent/predictor) variables by fitting a linear equation to the observed data. In the R programming ecosystem, **model fitting** refers to the computational process of estimating the unknown model parameters (coefficients) such that the fitted line best represents the data, typically by minimizing the sum of squared residuals using the **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** method.

In R, the workhorse function for fitting linear models is `lm()` (linear models), which belongs to the base `stats` package. The general syntax is:

```r
fitted_model <- lm(formula, data = dataframe)
```

Where:
- **formula**: A symbolic description of the model (e.g., `y ~ x1 + x2`)
- **data**: The data frame containing the variables

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must distinguish between *Simple Linear Regression* (SLR - one predictor) and *Multiple Linear Regression* (MLR - two or more predictors). The `lm()` function in R handles both seamlessly with a single syntax.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **real estate analyst** trying to predict house prices. You suspect that the size of the house (square footage) influences its price. If you plot all houses on a graph — with size on the X-axis and price on the Y-axis — the points form a cloud. **Linear regression** is the process of drawing the single best straight line through this cloud of points, such that the line passes as close as possible to every point simultaneously.

Think of it like this: you have a piece of spaghetti (the data points) scattered on a table, and you want to lay a ruler (the regression line) flat on the table so that the distance from each piece of spaghetti to the ruler is minimized. **Model fitting** is the act of finding exactly where to place that ruler.

In **R**, the `lm()` function acts as a highly efficient carpenter who automatically finds the exact position and tilt of this ruler using the mathematical principle of *least squares*.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Dependent Variable (Y)** | The response/outcome being predicted |
| **Independent Variable (X)** | The predictor/explanatory variable(s) |
| **Coefficient ($\beta$)** | The slope of the regression line |
| **Intercept ($\alpha$ or $\beta_0$)** | The value of Y when X = 0 |
| **Residual ($e_i$)** | The vertical distance between observed and predicted Y |
| **SSR** | Sum of Squared Residuals (the quantity being minimized) |
| **OLS** | Ordinary Least Squares estimation method |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition to Memorize:** A linear model assumes that the conditional mean of Y given X is a linear function of X. It does **not** require that Y itself or the relationship be perfectly linear — only the mean function is linear in parameters.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation of Model Fitting

The general **Multiple Linear Regression (MLR)** model with $p$ predictors is expressed as:

$$Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{1i} + \beta_2 X_{2i} + \cdots + \beta_p X_{pi} + \epsilon_i$$

Where:
- $Y_i$ is the observed value of the response for the $i$-th observation
- $\beta_0$ is the intercept (population parameter)
- $\beta_1, \beta_2, \ldots, \beta_p$ are the population regression coefficients
- $X_{1i}, X_{2i}, \ldots, X_{pi}$ are the predictor values
- $\epsilon_i$ is the random error term, assumed to be $\sim N(0, \sigma^2)$

### 2.1.1 The OLS Objective Function

The **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** method finds the estimated coefficients $\hat{\beta}_0, \hat{\beta}_1, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_p$ that minimize the **Sum of Squared Residuals (SSR)**:

$$SSR = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2$$

Where the predicted value is:
$$\hat{Y}_i = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 X_{1i} + \cdots + \hat{\beta}_p X_{pi}$$

And the residual is:
$$e_i = Y_i - \hat{Y}_i$$

### 2.1.2 Why Squared Residuals? (The 'Why' Behind the Method)

- Squaring **penalizes large errors more heavily** than small errors.
- It **avoids cancellation** of positive and negative residuals when summing.
- It produces a **differentiable, convex** objective function, guaranteeing a unique global minimum.
- It has **strong statistical optimality properties** under the Gauss-Markov assumptions.

## 2.2 Assumptions of the Linear Model (Gauss-Markov Conditions)

For OLS estimators to be **Best Linear Unbiased Estimators (BLUE)**, the following must hold:

1. **Linearity**: The relationship between X and Y is linear in parameters.
2. **Independence**: Observations are independent of each other.
3. **Homoscedasticity**: Constant variance of errors ($Var(\epsilon_i) = \sigma^2$).
4. **Normality**: Errors are normally distributed ($\epsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$).
5. **No Multicollinearity**: Predictors are not perfectly correlated (for MLR).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The `lm()` function in R does **not** check these assumptions. It will happily fit a model to garbage data. The statistician's job (i.e., the student's job in exams) is to validate these assumptions using diagnostic plots via the `plot()` function on the fitted model object.

## 2.3 The R `lm()` Function — Internal Workflow

When you execute `model <- lm(y ~ x1 + x2, data = mydata)`, R performs the following sequence:

1. **Parses the formula** `y ~ x1 + x2` to identify response and predictors.
2. **Constructs the design matrix** $X$ (with a column of 1s for the intercept) and the response vector $Y$.
3. **Solves the normal equations** using the closed-form matrix solution:
$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$$
4. **Computes fitted values** $\hat{Y} = X\hat{\beta}$.
5. **Calculates residuals** $e = Y - \hat{Y}$.
6. **Stores the model object** of class `lm`, which contains coefficients, residuals, fitted values, and other components accessible via accessor functions like `summary()`, `coef()`, `residuals()`, `fitted()`.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value bars and norms use `\vert` instead of the pipe symbol `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **R Implementation** | **Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Population Model | $Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \epsilon_i$ | `lm(y ~ x, data = df)` | Theoretical relationship |
| Estimated Model | $\hat{Y}_i = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 X_i$ | `fitted(model)` | Fitted values |
| Coefficient Estimate (Matrix Form) | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$ | Internal to `lm()` | OLS solution |
| Residual | $e_i = Y_i - \hat{Y}_i$ | `residuals(model)` | Model error |
| Sum of Squared Residuals | $SSR = \sum e_i^2$ | `deviance(model)` | Loss function minimized |
| Total Sum of Squares | $SST = \sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2$ | Manual computation | Total variability |
| Regression Sum of Squares | $SSR_{reg} = \sum (\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y})^2$ | Manual computation | Explained variability |
| Coefficient of Determination | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SSR}{SST}$ | `summary(model)$r.squared` | Proportion of variance explained |
| Adjusted R-Squared | $R^2_{adj} = 1 - (1-R^2)\frac{n-1}{n-p-1}$ | `summary(model)$adj.r.squared` | Penalized R² for MLR |
| Residual Standard Error | $RSE = \sqrt{\frac{SSR}{n-p-1}}$ | `sigma(model)` | Avg size of residual |
| F-statistic | $F = \frac{SSR_{reg}/p}{SSR/(n-p-1)}$ | `summary(model)$fstatistic` | Overall model significance |
| t-statistic for $\beta_j$ | $t_j = \frac{\hat{\beta}_j}{SE(\hat{\beta}_j)}$ | From `summary()` | Individual coefficient test |
| Confidence Interval for $\beta_j$ | $\hat{\beta}_j \pm t_{\alpha/2, n-p-1} \cdot SE(\hat{\beta}_j)$ | `confint(model)` | Range of plausible values |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering**: Predicting concrete compressive strength from mix proportions.
- **Mechanical Engineering**: Modeling wear rate vs. operating temperature.
- **Electrical Engineering**: Predicting power consumption from voltage and current measurements.
- **Data Science / ML**: The foundational building block for more advanced models (Ridge, Lasso, polynomial regression).
- **Economics / Econometrics**: Demand forecasting, risk modeling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the OLS Estimator (Matrix Form)

We start with the linear model in matrix notation:

$$\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{Y}$ is an $n \times 1$ vector of observed responses
- $\mathbf{X}$ is an $n \times (p+1)$ design matrix (with a column of 1s for the intercept)
- $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ is a $(p+1) \times 1$ vector of unknown parameters
- $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ is an $n \times 1$ vector of random errors

### Step 1: Define the Objective Function

The OLS estimator minimizes the sum of squared residuals:

$$S(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2 = (\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^T (\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})$$

### Step 2: Expand the Quadratic Form

We expand the squared error term:

$$S(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{Y}^T\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{Y}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} - \boldsymbol{\beta}^T\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{Y} + \boldsymbol{\beta}^T\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$$

### Step 3: Differentiate with Respect to $\boldsymbol{\beta}$

Taking the partial derivative and setting it to zero:

$$\frac{\partial S(\boldsymbol{\beta})}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} = -2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{Y} + 2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{0}$$

### Step 4: Solve the Normal Equations

Rearranging:

$$\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{Y}$$

### Step 5: Apply the Matrix Inverse

Assuming $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}$ is invertible (i.e., no perfect multicollinearity):

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{Y}$$

This is the **closed-form OLS estimator** that R computes internally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** If a question asks "How does `lm()` estimate coefficients?", this is the answer. R's `lm()` uses the LAPACK library's optimized routines to compute $(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}$ via Cholesky decomposition or QR factorization for numerical stability.

## 3.2 Simple Linear Regression — Exhaustive Worked Derivation

For **Simple Linear Regression (SLR)**, $Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \epsilon_i$.

### Step 1: Set Up the System

We have two normal equations (one for $\beta_0$ and one for $\beta_1$):

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_0} = -2 \sum (Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_i) = 0$$
$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_1} = -2 \sum X_i (Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_i) = 0$$

### Step 2: Solve the First Equation for $\beta_0$

$$\sum Y_i = n\beta_0 + \beta_1 \sum X_i \implies \beta_0 = \bar{Y} - \beta_1 \bar{X}$$

### Step 3: Substitute into the Second Equation

$$\sum X_i Y_i = \beta_0 \sum X_i + \beta_1 \sum X_i^2$$

Substituting $\beta_0$:

$$\sum X_i Y_i = (\bar{Y} - \beta_1 \bar{X}) \sum X_i + \beta_1 \sum X_i^2$$

### Step 4: Solve for $\hat{\beta}_1$

$$\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_i - \bar{X})^2} = \frac{S_{XY}}{S_{XX}}$$

### Step 5: Compute the Intercept

$$\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{Y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{X}$$

### Step 3.3 Numerical Verification in R

```r
# Sample data
X <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
Y <- c(2, 4, 5, 4, 5)

# Manual calculation
Xbar <- mean(X); Ybar <- mean(Y)
Sxy <- sum((X - Xbar) * (Y - Ybar))
Sxx <- sum((X - Xbar)^2)
beta1_hat <- Sxy / Sxx
beta0_hat <- Ybar - beta1_hat * Xbar

cat("Manual:  beta0 =", beta0_hat, "beta1 =", beta1_hat, "\n")

# Using lm()
model <- lm(Y ~ X)
cat("R lm():  ", coef(model), "\n")
```

**Output:**
```
Manual:  beta0 = 2.2 beta1 = 0.6 
R lm():  2.2  0.6 
```

Both methods produce identical results, confirming the OLS derivation.

## 3.4 R Code Template for Fitting Linear Models (Exam-Ready)

```r
# ===== Simple Linear Regression =====
model_slr <- lm(mpg ~ wt, data = mtcars)
summary(model_slr)

# ===== Multiple Linear Regression =====
model_mlr <- lm(mpg ~ wt + cyl + hp, data = mtcars)
summary(model_mlr)

# ===== Extracting Model Components =====
coef(model_mlr)              # Coefficients
fitted(model_mlr)            # Predicted values
residuals(model_mlr)         # Residuals
summary(model_mlr)$r.squared # R-squared
summary(model_mlr)$fstatistic # F-statistic
confint(model_mlr, level = 0.95) # 95% CIs

# ===== Diagnostic Plots =====
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(model_mlr)
```

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| **Scenario** | **R Formula Syntax** | **Model Type** |
|---|---|---|
| Single predictor | `lm(y ~ x)` | Simple Linear Regression |
| Multiple predictors (additive) | `lm(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3)` | Multiple Linear Regression (additive) |
| Interaction effect | `lm(y ~ x1 * x2)` | MLR with interaction (equivalent to `x1 + x2 + x1:x2`) |
| All variables | `lm(y ~ ., data = df)` | MLR using all other columns |
| Polynomial term | `lm(y ~ x + I(x^2))` | Quadratic regression |
| No intercept | `lm(y ~ x - 1)` | Regression through origin |
| Log transformation | `lm(log(y) ~ x)` | Log-linear model |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Exam Tip:** The tilde `~` in R formulas is read as "is modeled as" or "regressed on". So `y ~ x1 + x2` means "y is modeled as x1 plus x2". The `+` here means *inclusion*, not mathematical addition.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Linear Model Fitting Workflow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Define Research Question] --> A1[Collect and Load Data into R]
    A1 --> A2[Perform Exploratory Data Analysis]
    A2 --> A3{Choose Model Type}
    A3 -->|One Predictor| A4[Simple Linear Regression]
    A3 -->|Multiple Predictors| A5[Multiple Linear Regression]
    A3 -->|Polynomial| A6[Polynomial Regression]
    A4 --> A7[Specify Formula in lm]
    A5 --> A7
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8[Call lm function: model <- lm formula data]
    A8 --> A9[OLS Computes Beta Hat Matrix]
    A9 --> A10[Store lm Class Object]
    A10 --> A11[Apply summary function]
    A11 --> A12[Interpret Coefficients t values p values]
    A12 --> A13{Check Assumptions}
    A13 -->|Yes| A14[Model Valid: Make Predictions]
    A13 -->|No| A15[Transform Variables or Add Features]
    A15 --> A7
    A14 --> A16[Use predict function for new data]
    A16 --> A17[End: Deploy Model]
```

## 4.2 Linear Model Conceptual Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        B1[Data Frame data argument]
        B2[Formula y ~ x1 + x2 argument]
    end
    subgraph ENGINE[lm Engine in R]
        C1[Design Matrix Builder X with intercept column]
        C2[Response Vector y]
        C3[Normal Equations Solver]
        C4[Beta Hat = XT X inverse XT y]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Object of class lm]
        D1[coefficients]
        D2[residuals]
        D3[fitted values]
        D4[rank and df]
    end
    subgraph ANALYSIS[Post Fitting Analysis]
        E1[summary model]
        E2[plot model diagnostics]
        E3[predict model newdata]
        E4[confint model]
    end
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1
    C4 --> D2
    C4 --> D3
    C4 --> D4
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E2
    D3 --> E1
    D3 --> E3
    D1 --> E4
```

## 4.3 The OLS Geometric Intuition (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F0[Observed Data Points Yi Xi] --> F1[Vertical Projection onto Regression Line]
    F1 --> F2[Predicted Points Yhat i on Line]
    F0 --> F3[Compute Vertical Distance ei = Yi minus Yhat i]
    F3 --> F4[Square Each Residual ei squared]
    F4 --> F5[Sum All Squared Residuals SSR]
    F5 --> F6[Minimize SSR with respect to Beta]
    F6 --> F7{Beta values Adjusted}
    F7 -->|Yes| F8[Final Fitted Line with Minimum SSR]
    F7 -->|No| F1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The vertical projections in OLS are unique to the OLS method. Other methods (e.g., orthogonal regression) minimize perpendicular distances instead. For KTU exams, always specify *vertical* distance minimization.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) method used in linear regression. State the objective function it minimizes.**

**Model Answer:**

Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) is a parameter estimation technique used in linear regression to find the best-fitting line through a set of data points. The objective function it minimizes is the **Sum of Squared Residuals (SSR)**, defined as:

$$SSR = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2$$

Where $Y_i$ is the observed value and $\hat{Y}_i$ is the predicted value from the model. OLS finds the coefficients $\hat{\beta}_0, \hat{\beta}_1, \ldots$ such that SSR is minimized, ensuring the line is as close as possible to all data points simultaneously.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of the `lm()` function in R. Write its general syntax and explain each argument.**

**Model Answer:**

The `lm()` function in R is used to fit linear models using OLS estimation. Its general syntax is:

```r
fitted_model <- lm(formula, data = dataframe)
```

**Arguments:**
- **formula**: A symbolic description of the model in the form `response ~ predictor(s)`. Example: `y ~ x1 + x2`.
